Standard play is to raise for value and initiative. Opponents show frequent preflop raises to 300, so sizing to 300 (3x BB) is pot-building without overcommitting. Builds pot OOP while isolating weaker hands. Deep stacks support this; short stack Claude (1415) may shove but A10s crushes typical shortstack shoving range.

K2o is a weak holding that doesn't warrant engagement from the button when facing a UTG open raise. With my short stack at 1415 chips and two aggressive opponents (Minni in small blind and Flash in big blind) still to act, I need to preserve chips for premium positions and hands. K2 offsuit has poor equity against Elon's opening range and poor playability post-flop given its weak kicker. This is a disciplined fold that keeps my stack intact for better spots.

Qs4d is a weak offsuit holding with poor post‑flop playability and is often dominated by an UTG open. As the small blind I would be out of position against both the opener (Elon) and the big blind (Flash), so calling 250 into a 450 pot creates a marginal, multiway pot with little equity or fold equity. There's also a realistic chance of facing a 3‑bet/squeeze behind me. With a healthy ~85bb stack, it's better to conserve chips for stronger spots—fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Elon raised from under-the-gun to 300. Although the price to call is only 200 more, J7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an UTG open. Elon has shown significant aggression in previous hands, and I want to avoid entering a pot with a marginal hand where I will likely be dominated or forced to fold on most flops. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here.

Folding Td Ks from UTG. While King-Ten is a decent hand, my situation dictates a tight approach: (1) I'm in the worst position (UTG) with 4 active players still to act, (2) My stack is critically short at 1415 chips - I need to be selective and preserve chips for premium hands, (3) There are three aggressive opponents behind me, including Flash with a massive 16650 chip stack. Td Ks is not strong enough to warrant going to war from UTG with this stack depth. I should wait for better cards in better positions - pairs, premium broadway cards (AK, AQ, QQ+), or stronger holdings where I can make a stand. This is a disciplined fold that keeps me in the tournament with positive chip preservation.

Button with A Qo and only the blinds left is a clear open-raise spot. Claude folded UTG so there's no early raiser; I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds, and get value from worse hands while applying fold equity. Table standard preflop opens have been to 300, so I’ll use a 3x raise to keep sizing consistent and avoid giving good odds to the big blind. Stacks are deep enough to play postflop but I won’t overcommit — plan is to c-bet favorable flops, and proceed cautiously to a sizable 3-bet. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 6d, which is a weak starting hand. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. Calling 250 more to play this hand out of position against an active player like Minni is not profitable in the long run, even with decent pot odds. Additionally, the Big Blind (Elon) is still left to act and could potentially squeeze or raise further. Given my healthy stack and the early stage of the tournament (Hand 18), I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

AKo is one of the best hands in poker, especially out of position in the big blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown a tendency to raise frequently from late position with a wide range (e.g., Hands 1,2,3,6,9,10,11,14). Flash has folded SB, and Claude folded UTG, so it's heads-up vs Minni's likely wide button opening range. Calling risks multiway pots OOP and allows Minni to realize equity cheaply. A 3-bet polarizes our range effectively, extracts value from worse Ax, Kx, broadways, and takes down the pot often preflop. Sizing to 900 (3x their raise) is standard, building a pot while leaving room for 4-bets and postflop play. My stack (13,300) and theirs (8,185) support this aggression to maximize EV.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♥Q♣ and Elon 3‑bet to 900 from the big blind. With position, deep stacks (~82BB effective) and a strong but non-premium hand, I prefer to flat-call rather than 4‑bet. Calling keeps Elon’s wider 3‑bet range in, preserves postflop maneuverability, and avoids polarizing my range into a situation where a 5‑bet shove could put me in a tough spot. I’ll call the extra 600 (900 total) to see a flop and use my position to c-bet good textures or pot‑control and fold to heavy resistance when appropriate.

Preflop 3-bet with AKo was correct as discussed, isolating vs Minni's wide button opening range. Flop hits top pair top kicker (TPTK) on Qh Ac 9c, a strong but draw-heavy board (clubs flush draw, JT straight draw). Minni's 3-bet calling range includes Ax (worse like AQ/AJ/A9), broadways (KQ/QJ), pocket pairs (QQ/99/JJ/TT), suited connectors. My hand has great equity vs her range (~70-80% vs typical calling range), blocks AA/AQ/ATs. OOP but with initiative, c-bet for value from worse Ax/broadways and to charge/protect vs draws (flush/straight). Sizing 1200 (~65% pot) balances value extraction while denying equity cheaply, leaves room for turn play. Pot control via check risks giving free cards to draws or bluffs. Minni's history shows frequent calling stations (e.g., Hands 1,2,3,11), so expect calls from worse. Stack/Pot SPR ~6.5 post-bet supports continued aggression.

I have A♥Q♣ on Q♥ A♣ 9♣ — that’s two pair (Aces and Queens) on a relatively dry flop with two clubs. Elon 3‑bet preflop, so his range is value‑heavy (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQ, some bluffs), but my A and Q act as blockers to AA/QQ combos (those exact set combos are reduced). His flop bet is small (~1/3–1/2 pot), so checking/calling keeps many worse hands in but also gives free cards to club backdoor draws and the few pocket‑pairs that currently beat me (99/QQ/AA). I want to get value and deny equity to draws, so I’ll raise the minimum to extract from worse Ax/Kx/Qx hands and charge clubs and other turn outs. This leaves me plenty of stack to navigate turn decisions and preserves fold equity if he has a polarized monster. Plan: raise to 2400 total (min‑raise), re‑evaluate on any large 3‑bet/shove (we beat a lot of his continuing range but will respect heavy reraises).

Facing a flop raise from Minni after my 3-bet preflop and c-bet with AsKh for TPTK on Qh Ac 9c. This draw-heavy board (clubs flush draw, JT OESD) polarizes her button 3-bet calling range to nuts (QQ trips, 99 set, AQ/A9/Q9 two pair) or semi-bluffs/draws (AJc/ATc/KQc/JTc). My hand crushes bluffs/draws (~80%+ equity), blocks AA/AQ/ATs, and has ~30% vs monsters. Overall equity >35% needed for pot odds (1200 into 5450, ~18% implied + fold equity denied). Minni's history shows aggression when strong but wide preflop calling (Hands 1-5,11), suggesting bluffs/draws in mix. OOP call maintains pot control (SPR ~1.5 post-call with her 5185 behind), denies equity, allows turn hero call/shove if clean or value bet if improves. Raising risks commitment OOP vs shove (her stack ~5k), folding too nitty given blockers/equity. Call maximizes EV.

Turn board pairs the Qh with Qd, giving straight outs to JT (now filled) and improving Qx hands to trips. My AsKh remains TPTK (aces), blocking AA/AQ/ATs, crushing bluffs/missed draws (flush draw bricked, some straight draws hit but vulnerable). Minni's flop raise range (after 3bet call): value (AQ/A9/QQ/99/sets) + semi-bluffs (clubs/JTs/KQc). Equity strong vs polarized range (~60-70% vs bluffs/draws, ~30% vs value). OOP post-call, low SPR (~0.8 effective), checking pot controls pot size, induces bluffs/shoves from missed draws while denying her thin value bets. Betting risks overcommitting OOP vs her shove range (which she'd do with nuts anyway). Her history (calling stations but aggressive when strong) suggests checking exploits bluffs and controls vs value. River will be low SPR showdown, where TPTK often best. Maximizes EV by balancing pot control and fold equity denial.

I have essentially the nuts on the turn: Q♣ A♥ on Q♥ A♣ 9♣ Q♦ is QQQAA — queens full of aces. Almost nothing beats me; the only realistically better hand is pocket Aces (Ad/As), and my Ah/Qc in hand heavily reduces combinations of strong counter-hands. Elon 3‑bet preflop and called my flop min‑raise, so his range contains a lot of Ax (AK, AQ), KQ, some medium pairs and blockers — almost all of which are crushed by my full house and will call a large shove. He checked the turn (pot control or a trap), but given his earlier aggression he will call big with many worse hands. My stack (5,185) is smaller than the pot (6,650), so shoving now: 1) maximizes value from worse Ax/Kx and pocket pairs that will call, 2) denies a free river that could give unlikely counterfeiting/quads, and 3) forces the decision with only one realistic beat (AdAs). I will shove all‑in for my remaining 5,185 chips.

Facing Minni's all-in shove of 5185 on the turn after checking to her, with the board Qh Ac 9c Qd. My AsKh is top pair top kicker, solid but vulnerable. Her line—calling preflop 3-bet with button range, raising flop c-bet on draw-heavy board, then turn shove after pairing the Q—strongly polarizes to value: QQ (quads now), AQ (aces full of queens), Q9 (queen trips), A9 (two pair aces-nines improved), 99 (set). Missed draws (clubs flush, JT straight now complete but she'd have ~1/3 equity left) or bluffs are possible but unlikely given her aggression only when strong (e.g., Hand 5 river shove, Hand 11 deep calls). Equity vs her value-heavy shoving range ~20-30%, pot odds require ~30% (5185 into 17020 pot, ~23% direct + limited implied with SPR ~0.2 post-call OOP). Blocks AA/AQs but doesn't change fold EV+. Preserve 10k stack early in tournament (hand 18/150) for better spots; her wide ranges exploitable later. Fold maximizes long-term EV.

Qc5d is a weak offsuit queen with very poor playability from UTG (first to act) in a 4-handed table. Raising to 200 risks a shove from the short-stacked BB (Claude, ~13bb) or a 3-bet from Elon/Flash and leaves me out of position postflop. Limping (calling 100) would put me multiway and OOP with a dominated hand. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left to play, folding and avoiding a marginal, high-variance spot is the best EV decision here.

I am on the button with 9c 4d, a very weak hand. Minni has folded from UTG. Elon (Small Blind) and Claude (Big Blind) are left to act after me. Given that Claude is short-stacked with only 1315 chips (about 13 big blinds), any raise from the button might commit me to a call if he shoves, which I do not want to do with 9-high. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Preflop in small blind with AK suited, a premium hand, after UTG and button folds. Facing a short-stacked big blind (13BB). Standard play is to raise for value and to steal blinds or isolate. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a balanced size that pressures the BB while building the pot appropriately with position postflop. AKs performs well against a shoving range or calling range from BB.

With 3c-6d (one of the worst hands in poker), I must fold despite my short stack situation. While I'm down to 1315 chips (13 big blinds), 3-6 offsuit has poor equity against Elon's raising range from the small blind. Even accounting for my desperate stack position, burning chips with the worst hand at the table is not a profitable long-term strategy. I should look for a better opportunity to go all-in with a playable hand. Folding preserves chips for a spot where I have better equity and higher expected value.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Nine offsuit (Ad 9s) is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG), which functionally acts as the Cutoff. I have a significant chip lead (16,650) and want to maintain aggression. I'll raise to 300 to build the pot and put pressure on the blinds. I'm mindful of Claude in the Small Blind, who is short-stacked with only 1,265 chips (12.6 BBs); if he shoves, I will likely have the equity to call his jam. Against the deeper stacks of Elon and Minni, I'll proceed with caution if they show significant resistance.

Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise from Flash, who has a strong range. Even on the button, this is a standard fold to avoid difficult postflop spots, especially multiway potential with deep stacks and a short stack in Claude. Preserving stack for better spots in this long tournament.

K8 offsuit from small blind against UTG raise has marginal equity (~30-35%) against Flash's opening range, falling short of the 36% needed for break-even pot odds. My short stack (1,265) and worst position (small blind out of position) make this a poor spot to invest chips. I should preserve my stack for higher-value situations with better position or hand strength.

Face an early-position open to 300 from Flash (UTG) with Q♦4♦ in the big blind. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~30.8% equity (200/(450+200)), but Q4s is heavily dominated by an UTG raising range (AQ, KQ, Qx, broadways, suited connectors) and has poor postflop playability out of position. A tiny 3‑bet to 500 is a thin, high-variance bluff that the opener will often call or 4‑bet; with ~117 BB I can wait for a much better spot. I’ll conserve my stack and fold this marginal holding preflop.

Qd8s is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by many better hands. From UTG in a 4-handed game, even with deeper stacks, my opening range should be tighter to avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position. No prior action, pot is small, but folding preserves stack for better spots in this long tournament (21/150 hands). Opponents like Minni are aggressive, but this hand doesn't warrant entering the pot.

Pocket jacks (JJ) is a premium starting hand that ranks in the top tier of all poker hands. Playing from the button gives me excellent positional advantage, and with Elon already folded, I'm only facing the blinds. At my current stack depth of 1265 chips, I need to play aggressively with strong hands to build value. A raise to 400 total chips (300 on top of the 100 to call) accomplishes several objectives: (1) it builds the pot with a strong hand, (2) it puts pressure on the blinds to fold or commit, and (3) it leaves me with 865 chips remaining for post-flop play if called. This is a standard aggressive raise that appropriately reflects the strength of my holding while managing my short-stack situation strategically.

I’m in the SB with T♠ J♥ facing a 400 button open (Claude only has 865). This is a short-stack open with a wide/shove-capable range. JTo has decent playability and equity vs a wide button range and vs the BB’s likely defense, so I prefer to keep the pot controlled and see a flop rather than inflate the pot with a marginal 3-bet. A min‑3bet to 700 is unlikely to fold the deep BB and would commit unnecessary chips; folding is too tight here. Plan: call 350, see the flop, and play post‑flop based on texture (c-bet good, pot‑control or fold to heavy action on dry boards).

I am in the Big Blind with 8h Th, a strong suited connector. Claude, on the button, has raised to 400 and is extremely short-stacked with only 465 chips remaining behind. Minni has called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 900, and it costs me 300 to call, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. My hand plays very well in multi-way pots, and I have a significant chip lead. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and potentially eliminate Claude if the board connects, while maintaining a stack that can handle post-flop play against Minni.

Check. The Q♠-9♥-5♦ flop strongly favors the button’s opening range (lots of Qx, Kx, broadways) and I’m out of position to both the BB and the button. My T♠J♥ has no pair, only a K-gutshot (4 clean outs) and thin pair-outs that generally still lose to a likely Q. Claude is short-stacked and shove-capable, so betting here risks facing a shove that I’m a big favorite against the range (I’d need roughly ~29–30% equity to call a full shove ~865 into this pot) while my realistic equity to beat a Qx by the river is well below that (~16% to hit a king). Better to keep the pot small and see a turn card. Plan: check and evaluate — call a small c-bet to realize equity, fold to a large shove or overbet, and only become aggressive if I pick up a K (straight) or a safe two‑pair/strong improvement on the turn.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw with the 8h Th on a 9h 5d Qs board. Since Minni (Small Blind) has already checked and Claude (the pre-flop raiser) is short-stacked and yet to act, checking is the most appropriate move. This allows me to see how Claude reacts and potentially see a turn card for free or a small price if Claude bets, while keeping the pot controlled against the other deep stack, Minni.

With pocket jacks on a dry flop (9h 5d Qs), I have a very strong hand that's likely ahead of my opponents' ranges. Both Minni and Flash checked the flop, indicating weakness. My pair of jacks has no obvious threats on this board - there are no flush draws, and the only potential straight would require specific cards (8-K combos for a straight, which are unlikely given the preflop action).

I'm in a short-stack situation with 865 chips remaining after my preflop raise. I need to build value while I can with this premium hand. A bet of 450 chips accomplishes several objectives:

1. **Bet sizing**: 450 is roughly 37.5% of the pot (1200), which is an appropriately aggressive but not committed bet size
2. **Value building**: Gets money in the middle with my strongest hand so far in this tournament
3. **Stack management**: Leaves me 415 chips remaining, so I maintain some flexibility on future streets
4. **Thin protection**: Prevents opponents from getting a free turn card while they have position
5. **Inducing calls**: A measured bet is more likely to induce calls from weaker hands (underpairs, Qx with weak kickers) than an all-in shove

Both opponents have substantial stacks and checked, suggesting they may have missed or have marginal holdings. This is the right moment to bet my strong hand and build the pot.

Call 450. I have T♠J♥ on Q♠-9♥-5♦: this is an open‑ended straight draw (8 clean outs — four Ks and four 8s). Hitting by the river is ~31.4%, while the call is 450 into a 1650 pot (required equity ≈ 21.4%), so this is +EV to see more cards. Claude is short and shove‑capable, but his flop sizing is small; I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with only a draw. Raising would either fold out weaker hands I have good equity against or get me committed facing a shove. Plan: call to realize equity, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a large polarized shove/overbet if necessary, and only become aggressive if I improve to a straight or a clear two‑pair/strong holding.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and a backdoor heart flush draw with 8h Th on a 9h 5d Qs board. The pot is currently 2100 and it costs 450 to call, giving me pot odds of approximately 4.67-to-1. I need roughly 18% equity to make this call profitable. With 4 outs to a straight plus backdoor potential, I have sufficient equity to see the turn, especially since I am closing the action on this street and Claude is extremely short-stacked, limiting the potential for further heavy betting from his side.

Check. I have T♠J♥ on Q♠-9♥-5♦-5♥ — I only have an 8‑out straight (four Ks and four 8s) to make a strong hand (~31.5% to hit by the river). I’m out of position in a multiway pot (Flash behind me and a very short‑stacked Claude who already fired the flop), so betting here just bloats the pot and risks being forced to call a shove. Claude has only ~415 left and can easily shove; I don’t want to commit with a draw without seeing the river. Plan: keep the pot controlled — check and realize equity. If I face a small bet I’ll consider calling to see the river; I will fold to a large shove/overbet unless I pick up a made hand on the river.

The turn 5h has significantly improved my hand, giving me a heart flush draw to go along with my gutshot straight draw. Minni has checked, and the action is now on me with Claude (the short stack) yet to act. Claude only has 415 chips remaining. By checking, I allow Claude to potentially commit his last few chips or see a free river card. If Claude shoves his small remaining stack, the pot odds will be extremely favorable for me to call and try to realize my equity. There is no strategic advantage to betting into a dry side pot against Minni at this stage when I am still on a draw.

Pocket jacks is a premium hand, and both opponents checked the turn on a relatively blank 5h, indicating weakness. With only 415 chips remaining and a substantial 2550-chip pot, I'm in critical short-stack territory. This is the moment to commit all remaining chips with my likely best hand. A bet of 415 applies maximum pressure, builds the pot when ahead, and forces opponents to make immediate decisions rather than allowing a free river card. Given the weakness shown and my hand strength, this is a value bet/shove.

Call 415. Claude is effectively all‑in for 415 into a 2,965 pot; calling 415 makes the total pot 3,380 so I need ~415/3,380 ≈ 12.3% equity to break even. With T♠J♥ on Q♠-9♥-5♦-5♥ I have an 8‑out straight draw (four Ks and four 8s) — 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. That exceeds the required equity, so a call is +EV versus Claude’s all‑in. Raising OOP here is poor: Claude is already all‑in (no extra fold equity there), it bloats a multiway pot versus Flash, and I don’t want to commit more with only a one‑card draw. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my stack size. Plan: call to realize equity and see the river; if Flash raises large on the turn I will fold to avoid committing with just a draw; if I hit the straight on the river I’ll look to extract value, and if I miss I’ll fold to significant river aggression.

I have 8h Th on a board of 9h 5d Qs 5h, giving me a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 outs total). The pot is 3380, and it costs 415 to call Claude's all-in bet, which Minni has already called. This gives me pot odds of over 8-to-1. I have roughly 24% equity to hit my draw on the river, and I only need about 11% equity to make this call profitable. Since I am closing the action on the turn and Claude is all-in, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. Raising is unnecessary as I am still on a draw and would only be inflating a side pot against Minni.

I hold J♥T♠ and the river J♠ gives me second pair (pair of Jacks) on a paired board 9h-5d-Qs-5h-Js. Claude is already all‑in and committed (I’ll see showdown with him no matter what), and Flash has been passive (check/call lines) — his range looks like Qx/9x/Jx or cautious value hands. Betting OOP in a multiway pot with a mediocre second pair has poor fold equity and is likely to be called by hands that beat me (Qx, any full house) while only extracting thin value from worse hands that may not call. I’ll check down to avoid bloating the pot and preserve chips; plan to call a small river probe but fold to large overbets that represent full houses/quads.

On the river, the Jack of Spades completes my straight (8-9-10-J-Q). The board has paired Fives, which introduces the possibility of a full house, although the passive play from Minni (check-calling) makes it less likely she is holding a monster. Claude is already all-in, so there is no more action from him. Since Minni has checked to me, checking back is the safest play to ensure a showdown. This guarantees I can win the main pot with my straight without risking a side-pot confrontation against Minni, who could potentially have a higher straight (K-T) or a full house if she were trapping. Given my tournament position and stack size, protecting my lead and taking the pot at showdown is the optimal low-risk strategy.

I'm holding As7h in UTG position with a short stack (3795 chips, ~37.95 BB). While this hand has an ace and could see action, As7o is a marginal hand from early position that's vulnerable to re-raises. Given my stack constraints and UTG position, I should be selective and wait for stronger holdings to make my stand. Folding here preserves my chips for situations where I have better equity, such as premium pairs or broadway cards. With only 22 hands played, there's ample opportunity to find better spots to accumulate chips.

Button with 4♦6♠ — a weak, unconnected offsuit hand. I have positional advantage, but both blinds (Flash in SB and Elon in BB) have shown willingness to defend and play back; a light raise risks a 3‑bet or sticky calls from two players and I don’t want to inflate the pot with such a marginal holding. Calling 100 is cheap given the 150 pot and lets me realize equity and use position to fold to aggression or continue only with a real piece (pair, strong draw). Plan: see the flop in position, proceed only with meaningful flops or good turn/river opportunities; avoid bloating the pot out of position or folding to large pressure.

I am holding AQo in the Small Blind, which is a very strong premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni limped from the Button, which typically suggests a wide or capped range. I want to raise here to build a pot, take initiative, and potentially fold out the Big Blind. A standard raise size over one limper in this position is around 4-5 times the big blind. I will raise to 450.

Preflop in the big blind with K7o offsuit facing a 4.5x raise from the small blind after a button limp. The pot is 650 and it costs 350 to call, offering about 2:1 odds, but K7o has poor post-flop playability out of position against a raiser's range and a limper. Flash's raise from SB could be wide for value/steal, but K7o dominates few hands in their range and is vulnerable to domination (e.g., KQ, KJ). Minni's limp suggests weakness or trapping, complicating dynamics. With deep stacks (~100BB), no need to speculate with a marginal hand; folding preserves stack for better spots. No strong blockers or equity to justify calling or 3-betting.

I was planning to see a flop after calling the 100, but Flash’s raise to 450 changes the math. Pot is 650 and it costs me 350 more to call, so I need ~35% equity to break even. 4♦6♠ is a weak, unconnected offsuit hand that will be dominated a lot by a typical SB raising range (pairs, Ax, broadways) and won’t have the equity to justify calling here. 3-betting as a bluff is too thin — I have poor blockers and Flash is an aggressive player who can easily 3-bet or call and make me pay to realize very little equity. With healthy stacks I’d rather conserve chips and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

J♥6♠ is a weak, unsuited hand and is a fold from UTG in a 4‑hand game. There are three players to act behind (including an aggressive button and a defending big blind with ~37BB), so I’d be out of position and likely dominated or facing raises. Calling 100 to see a flop will often put me in multiway pots with few profitable backdoors; raising is unnecessarily loose this early with a healthy 103BB stack. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey of spades. While I have the positional advantage, this hand is significantly below average in strength. 4-handed play allows for wider opening ranges, but Ten-Trey suited is still a weak candidate for an open, especially with Claude being relatively short-stacked in the Big Blind and potentially looking for a spots to jam. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folded to me in the small blind after UTG and Button folds, creating an excellent stealing opportunity. K3s is suited with a strong king blocker, good post-flop playability including flush draw potential, straight draw possibilities, and dominates weaker kings. BB will defend wide, but we have good equity realization in position and can fold to a 3-bet. Raising small builds the pot or takes down the blinds frequently. Standard size around 3x BB. Avoid limping as it plays poorly OOP multiway or vs BB raise.

8-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with minimal showdown value. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (400 in pot, 200 to call), the hand has very poor equity against Elon's raising range. Out of position from the big blind with a small stack (3695), calling would put me in a difficult postflop situation against an aggressive opponent. Even against a min-raise, 8-2 is too weak to defend. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game, which is effectively the Cutoff. While A-high hands have some value in short-handed play, A4o is a very weak ace that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a substantial chip lead and the players behind me (particularly Minni and Elon) have shown aggressive and sticky tendencies in previous hands, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to exploit my stack advantage.

Folded to the button pre-flop with 5s8h offsuit. This hand is playable from the button in a 4-handed game due to excellent position on the blinds. Opening range from BTN vs blinds typically includes 45-55% of hands, encompassing low offsuit connectors/gappers like 58o for steals and post-flop playability (straight potential, floats). Blinds are loose based on history (frequent calls), but position mitigates that. Claude has ~36BB (shorter stack, wider defense possible), Minni deep. Standard 3x open to 300 to build pot or take it down immediately. Risk-reward favorable for stack maximization in tournament.
Q5o is a weak hand with minimal equity from the small blind out of position against Elon's raise. With 36.5 BBs stack depth, I should be selective and avoid marginal spots. Clear fold.

Holding 2♦3♠ (2h3s) in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (3x) — this is a very weak, largely dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Pot is 450 and calling 200 (300 total commitment) gives ~30% pot odds, but my equity vs a BTN opening range is well below that. A light 3-bet bluff here is thin with no specific read and would bloat pot unnecessarily. With ~103bb effective I should preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop UTG in a 4-handed game with QcTd offsuit. This is a marginal hand at best for an open-raise; it's offsuit, has poor playability postflop, and is easily dominated by better queens or tens. Standard opening range UTG 4-handed typically includes QTs+ but folds QTo. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. Folding preserves stack for premium spots, especially deep-stacked early in the tournament (hand 25/150). Opponents like Minni are aggressive, so better to wait.

Calling with 8s5s on the button. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds (100 to win 150) with a speculative suited connector in excellent position. This hand has reasonable equity potential for flushes, straights, and pairs. Stack size (36bb) suggests selective aggression is wise - calling to see the flop in position with good odds is optimal. If I whiff the flop, I can fold cheaply; if I hit, I can extract maximum value from my remaining chips.

Holding 5♥4♦ in the small blind facing a button limp and a big blind behind is a marginal spot. This is an unsuited, low connected hand with poor playability out of position; it only has weak straight equity and no flush potential. Calling 50 to play a likely 3‑way pot gives poor equity and forces tough postflop decisions; raising to isolate isn’t attractive either because both the button and the big blind will often call, leaving me out of position in a bloated pot. With ~102bb effective and good SPR opportunities later, preserve chips for better spots — fold and wait for a stronger hand or a clearer opportunity to attack.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven of diamonds. Claude limped from the button and Minni folded the small blind. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can improve my hand without committing more chips out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven diamonds. The flop is King-high with two hearts (2h 9h Kc), and I have missed completely. Since I am out of position against Claude, who limped from the button, the standard play is to check and see what he does. I have no reason to bluff into a limped pot this early with no equity.

Betting 175 on a draw-heavy flop in position. I have 8♠5♠ against a checked BB, providing ~15-16 outs to improve. The dry 2♥9♥K♣ board combined with Flash's check indicates weakness. This bet maintains fold equity (crucial with 35 BB), can take down the pot immediately, and builds it when I have equity. The bet size (~70% pot) balances aggression with stack preservation.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven of diamonds and have completely missed the flop of King-Nine-Deuce. Claude, who limped from the button, has placed a bet of 175 into a 250 pot. Given that I have no pair, no draws, and am out of position, there is no reason to continue in this hand. My hand has almost no equity against any betting range, and even with decent pot odds, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

As3h in UTG is a weak hand that doesn't warrant investment. Although I have an ace, the three kicker is poor and plays poorly in early position against a full table. With my stack at 3,795 (the shortest at the table), I need to be selective and preserve chips for premium holdings. Standard UTG ranges demand at least AJ, pairs, or broadway cards. At a 4-handed table, the blinds will cycle quickly, so I can afford to fold marginal holdings and wait for better spots. Folding is disciplined and correct here.

I will open-raise to 300 (3x BB). On the button versus the SB and BB I want to take the initiative — K♥7♥ is suited, has decent playability and a King blocker, and raises often pick up the blinds or isolate one defender. 300 is a standard, controlled sizing given our 10k stack: it gives fold equity against Flash’s wide SB defense while keeping the pot manageable postflop. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a heavy shove or 3-bet from the very aggressive player), but calling 100 is too passive here.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8h 7s. Minni opened to 300 from the Button. While 87o has some connectivity, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a regular raiser like Minni. Additionally, Elon is still to act in the Big Blind and has shown aggression in previous hands. Calling 250 into a pot of 450 (requiring 35% equity) is mathematically borderline, but the positional disadvantage and the risk of being squeezed make this a clear fold in a high-roller tournament context where I want to preserve my chip lead.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). Hand is T3o, offsuit, poor connectivity, low playability, easily dominated. Minni raises frequently from button, but T3o is too weak to defend profitably even wide. Pot odds ~2.25:1 require ~30% equity, which this hand lacks vs a wide button range. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

UTG with A♠T♠ in a 4-handed game is a clear open. This is a strong, playable hand that wants to take the initiative preflop and can realize a lot of equity postflop (nut-flush and top-pair potential). My table image and recent hands show I open to 300 often; a 3bb open (300) is standard sizing here — it builds the pot with hands I dominate, pressures Claude (shorter stack) and forces action from the aggressive Button (Flash) and Elon (who has 3-bet in past hands). Plan: open to 300; if faced with a small 3-bet I can call to see a flop and play my spade equity, but I will respect very large 3-bets/overbets and fold if the price is unfavorable. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures and play draws aggressively.

I am on the button with 4d 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the under-the-gun position. Given the strength of Minni's range from that position and the low equity of my hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Qc7c is a marginal suited hand but faces an UTG raise from Minni, who frequently raises but has a strong range from early position. I'm out of position in the SB postflop, where this hand plays poorly. Pot odds are decent (250 to call into 450), but equity against Minni's range is insufficient, and multiway potential with Claude in BB adds risk. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase.

3-8 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. While I'm in the big blind and only need to call 200 more chips to see a flop with a decent pot (450), the hand has virtually no equity against Minni's raising range from UTG. Early position raises typically indicate a strong range (pairs, broadway cards, AK-AJ), and my 3-8 will be dominated in most matchups. At this stage of the tournament (hand 27 of 150), making disciplined folds with trash hands is crucial for long-term stack preservation. The cost-benefit doesn't favor calling with such a weak holding.

I am dealt 7s6s UTG in a 4-handed game. Suited connectors have good playability post-flop and are strong enough to open in short-handed play to maintain an aggressive image and balance my range. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here.

T5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Position on the button is helpful for speculative hands, but T5o doesn't connect well for straights or flushes and dominates nothing. Facing a standard open from early position with deep stacks, folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament setting where survival and chip accumulation over 150 hands is key. No fold equity or implied odds justify a call or 3-bet.

Folding 5-10 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak hand with poor equity against Flash's UTG raise. Key factors supporting the fold: (1) 5-10o is well below the threshold for defending the blind against an UTG raiser, (2) I'm out of position and will face postflop aggression, (3) The big blind (Minni) still needs to act, potentially re-raising, (4) Early in the tournament (hand 28 of 150) with a decent stack (3645), so no need to play marginal hands. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for premium situations.

I’m in the big blind with J♠9♦ facing an early-position open to 300. I’ve already got 100 in and need 200 more (total 300) to call. J9o is marginal but defendable from the BB versus a single raise — pot odds (~30%) are reasonable and stacks are deep (~104bb), so calling preserves equity and allows me to realize postflop equity. I won’t 3-bet as a light bluff vs an UTG range (too loose here) and folding is too tight. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously OOP — continue with top pair, decent draws or good turn cards; fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I haven’t improved.

Flop Jc 2d 9s with Js 9d — I’ve flopped two pair (Jacks and Nines) heads‑up vs the UTG opener. This is well ahead of most of his continuation range except for flopped sets (JJ/99/22) or the unlikely exact two‑pair ties; holding a J and a 9 also blocks some of those combos. Out of position I want to extract value and deny equity to straight draws (Q10, 10‑8, etc.), so I should bet rather than check and give a free card. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~60% pot (400 chips) to charge worse jacks, 9s, single pairs and drawing hands while keeping future options against a raise — if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely a set) and proceed cautiously.

I opened 7s6s from UTG and was called by the Big Blind (Minni). The flop of Jc-2d-9s does not hit my range particularly well, and Minni has led out with a donk bet of 400 into a 650 pot. With only 7-high and some weak backdoor draws (straight and flush), I do not have sufficient equity to continue against this aggression. While I could consider a raise as a semi-bluff, Minni's previous play suggests they can be sticky, and I have no real showdown value. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG in 4-handed play with Jd5d, a weak suited gapped hand. Poor post-flop playability, especially multiway out of position. Standard range for UTG open doesn't include J5s. Deep stacked, no need to enter pot with marginal hand pre-flop. Fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

9-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I'm on the button with Elon having already folded, this hand has minimal equity and poor playability post-flop. My stack of 3645 is healthy enough that I don't need to take marginal spots. I'll preserve my chips and wait for better opportunities. This is a clear fold.

2♦9♠ is a very weak, unsuited hand. Both Elon and Claude folded so this is heads-up vs the big blind. It costs only 50 more to see a flop into a 150 pot (excellent pot odds), and postflop I will have positional advantage, so calling is the lowest-variance, +EV play here. I won't raise with this junk hand (too many hands will have me dominated and I don't want to inflate the pot or face a 3-bet). If I miss the flop I'll fold to significant aggression; if I hit a pair or a good draw I'll proceed. Total commitment after calling will be 100 chips.

I am in the Big Blind with K9s. The Small Blind (Minni) has just completed the blind. In Hand #22, Minni also limped from the Small Blind and folded when I raised from the Big Blind. K9s is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation, and raising allows me to take control of the pot with position or win it immediately if Minni's range is weak/capped as suggested by the previous encounter.

2♦9♠ is one of the worst hands. Facing a raise to 400 (pot 500) I must call 300, which requires ~37.5% equity to be +EV. Against a normal opening range from the big blind 2‑9o has well under that equity and will be dominated frequently. I’m out of position postflop, so realizing fold equity or implied odds is poor. A 3‑bet bluff here is too spewy with this garbage holding and the opponent has shown regular preflop aggression, so I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

From UTG with A2o, folding is the correct play. While the ace has some value, the deuce kicker makes this a weak hand in early position where I need a stronger opening range. My stack of 3645 chips is moderately short, so I should preserve chips and wait for premium or strong hands. A2o has poor reverse implied odds and will struggle against the wide 3-betting and 4-betting ranges of aggressive opponents like Minni and Elon from later positions. Folding early with weak holdings allows me to stay disciplined and protected for when I have stronger hands.

I’m on the button with 4♠8♣ — a weak, unsuited hand but great position and deep stacks (≈107 BB). The pot is 150 and it’s only the blinds left to act. Calling 100 is cheap but gives less fold equity and can lead to uncomfortable multiway spots with a poor hand. Opening here puts pressure on the blinds and is standard exploitative play; opponents have shown a mix of folds and calls but not constant 3-betting. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing (300 total) to try to take the blinds down or play in position if called. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll c-bet on dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards.

I am holding AQo in the small blind, which is a very strong premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni has opened from the button, a position from which players typically have a wide opening range. To gain initiative, extract value, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position against the big blind, I will 3-bet. A sizing of 1000 (roughly 3.3x the open) is standard for being out of position and puts pressure on the button's wider range.

Preflop in BB with 3h4c offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. Facing button open to 300 and SB 3bet to 1000. Pot odds are 1400:900 (~1.55:1, requiring ~39% equity), but 34o has terrible equity vs any 3bet range, especially multiway. No playability postflop. Opponents deep-stacked; folding trash preserves stack for better spots. Clear fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 4♠8♣ and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000. The pot is ~1400 and it’s 700 to call (~7 BB). 4-8 offsuit is very weak and poorly connected to most flops; although I’d be in position postflop, I’m likely dominated by SB’s 3-bet range (pocket pairs, broadways, suited connectors) and don’t have the equity or playability to justify continuing. A 4‑bet bluff would require a large sizing (min to 1700) and unnecessarily risks chips with a marginal hand. I’ll stick to my plan and fold, preserve my ~10.47k stack, and wait for a better, playable spot on the button.

Fold. 6♠2♣ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor kicker and negligible playability out of UTG. I’m first to act with three players to act behind, so this will often become a multiway pot or face aggression—both bad for this holding. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives ~28.6% pot odds, but my equity against standard opening ranges (and potential 3-bets) is well below that. Attempting a steal by min-raising is reckless from UTG with this hand given deeper stacks (Flash 15785, Elon 10050) and a competent big blind (Claude 3545). Preserve the ~10470 stack and wait for a profitable spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Five suited. While this is a potential opening hand in a short-handed game to steal the blinds, J5s is at the very bottom of a button opening range. Claude in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked (35BB), making him more likely to shove over a light open. Given my current chip lead and the marginal nature of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play here to wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in SB with Q2o after UTG and Button folds, isolating BB. Q2o is a marginal hand but has Q blocker and decent high card. 4-handed play widens ranges significantly; SB steal vs BB is standard with wide range (~50%+). Pot 150 to win by raising min to 200 (pot becomes 300). Claude's history shows some calling stations but folds pre often enough; equity realization postflop acceptable if called (positionally disadvantaged but deep stacks). Folding dead SB money is suboptimal long-term for tournament stack maximization. Raising exploits dead money and position effectively.

I'm in the Big Blind with 8s 4d, one of the weakest hands in poker. Elon min-raised from the Small Blind to 200, putting in only 100 more chips. While this small raise might suggest weakness and I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips, several factors make folding the correct play:

1. **Hand Quality**: 8-4 offsuit is virtually unplayable. It has almost no showdown value and weak kicker problems.

2. **Position**: I'll be out of position post-flop against Elon, which is a significant disadvantage with a weak hand.

3. **Stack Depth**: With 3,545 chips, I have 35 BBs - a moderate stack that I should protect with better hands.

4. **Risk-Reward**: Even though I'm getting decent pot odds, the hand is too weak to justify continued play. I'll better preserve chips for spots where I have a genuine advantage.

Discipline is crucial in tournament poker. Folding weak hands from the BB helps maintain chip preservation for premium situations.

I am the chip leader and holding 8d 6d in the UTG position (4-max). This is a speculative but playable hand that adds balance to my opening range. Raising to 300 is a standard size that allows me to take control of the pot or see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop playability and straight/flush potential. Minni has been active, so I need to be cautious if she 3-bets, but generally, I want to maintain pressure given my stack size.

Preflop on the button with Ts3h, a complete trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards with no high-card strength or straight/flush potential. Flash's UTG raise indicates strength, and calling 300 into a 450 pot offers poor implied odds even in position. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy against aggressive opponents like Flash and Minni.

Q4o from the small blind against a UTG raiser is a marginal hand with poor equity. I'm out of position and facing Flash who has a strong stack and showed strength with an early position raise. My stack size (3495) is moderate and I should preserve chips by playing tight. Q4o doesn't have the showdown value or potential to justify calling 250 more chips here. Folding the small blind (losing my 50 chip investment) is the correct strategic play. I'll wait for stronger hands in better positions to build my stack.

I’m in the big blind with Kh6h facing an open to 300 from Flash. Stacks are deep (~100+ BB) and this is effectively heads-up vs a player who opens a lot. K6s is marginal but has suitedness and decent playability — it’s worth defending from the big blind rather than 3-betting out of position. Calling 200 gives me ~30% pot odds (200 to win ~650) and allows me to see a flop and realize equity; a small 3-bet OOP with this hand is suboptimal. Plan: call, play the flop cautiously (fold to heavy aggression when missed, continue with a king/6/heart or strong draw).

Flop: I have K♥6♥ on 3♠ K♦ 5♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker on a dry board with no heart or straight draws. Flash is the preflop raiser and is an aggressive, frequent opener. With deep stacks I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal K6. Checking preserves my ability to call a standard continuation bet and to fold to heavy aggression (or raise vs a clear blowout), while also allowing me to induce bluffs from an aggressive opponent. Plan: check and evaluate. If Flash c-bets a small-to-medium size (~<=½ pot ≈ 300–350) I will call to realize equity and control the pot; if he fires a large overbet/raise I will strongly consider folding unless I improve on the turn. If he checks back I take it to showdown cheaply — I’m happy to let him bluff off or show air and keep stacks intact for later hands.

On a very dry King-high board (Kd-5c-3s), I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Although 8d 6d has missed the flop completely, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. Minni's check-call tendency is noted, but I must represent the King to take down the pot now. A bet of roughly 40% of the pot should be enough to fold out her non-pairing hands and weak draws while keeping my risk relatively low if she decides to continue.

Flash is an aggressive, frequent opener and has fired a small c-bet (275 into the ~650 pre-bet pot, pot now 925). I hold K♥6♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker on a relatively dry board (3♠ K♦ 5♣). The small sizing is consistent with a wide c-bet range (value Kx, some pairs/sets, and many bluffs/floaters). With deep stacks and OOP, my priority is pot control and realizing equity rather than inflating the pot with a marginal kicker. Calling 275 to win ~1200 gives ~23% odds and keeps worse Ks and bluffs in the pot; it also lets me see a turn and fold to clear, large aggression. Plan: call now, check/ call a standard turn continuation if he barrels small, fold to a large raise/overbet unless I improve (6, heart, or a very favorable card), and extract thin value on safe rivers against his frequent barreling.

I have K♥6♥ on 3♠ K♦ 5♣ 7♠ with pot ≈1200. This is top pair with a very weak kicker and I’m out of position versus an aggressive, frequent opener who fired a small flop c-bet (wide range: Kx, pairs, floats and bluffs). With deep stacks I prioritize pot control and realizing equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal K6. Checking keeps his bluffs and worse Kx hands in the pot and lets me call reasonable barrels; I’ll fold to a very large shove/overbet unless I improve. Plan: check and react on Flash’s action — call small/medium bets to realize equity, fold to clear heavy aggression, and take showdown cheaply if he checks back.

On the turn, the 7s gives me an open-ended straight draw (8-7-6-5). Since Minni has shown a strong tendency to check-call across many previous hands (notably Hand #11 and Hand #13), a second barrel bluff is less likely to succeed here. By checking behind, I take a free card to realize my equity and potentially hit a 4 or 9 on the river. This also keeps the pot manageable and disguises my hand strength if I do complete the straight.

I have top pair K♥6♥ but a very weak kicker on a paired river (3♠ K♦ 5♣ 7♠ 7♥). Villain is an aggressive, frequent opener who c-bet small on the flop then checked the turn and river — consistent with a wide, mostly missed range or pot-control with Kx. The river pairing also gives any 7 in his hand trips/quads, and many Kx (with kickers >6) already beat me. Betting here would either fold out worse hands or get called by better ones, so it’s low EV. With deep stacks and a relatively small pot, I preserve chips and take showdown cheaply. Plan: check to see his action; call a small value bet, fold to a large overbet/shove.

I am holding 8d 6d on a board of 3s Kd 5c 7s 7h. I missed my open-ended straight draw that I picked up on the turn. My opponent, Minni, has shown a significant tendency to be a 'calling station' in previous hands (notably Hand #11 and Hand #13), where she check-calls multiple streets with wide ranges. On the flop, she check-called my continuation bet, suggesting she has at least some piece of the board or a drawing hand. Since the board paired on the river and I have no showdown value with 8-high, the only way to win is to bluff. However, bluffing a player who frequently check-calls is a low-percentage play. Given my chip lead and the tournament context, it is wiser to check back, concede the pot, and avoid losing more chips to a player likely to call with any pair or even Ace-high.

UTG with AQo offsuit in a 4-handed game is a strong premium hand warranting a raise. Standard opening range here includes AQo+, so I raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, deny equity to weaker hands, and take initiative. Opponents like Minni and Flash are frequent raisers but also callers; Claude is short-stacked but this hand holds up well. Deep stacks allow for post-flop play if called. Folding or limping is too passive and misses value.

Elon has raised to 300 from UTG with a standard 3x raise. While A7o is a decent hand from the button in isolation, several factors make folding the correct play here: (1) My stack is critically short at 3495 chips - calling 300 would leave me only 3195, putting me in a precarious spot if I miss the flop; (2) Minni and Flash still have to act behind me, and one of them could re-raise, forcing me into a difficult decision; (3) UTG raises tend to represent stronger hands than other positions; (4) A7o is not strong enough to risk my tournament life against the remaining action. With a short stack, I need to be more selective and wait for premium spots. Folding here preserves chips for a better situation where I can get closer to all-in with a superior hand.

I’m in the small blind with 7♠6♣ facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250 to make it 300). This is a marginal, unsuited connector that performs poorly out of position versus an UTG opening range and will often be dominated. Calling requires ~36% equity (250 into a 700 pot after my call) which 7-6o is unlikely to have against an UTG-range, and if I call I’ll be OOP against the raiser and still have the big blind to navigate. Squeezing/raising is also a poor play here — I have little fold equity and get crushed by better hands or a re-raise. With a healthy 110BB stack and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct long-term decision.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h 6h. Elon opened from UTG to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.25 to 1. While I am out of position, 96s has decent playability and the price is right to defend my blind, especially as the chip leader. Folding is too tight given the suited nature of the hand, and raising is unnecessary against a standard UTG open.

I have missed the flop completely with 9-high. As the Big Blind defending against an UTG raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I have very little equity on this 10-high board and no reason to lead out.

Preflop raise with AQo OOP was standard, and Flash's BB call suggests a wide defensive range including suited connectors, Ax, broadways, pairs. Flop is 4c-3s-Th rainbow, very dry with minimal draws (gutshots like 52, J5? unlikely), and disconnected. Missed completely but Ace-high blocks AA/AK/AT and has good showdown value. As PFR OOP, Flash checks (weakness), so continuation bet exploits fold equity against floats/air while getting value from weaker Ax/Tx/pairs. Small sizing ~60% pot balances range, denies equity to overcards/draws, and keeps pot manageable post-flop. Checking risks giving free card to draws/improvers and loses initiative. Effective stacks deep (~100BB), so bet-fold ok if raised.

I defended the Big Blind with 9h 6h against an UTG raise. The flop of 10-high (4c 3s Th) has missed me completely. I have no pair and no significant draw. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a bet of 400 into a 650 pot. Given that I have 9-high and very little equity against an UTG range on this board, there is no justification for calling or raising. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5-2 offsuit is the worst hand in poker and is unplayable from any position. From UTG (the earliest position) where I have no information about opponent actions, folding is the clear choice. My stack of 3495 chips is moderate-sized, so I need to be selective and preserve chips by folding marginal and weak hands. This is a textbook fold situation.

I’m on the Button with Q♠T♥ — a playable broadway hand with good post‑flop potential and lots of fold equity from the blinds. In position I should be opening this hand rather than limping or folding. A standard sized open (to 300) is consistent with the table, applies pressure to the SB/BB, and preserves maneuverability postflop. If I get single‑called I’ll play aggressively in position (c‑bet dry flops, evaluate turns); if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll largely fold as QTo is often dominated. Raising to 300 maximizes expected value here.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations. Given the strength of my hand relative to a standard Button opening range, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

Preflop in the big blind with 4c7d offsuit, a very weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni on the button, who has been aggressive in previous hands with frequent raises from late position. Flash in SB already folded, isolating to Minni. Pot is 450, to call 200, but out of position postflop with trash, equity is poor against her wide button range. Raising has no fold equity with this hand and commits too many chips. Deep stacks (~100BB), no need to defend wide here. Standard fold to preserve stack.

Holding J♣5♥ UTG in a 4-handed game: this is a weak offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability and is easily dominated by typical opening ranges. Opening here would likely put me out of position against the Button and the blinds and could induce calls or a shove from the short‑stacked Big Blind (Claude ~33bb), creating awkward multiway or shove decisions with little equity. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left to play, I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am on the button with King-Six suited (Ks 6s). In a 4-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. Minni has folded from UTG, leaving only the blinds to act. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Claude is relatively short-stacked at roughly 34 big blinds, so I should be prepared for a potential three-bet shove, but Ks 6s is generally too strong to fold in this position.

A5o is a weak offsuit ace with poor post-flop playability out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Flash's button opening range is wide, but calling 250 into 450 offers poor implied odds OOP, especially with the BB still to act who could squeeze or call wide. 3-betting is not justified with this hand as it doesn't block strong hands well and plays poorly post-flop. Folding preserves our stack for better spots in this deep-stacked tournament.

With J3 offsuit from the big blind, this is a straightforward fold despite the decent pot odds. While I'm getting 2.25:1 (450:200) and have position, my hand has minimal equity against Flash's button-raising range (likely 25-35%). With a short stack of 3,395 chips, I need to preserve my remaining 3,195 and wait for a premium or stronger marginal hand where I have better equity. J3o has poor playability and fold equity, making this a chip-preservation fold. Minni and Elon have already folded, so there's no additional value to contest this pot with such a weak hand.

I am in UTG position with Qh3c. This is a very weak hand even in a 4-handed game. My stack is healthy at 150 BBs, and there is no reason to open with such a low-equity hand from early position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

A9o is a strong hand from the button in a 4-handed game, with excellent position on both blinds. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is sized appropriately given the pot of 150 and min-raise of 200, maintaining fold equity while not overcommitting with deep stacks.

I have AK, one of the strongest starting hands in poker. From the small blind facing Elon's raise to 300, I should play this aggressively. Raising to 900 total accomplishes several things: (1) it takes control of the hand, (2) it narrows the field, (3) it builds the pot when I have a premium holding, and (4) it puts pressure on both Elon and Minni to fold or commit with weaker holdings. With my stack at 3345, raising to 900 leaves me with 2445, which is a manageable position if the hand goes postflop. AK performs best when played aggressively preflop, especially when short-stacked relative to opponents. This is a standard play with premium holdings from the blinds facing an open raise.

Facing a button open to 300 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900, I hold 5♦2♦ in the big blind. Calling 800 into a 1,300 pot requires roughly 38% equity — 5‑2s has very poor equity versus a typical 3‑bet (often QQ+/AK or polarized) and performs worse multi‑way and out of position. Claude is short (≈2,495) so his 3‑bet is likely commit‑able and could turn into a shove, forcing a difficult decision with garbage. A minimum 4‑bet to 1,500 would be an overly spewy bluff here and can get me into a costly all‑in. Preserving my healthy stack early in the tournament and waiting for a much better spot is the correct play, so I fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the SB after my button open with A9o. Position is excellent on the button heads-up. A9o is a strong calling hand here: ace-high with good postflop playability, straight potential, dominates weaker aces, and blocks AA/AK. Claude's SB 3-bet range after folding BB is likely polarized (QQ+/AK for value, some suited broadway/bluffs), against which A9o has solid equity (~35-45%) and plays well in position. Pot odds are favorable (600 into 1300 for ~3:1). Calling keeps the SPR manageable (post-call pot ~1900, his remaining ~1600 after his 900 committed), allowing postflop flexibility without overcommitting. Folding is too tight given position and hand strength. 4-betting risks him jamming his ~25BB effective stack, where A9o is often behind value and folding to a jam loses the initiative poorly. Prefer to realize equity OOP no, wait IP.

With AK on a J76s flop, I have approximately 15 clean outs (spade flush draw plus ace and king overcards), giving me ~55% equity against Elon's likely holdings. Betting 1200 (2/3 pot) builds the pot while I have strong equity, applies pressure to Elon's range, and maintains aggressive control of the hand. This sizing is appropriately balanced relative to my shorter stack, leaving me flexibility for future action while achieving my goal of value-building with a premium draw.

Facing a donk-lead of 1200 into 3100 from short-stacked Claude (1295 behind) on a highly coordinated monotone spade flop Js6s7s. Holding As9d gives the nut flush draw (9 outs), ace-high blocker, and backdoor straight possibilities. Claude's SB 3-bet preflop range (polarized: QQ+/AK value, broadway bluffs) hits this board reasonably: possible sets (77/66), two-pair (J7s/J6s unlikely), TP w/draw (AJs/KQs/QJs), or worse draws (KsTs/9s8s etc.). Nut FD has 35-50%+ equity vs his range, excellent vs worse draws/pairs he might hero-call with. Position on button heads-up. Min-raising to 2400 (adding 1200) forces him to call 1200 more (pot-odds ~4:1 post-call) with his last 1295, realizing equity while extracting value from overpairs, weaker draws, and trapped hands. Calling risks poor SPR dynamics (his short stack likely checks turn or shoves tiny), denying value on bricks. Larger raise/shove unnecessary as he can't call more than 1295 and fold equity minimal against committed shorty. Opponents deep but folded pre. Goal: stack-off profitably vs short stack.

Analyzing the current situation:

**Hand Strength:** I hold AK with one card being the King of Spades. On a board of Js 6s 7s (three spades), I have a King-high flush draw (9 outs), plus two overcards. This is a strong drawing hand with significant equity.

**Stack Situation:** I have 1295 chips remaining. I've already committed 2100 preflop and on the flop. To call Elon's raise costs 1200 more, leaving me with only 95 chips - effectively all-in.

**Pot Odds:** The pot is 5500. I'm calling 1200 to win 5500, giving me approximately 4.6:1 odds. With a King-high flush draw plus overcard possibilities, I have roughly 45-50% equity, making this a profitable call.

**Opponent Analysis:** Elon is aggressive and could have various holdings - pairs, overcards, or even his own draw. His aggressive line doesn't necessarily indicate a made hand; it could be a continuation bet or even another draw.

**Strategic Rationale:** At this extreme short stack, I'm committed to the pot either way. AK with a K-high flush draw is precisely the type of hand to commit with. The flush draw combined with overcards gives me legitimate winning chances. Folding here abandons too much equity in a situation where I'm obligated to play tight to rebuild my stack.

Call and go to the turn.

With my stack reduced to 95 chips after the flop action, I'm analyzing the turn situation with Js 6s 7s Qc board and my Ah Ks. Current hand analysis: I have a 4-card flush in spades (King-high) and multiple outs including 9 spade outs for a flush, 3 Ace outs, and 3 King outs - approximately 14 clean outs giving me roughly 30% equity. With a pot of 6700 and only 95 chips remaining, I'm in an extreme short-stack situation where pot odds heavily favor action. The implied odds (6700:95 ≈ 71:1) require only ~1.4% equity to break even, and my 30% equity is well above this threshold. At this stack depth, fold equity considerations are minimal - my 95-chip bet barely impacts the pot dynamics. Going all-in accomplishes three things: (1) commits my remaining stack with positive expected value, (2) gives my drawing hand the chance to improve against likely calls, and (3) is mathematically sound given the massive pot odds. Checking would only delay the inevitable and reduce my ability to win the hand immediately. With 14 outs and such favorable odds, this is a clear mathematical commit.

Heads-up vs short-stacked Claude in SB after preflop 3-bet/call and flop raise/call where I had the nut flush draw on a monotone spade board. He now shoves his final 95 chips into a 6700 pot on the Qc turn brick (no spade, opens some straight draws like T9/89 but doesn't complete mine). Pot odds are exceptional (~71:1 implied), making this an automatic call regardless of equity. A9o (As blocker) has decent showdown value as ace-high, blocks some of his strong flush hands (e.g., Axs), and remains live vs his possible overpairs, two-pair combos (J7/J6), weaker draws he flatted flop with, or bluffs. Folding wastes the massive overlay; we realize equity cheaply with position into river. His history shows aggression but also calling stations (e.g., Hand 5,13). This spot maximizes EV by calling down profitably against his polarized range.

Preflop from UTG with 4sTc, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low playability, and no high-card strength. With pocket deuces on the button, I'm in an excellent position to play this hand. The situation is now heads-up with Minni (small blind) and Flash (big blind) after Elon folded. I need to call 100 to win a pot of 150, giving me 1.5:1 odds. Pocket pairs have strong playability in position, especially on the button where I'll act last post-flop. While a small pair, deuces can easily make a set and win a large pot. Folding would be unnecessarily tight from the button against two opponents. Raising could be reasonable, but calling allows me to control pot size while maintaining position and maximum flexibility on the flop. If I miss the flop completely, I can fold to aggression; if I hit a set, I'm in a great spot to extract value. This is a standard +EV call.

I’m in the small blind with 10♠4♦ facing a limp from the button and the big blind to act. Pocket deuces on the button in a heads-up situation against Flash's raise. I'm getting favorable pot odds (550:300, or 1.83:1) requiring only ~35% equity to call profitably. I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a dry, uncoordinated board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage here, especially on an Ace-high flop. Pocket deuces vs Flash's flop bet. I'm getting 3.125:1 odds, requiring only 24% equity to call profitably. With 2d8d from the small blind, I'm facing a 50-chip call to win the 150 in the pot. Folding weak hands in marginal spots is a core principle of +EV poker. This is exactly the type of hand to release.

I'm in the small blind with 9♥8♥ facing a button open to 350 (pot 500, I need to put in 300 more). 9♠8♠ (suited connector) has good playability and implied odds with these stacks, so I prefer to call 350 total rather than fold or make a small 3-bet. I have 9♥8♥ on T♠5♣8♦ — a made middle pair (pair of eights) with a decent 9-kicker in a multiway pot (button opened, BB called). If I face a large raise I’ll fold OOP; I’ll call small single raises to see a turn and re-evaluate. Betting also prevents giving a free turn that could complete a 2-card straight draw. Calling with K♥Q♦ on a T♠5♣8♦ flop. I have a strong premium hand with excellent equity against likely opponent ranges (35-45%), and the pot odds of 4.6:1 are favorable. Check. The turn Qh is a dangerous overcard — I currently have a marginal middle pair (pair of eights with a 9 kicker) in a multiway pot (pot 2250, my stack 10645). I’ll check to see the river and preserve chips — I will call small-to-medium river bets to realize equity, fold to large aggression if I don’t improve, and value-bet or raise only if I actually hit (9/8/J or trips).

I have successfully completed a Queen-high straight (8-9-10-Jack-Queen) on the turn. Betting 1200 on the turn with top pair, top kicker (K♥Q♦ on T♠5♣8♦Q♥). The turn card (Q♥) significantly improved my hand, and both opponents' checks suggest weakness. I have 9♥8♥ on T♠5♣8♦Q♥ — a marginal middle pair (pair of eights) with a 9-kicker. Pot is 3450 and Claude (BTN) has just raised to 1200 after two checks. I have turned a Queen-high straight (8-9-10-J-Q). This is currently the second-best possible hand, losing only to King-Jack which would form a King-high straight. Calling the all-in with K♥Q♦ (top pair, top kicker) on T♠5♣8♦Q♥. Flash's check on the turn followed by an aggressive check-raise suggests a draw or medium holding rather than a strong made hand. While I have a piece of the board, it is highly vulnerable to any heart, overcard, or straight-completing card. Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot and realize my equity without over-committing to a marginal hand in position.

Checked the wet flop to control the pot and gather info with a marginal but vulnerable hand. On the turn the board pairs (8h-5h-6h-6c) — I still have top pair (8s) with a 9 kicker plus a one-card heart flush draw. That combination has good equity and also needs protection: I want to charge heart draws and worse pairs/overcards while keeping the pot manageable given deep stacks. Betting ~pot (600 into 600) achieves that — it extracts value from hands that call (weaker pairs, 9x, some draws) and denies correct odds to many pure draws. If Flash check-raises very large I’ll respect that line (likely representing a made flush or trips) and fold to a shove or extreme sizing; otherwise I’ll proceed to the river and re-evaluate.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 5d. The board is 8h 5h 6h 6c. I have two pair, 6s and 5s with a Ten kicker. Minni checked the flop and has now lead out for a full pot bet (600 into 600) on the turn. This board is extremely coordinated; any heart makes a flush, and any 7 makes a straight. Furthermore, the 6c on the turn pairs the board, meaning any 8 or any 6 now beats my hand. My two pair is effectively the bottom of the range for a made hand here. Given the pot-sized bet on such a dangerous board, Minni is either representing a very strong hand (a flush, a 6, or a straight) or is executing a high-variance bluff. I do not have enough equity to justify a call, especially with one more street to play where I will likely face another large bet on many scary river cards.

I’m on the button with K♠Q♥ — a strong broadway hand in a three-handed pot. I want to open for value and use positional advantage, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB), which matches the table’s standard sizing. This puts pressure on the blinds and builds a pot I can play post‑flop. Flash (SB) is deep and sticky; Elon (BB) has shown willingness to 3‑bet to ~900 before — I’ll call a moderate 3‑bet and play in position, but fold or 4‑bet only against very large 3‑bets depending on sizing and stack-to-pot. If called, I’ll continuation-bet appropriate king/queen-high or dry boards and use position to extract value or fold when clearly behind.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened for 300. While a Button opening range is typically wide, King-Seven offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, especially with a short-stacked Big Blind (Elon) still to act who may look to squeeze or shove. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a more advantageous spot.

7h2s is one of the worst possible hands in poker: unsuited, disconnected, low cards with no high card strength or straight/flush potential against a button raise. Minni is an aggressive raiser from history, and defending the BB with trash like this leads to difficult postflop spots where we have minimal equity and are often dominated. Pot odds (200 into 450) aren't sufficient to justify calling with zero fold equity or playability. Folding preserves our stack for better spots in this tournament with 105 hands left.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds. Given my chip lead, I should maintain an aggressive posture. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

Q6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position in the small blind facing a button raise. Flash likely has a wide range, but calling 250 into 450 pot OOP with no initiative and bad equity is -EV. No good 3-bet blockers or value. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I have J♦9♣ in the big blind facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). The open is small and Flash on the button likely has a wide raising range — J9o has reasonable equity against that range and also blocks some of his broadway combos. Calling gives me ~3.25:1 pot odds (need ~23% equity), which makes defending correct from this stack depth (~56 BB). I avoid a light 3-bet because I’ll be out of position postflop and don’t want to bloat a multi-street pot without a stronger holding; a flat call preserves stack, lets me see a flop cheaply, and keeps weaker hands in his range. Plan postflop: fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K-high boards, but continue with pair or good draws and look for value/bluff opportunities on later streets.

I have top pair (J) with a weak 9 kicker on J♠ Q♦ 5♠, out of position against a wide button opening range. Pot is 650 and stacks are deep (~110 BB), so leading out OOP with a marginal one-pair hand will bloat the pot and often get raised or called by hands that dominate me (Qx, better Jx, broadways). Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range, and lets me call reasonable c-bets to realize equity. Plan: check-call small-to-medium continuation bets (~≤1/2 pot) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to large raises or apply more aggressive lines only if I improve or have a sharp read. For now: check to control size and extract value later when appropriate.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have Ace-high on a Queen-Jack high board. This board texture generally favors my perceived range as the button opener. Minni checked to me, which is standard for the big blind defender. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to represent a strong hand like a Queen, a Jack, or a high pocket pair, and to try and win the pot immediately.

I have top pair (J) with a weak 9 kicker on J♠ Q♦ 5♠. Flash's flop bet is small (325 into 975, ~1/3 pot) and from the button his range will be wide (broadways, some Qx, bluffs). Folding here surrenders too much equity against his bluffs and thin calls; raising OOP with a marginal one-pair hand would bloat the pot and risk a tough 3-bet or larger action on later streets. Calling controls the pot, keeps worse Jx/Qx and bluffs in, and gives good pot odds to see a turn. Plan: call the 325, evaluate the turn — extract value from worse hands or bluff-catch vs small turns, and fold to heavy aggression on scary cards (A/K/Q) if I don't improve.

Check. I have top pair J with a poor 9 kicker on 5♠ J♠ Q♦ A♠. Flash opened wide and his small flop c‑bet kept a very wide range (Qx, Jx, Axs, broadways, and bluffs). The turn Ace is a very “scary” card that improves many of his value combos (Ax, AQs, AJs, some two‑pair combos) and it also brings a three‑spade board, creating made flushes for anyone holding two spades. With ~10.7k behind and a 1,300 pot, leading out OOP with a marginal one‑pair hand will often get raised or priced off by hands that dominate me. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity against large turn bets, lets me call small/medium bets to realize equity, and gives me the option to re‑evaluate on the river (call small bets, fold to big aggression, or attempt a river bluff/value depending on the card and line).

The turn has brought the Ace of Spades, giving me top pair.
