While the three-spade board is a potential threat for a flush, Minni's check indicates she likely hasn't improved to a monster hand or is playing a marginal made hand like a Queen or a Jack cautiously. I will bet for value, as I likely have the best hand and want to charge any remaining draws or weaker pairs (Qx, Jx) to continue. A bet of 800 into the 1300 pot is a standard sizing to maintain pressure.

I have top pair (J) with a weak 9 kicker on a scary turn (A♠ added; board now 5♠ J♠ Q♦ A♠). Flash is a wide button opener who c‑bet small on the flop and now continues — his range contains many Ax, Qx, some better Jx, and two‑spade combos that make a flush on this turn. Folding here surrenders too much equity and value; a raise OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and get me in trouble against A‑hands, two‑spade flushes, or better Js. I am getting roughly 2.6:1 pot odds (800 to call into ~2100), so I’ll call the 800 to realize equity, keep his bluffs/worse Jx in, and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to heavy action on A/Q/spade rivers if I don’t improve, and extract value or block‑raise only if the runout is favorable.

Board: 5♠ J♠ Q♦ A♠ T♥ — I hold J♦9♣: top pair with a weak kicker on a very wet, high-card board. Flash opened wide from the button, c‑bet tiny on the flop and continued with a turn bet — his line is consistent with a wide range that contains Ax, Qx, better Jx, two‑spade combos (already a flush on the turn), and broadway combinations that the river K would complete into a straight. Many of those hands beat my one pair; betting OOP here simply bloats the pot and will be called by many hands that are ahead (Ax, QJ/AJ, Kx straights, spade flushes) while folding out mostly bluffs. Pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot manageable, preserves our stack, and lets us call a small river stab from a missed bluffer but fold to heavy river aggression. I’ll check and go to showdown; if Flash bets a small amount I’ll call for pot odds and to catch bluffs, but I’ll fold to a large river shove because too many hands that beat me are in his line.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (7) on a very coordinated board. While I have been the aggressor, the Ten on the river is a dangerous card that completes potential straights for any King (Kx). Minni has called both the flop and turn bets, indicating she likely has a piece of this board, such as a Queen, a Jack, or a drawing hand. By checking back the river, I can realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise from a better hand (like a straight, two pair, or a flush). I don't expect many worse hands to call a third bet here, so checking is the most prudent play.

Preflop on the button with J2o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against ranges, and no suited or connected value. Pot is small (150), to call 100 offers poor implied odds even in position. Opponents are aggressive raisers from history (Minni often opens, Flash defends/raises), so facing a raise post-call is likely. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament setting where survival and selective aggression matter over 150 hands. Tight play here maximizes EV long-term.

I’m in the small blind with K♠J♣ heads-up vs the big blind. This is a strong heads-up holding and I’ll have positional advantage after the flop. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; a raise to 200 (2×BB) is a standard, economical sizing that accomplishes isolation, creates fold equity, and lets me take the initiative while keeping the pot manageable with ~99BB effective. If called I’ll continue to C‑bet favorable runouts; if Flash 3‑bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a big 3‑bet, but continue vs smaller aggression). Raise to 200 to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position for value.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Seven offsuit. The Small Blind, Minni, has made a min-raise to 200. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win 300) and the fact that I will have position throughout the rest of the hand, Ace-Seven is a strong enough hand to continue with. Calling is the most prudent play to keep the pot controlled and utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

I raised preflop and was called wide from the BB, so Flash’s calling range is very broad and contains a lot of Ax, Qx and pocket pairs. The flop Q♠ Q♦ A♠ completely misses K♠J♣ — I have no pair and only 6 clean outs (3 Ks, 3 Js), many of which still leave me behind to A/Q holdings. A continuation bet here has very poor fold equity (villain will continue a lot with A/Q/pairs) and just bloats the pot out of position when I’m likely behind. I’ll check to control the pot and see what he does. Plan: fold to a sizable bet (we’re cleanly behind to most value ranges), consider calling a very small c-bet for pot control and to see a turn, and if checked through re-evaluate on the turn for possible delayed bluff or free-card value.

I have top pair with an Ace on a paired board of Queens. Minni checked, which could indicate a weak Ace, a pocket pair, or a trap with a Queen. Given the board texture and my mediocre kicker (7), checking back is the most prudent play for pot control. This allows me to see a turn for free and keeps the pot manageable while I am in position.

Board is Q♠ Q♦ A♠ 3♣ and I hold K♠ Jc — I have no pair and only dirty outs (Ks and Js) that often still lose to A/Q holdings. I checked the flop and Flash checked back; his passive line is consistent with a range heavy in Qx/Ax and pocket pairs that will call a delayed c‑bet. Betting the turn here would have very low fold equity and just bloats the pot out of position when I’m likely behind. Pot control is the priority: I’ll check back, see the river, and only bluff or value‑bet when the runout gives a much clearer story (a clean K/J or a river that makes a small, credible river bluff), otherwise fold to significant river aggression.

I have top pair (Aces) with a 7 kicker on a board featuring a pair of Queens. Minni has checked twice now (flop and turn). While it is possible she is trapping with a Queen, it is more likely she has a weak Ace, a mid-pair, or is giving up. However, betting here doesn't achieve much value, as most hands that call me likely have me out-kicked (A8-AK) or have trip Queens. By checking back, I maintain pot control and am happy to take my showdown value to the river.

Board is Q♠ Q♦ A♠ 3♣ A♦ and I hold K♠J♣ — I have no pair and no realistic showdown value. Any Q or A in Flash’s calling range (and several pocket pairs like 3s) now make a full house; many more hands (Ax, Qx, pocket pairs) beat me. Flash checked flop, turn and river — a passive line that can be either air or a slow‑played monster, so my fold equity is very poor. Betting here would risk a large portion of my stack to fold out only the very weakest hands and get called by most hands that beat me. Pot control and chip preservation are correct: check and concede the pot if he shows down a made hand, fold to aggression.

On this board of Qs-Qd-As-3c-Ad, my hand Ac-7d makes a Full House: Aces full of Queens (A-A-A-Q-Q). In Texas Hold'em, a Full House consists of exactly five cards, and since the board and my hand provide three Aces and two Queens, my kicker (the 7) is irrelevant. Any other hand with an Ace (like AK or AJ) will result in the same five-card hand, leading to a split pot. The only hand that beats me is Four of a Kind Queens (Qh-Qc), which is extremely unlikely given the betting pattern. Since Minni has checked the flop, turn, and river, I will place a value bet. She may call with a Queen (which I beat), a pocket pair, or her own Ace (resulting in a split).

I have K♠J♣ on Q♠ Q♦ A♠ 3♣ A♦. I have no pair and very little showdown value: any A or Q in Flash’s range is now trips/full house and beats me, and hands like pocket 33 (or AA/QQ) also crush me. Flash’s line (checking flop and turn, then betting a small 300 into a 700 pot on the river) is exactly the kind of thin value or blocking bet I expect from hands that contain an A or Q — and he has a wide preflop calling range that includes lots of those combos. Calling 300 into 700 requires ~30% equity to break even. My realistic equity versus a river-betting range polarized toward A/Q/trips/full houses is well below that (likely <20%, in practice much lower), so a call is -EV. This is early enough in the tournament and my stack is healthy, so preserving chips is the correct long-term play. I’ll fold to the river bet.

Button with 8♠7♦ is a playable, speculative hand — especially in position against the blinds. Pot is 150 and to-call 100; opening to 300 (≈3x BB) is the standard line: it attempts to steal the blinds, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me play post-flop with initiative and position. If Flash (SB) calls I'll proceed to evaluate the flop with position; if Elon (BB) 3-bets big I will generally fold this hand rather than invest a large portion of my deep stack. Limping is too passive here and invites aggression, so I open-raise to apply pressure.

In a 3-handed game, hand values increase significantly. Minni is raising from the button, and T9o is a reasonable hand to defend from the small blind. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds (calling 250 to win 450) and the widened ranges of 3-max play make this a standard call to see a flop.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 from Minni (loose raiser from history) and a small blind call from Flash (deep stack, sometimes defends wide). Pot is 700, calling 200 more for 3.5:1 odds. J5s is suited with straight and flush potential, plays well multiway postflop. Folding is too tight in position with these odds; raising is overplaying a speculative hand OOP. Standard defense: call and see flop.

On a board of 2s-3s-Jc, I have completely missed with my 9h-Ts. I am out of position against two players, one of whom was the pre-flop aggressor. There is no reason to lead out here with air. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as my hand has very little equity to continue.

Flopped top pair with a weak 5 kicker in a multiway pot on a draw-heavy board (spade flush draw, wheel straight draws like 45, A4). Flash checked from SB, which could be weakness or trap, but Minni on button is aggressive and likely to bet wide. Betting risks getting raised by better hands (KQ, AQ, sets) or draws, while checking keeps the pot small and allows us to realize equity OOP with position over Minni. Weak kicker vulnerable; prefer to check and evaluate turn action.

I raised preflop and have position; the flop (2♠ 3♠ J♣) misses my 8♠7♦ — I have no pair, no real straight draw, and only a very weak runner-runner spade possibility. Both opponents checked, showing relative weakness. With a marginal hand I want to try to take the pot now without bloating it if called. I'll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) to fold out unpaired overcards and small pockets that checked, retain initiative, and keep pot control if I'm called. Plan: fold to a significant raise (I have too little equity to continue vs strong action); if I'm called by one player I'll use position to re-evaluate on the turn and only barrel with improvement or a clear fold equity line.

I have completely missed the flop with Ten-high and no significant draws. Minni's continuation bet in a multi-way pot, even if small, is likely to be ahead of my current holding. Calling with zero equity and a player left to act behind me is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Facing a button continuation bet of 300 into 1200 after both blinds checked on a highly coordinated flop (2s3sJc: spade flush draw, wheel/OESD potential). Hold top pair weak kicker (Jh5h) with backdoor flush draw (hearts). Minni is aggressive preflop raiser from history (frequent opens, cbets flops), likely betting wide including Ax suited, broadway draws, pocket pairs, and air. Flash's fold from SB reduces multiway dynamic. Pot odds 4:1 direct, plus implied odds vs deep button stack (9170). Folding top pair OOP too tight; raising risks isolation vs better (JQ+, sets, 2p) or induces folds from worse. Call keeps pot small, realizes equity vs bluffs/draws, controls pot OOP, re-eval turn (non-spade low likely improves us relative). Standard play for speculative top pair on drawy board vs aggro villain.

Heads-up on turn, first to act OOP with top pair weak kicker (Jh5h) on a very draw-heavy board (2s3sJcKc: open spade FD, gutshots like A4, broadway SDs like QT, possible clubs FD now). Pot 1500, villain Minni (button aggressor from history: frequent opens/cbets, shown down value but bluffs draws too). Betting risks: thin value gets called/raised by better TP/TK (AJ/KJ), two-pair, sets, str8 draws that hit; fold equity low vs aggro range. Preflop raiser flop cbettor likely contins with wide range (Ax suited, broadway, pairs, draws). Checking controls pot, induces bluffs from missed draws, realizes equity vs worse (weaker Ax/pairs/draws), allows villain reps K or sets. Stack depths allow implied odds if check-raise value or check-call. Folding impossible with TPTK; betting overplays vulnerable hand OOP. Standard: check to pot control and exploit villain aggression.

I c-bet the flop to take down the pot, but Elon called — that usually shows some real showdown value (top pair Jx, a small pair, a float or a draw). The K♣ on the turn is a big overcard that improves his range more than mine and it also kills my tiny backdoor equity (I needed both turn+river spades to make a flush). I have no pair and no meaningful draw, and Elon has shown a tendency to call down. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff with low fold equity and a high chance of getting called or raised. I’ll check to control the pot, avoid getting blown off a big portion of my stack, and re-evaluate the river — if a safe bluffing card (7/8 or a spade) comes I can consider a river bluff; otherwise I’ll check back or fold to significant aggression.

River pairs the 3 (3s and 3h), creating possible trips for villain's range (3x, 23, 32 suited connectors she might raise with pre). Board remains draw-heavy but many draws missed (no spade flush, clubs draw incomplete, broadway like QT open-ended on flop now has gutshot or missed). Hold top pair weak kicker (Jh5h), which beats weaker Ax/Jx (worse kicker), overpairs that missed K (88-QQ unlikely OOP raise pre), and bluffs/air from her flop cbet range (suited connectors, broadway draws). Beats some but loses to KJ/AJ/QJ (better TPTK), Kx top pair, sets (JJ/22/33 rare), two-pair combos (K3/J3). Villain checked turn behind on Kc (high card aiding broadway), suggesting not super strong (would bet Kx or str8s for value) but capping range. OOP first to act after pot control checks; betting thin value risks getting called/raised only by better (low fold equity from aggro villain history) and stacks deep but vulnerable kicker reduces implied. Checking realizes showdown value vs her medium/wide checking range (missed draws, weaker pairs), induces bluffs if she bets, controls pot, and avoids difficult spots. Standard play: check to pot control and showdown TPTK OOP vs button aggro on scary paired board.

Check. I never improved with 8♠7♦ — my flop c‑bet was a pure continuation and Elon’s call there shows real showdown value (Jx, a small pair) or a draw/float. The K on the turn was a big overcard that hurt my range and killed my tiny backdoor outs; the river 3 pairs the board and is a poor bluff card (it increases the chance he now has a made hand or trips). Elon’s line (call flop, check turn) looks passive and calling‑station‑like, so my fold equity on a river bluff is low and a bet will mostly get called by better hands. I’ll check to control the pot and either see a showdown for free or fold to significant aggression — no reason to bloat the pot with air.

Holding King of Hearts and Jack of Hearts on the button is a very strong hand in three-handed play. I have the chip lead and should open with a standard raise to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is standard for this spot.

J4o is a trash hand with no playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in previous hands. Calling 250 into a 450 pot offers poor implied odds and sets up difficult post-flop decisions OOP. Raising would be a bluff with a hand unlikely to hold up. Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Button opened to 300 and I’m in the BB with A♣3♦. This is a marginal but defendable hand versus a wide BTN raising range. Calling the 200 (300 total committed) gives ~30.8% pot odds and keeps the pot manageable out of position. A min-3bet to 500 would be thin — Flash has a big stack and will call a lot, putting me OOP with a hand that doesn’t play well multi-street. Folding is too tight given the price and blocker value of the ace. Plan after calling: look to extract value when I pair the Ace, otherwise play cautiously (check/fold to large aggression on dry boards, call small bets with backdoor equity and re-evaluate on the turn).

I have top pair (A♣) but a very weak 3 kicker on a two-heart board (A♥ K♠ 9♥). Flash opened from the button and will have a wide C-bet/call range including worse A‑x, many Kx/9x hands and heart draws. Betting for value and protection is optimal: a ~half‑pot sized bet charges heart draws, gets value from worse Aces/Ks/9s and keeps weaker hands and draws in. I’ll size down to avoid bloating the pot out of position versus a big stack — if I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy aggression with this weak kicker).

On a flop of Ah-Ks-9h, I have middle pair (Kings) with a strong heart flush draw (Jh-Kh). Minni's lead of 350 into a 650 pot (donk bet) often indicates a marginal hand like a weak Ace, a King, or a draw. Since I have position and a hand with massive equity (14 outs if behind an Ace), calling is the most strategic play. Raising might force out her bluffs or marginal hands that I currently beat or have high equity against, and it bloats the pot unnecessarily against her potential monsters like AK or 99. By calling, I maintain control of the pot and can decide how to proceed on the turn based on her action and the card that falls.

I have top pair (A♣) with a terrible 3 kicker on Ah Ks 9h 8d. I led flop (350) and was called, so Flash’s range is wide (worse Aces, Kx, 9x, heart combos/draws, some pairs). The turn 8♦ is a relatively blank for my opponent’s calling range but there are still heart draw combinations to charge. I want to extract value from worse Aces/Ks/9s and charge draws while keeping the pot manageable out of position against a much bigger stack. A ~half‑pot continuation bet accomplishes that and keeps me flexible — I can fold to significant raise(s) with this weak kicker but will get value from a lot of his calling hands. Bet half pot (≈675).

On the turn, Minni continues her aggression with a bet of 675 after donking the flop. I have second pair (Kings) and the nut flush draw (Jack-high flush draw with the Ace of hearts on the board). This gives me significant equity; even if I am behind an Ace, I have approximately 12 clean outs (9 hearts, 2 Kings, and potentially 3 Jacks, though the Jacks are less certain). The pot odds are roughly 3:1, and calling allows me to realize my equity in position without inflating the pot unnecessarily. Raising might force out her bluffs or semi-bluffs that I currently beat, while calling keeps her range wide and allows me to see the river cheaply.

I have top pair (A♣) but a very poor 3 kicker on Ah Ks 9h 8d 7d. I led both streets and was called, so Flash’s continuing range is wide and contains hands that both beat me (AK, A9, A8, A7, some two‑pairs) and hands I beat (Kx, 9x, weaker single pairs), plus a few rare straight combos. The 7♦ is a relatively dangerous, low connectivity card that can complete some straights; it doesn’t improve my kicker. Betting for thin value here is marginal — I’ll get called by better Aces and two‑pairs and possibly by worse hands, and a raise from a big stack would put me in a tough spot with a weak kicker. With pot 2700 and my stack ~7845, the low‑variance line is to check and go to showdown. If Flash now leads small I’ll consider a call for price; I’ll fold to large river aggression.

On this board of Ah-Ks-9h-8d-7d, I have second pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker. Minni's line of leading out on both the flop and turn and then checking the river suggests she either has a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or a King) that is looking to get to showdown cheaply, or she was on a draw that missed (like hearts or a straight draw). By checking back, I realize my showdown value. If she has a missed draw, I win the pot without further risk. If she has a weak Ace or a better King, I am unlikely to fold her out with a bet, and I certainly won't get called by many worse hands. In this tournament position as the chip leader, taking the free showdown is the most prudent and strategic play.

Preflop on the button with 2s5d, an offsuit, low, gapped trash hand. No speculative value, no suited connectors, no high card strength. Facing a to-call of 100 into a 150 pot with deep stacks (73BB effective). Opponents like Minni and Flash are aggressive raisers historically, so defending wide risks domination postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament with 100 hands left.

Button folded, so I'm heads-up to the big blind with K♥T♠. This is a playable hand from the SB and I want to take the initiative rather than limp and play passively OOP. Raise to the minimum allowed (200 total, ~2x BB) to isolate Flash, build a pot when I have decent equity, and keep SPR manageable with ~100bb stacks. If Flash calls I’ll proceed with standard c-bets on favorable flops; if he 3-bets big I will fold this hand unless the sizing or read justifies a call.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 4h. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). While I have position post-flop and the pot odds are 3:1 to call, J4o is a very weak hand. Even against a wide Small Blind opening range, J4o frequently underperforms and can lead to difficult spots post-flop. Given my significant chip lead, I prefer to fold these marginal bottom-tier hands and wait for better opportunities to utilize my stack advantage.

I have KK on the button — a premium hand in a 3-handed game. With both blinds posted (SB Flash is deep and aggressive; BB Elon is mid-deep) I want to open for value and to isolate heads-up where my position and hand are strongest. A 4x BB open (raise to 400 total) extracts value from worse hands (AK, AQ, mid pairs), discourages multiway calls from speculative hands, and still leaves room to maneuver post‑flop. Plan: open-raise to 400 now; if I face a light 3-bet from the deep SB I’ll consider a strong 4-bet/isolation (or shove depending on sizing); versus a 3-bet from Elon (shorter) I can either call to play in position or 4-bet for value depending on the size.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine of hearts, which is a very strong speculative hand. The Button (Minni) has raised to 400. While I am out of position, the hand has excellent playability and post-flop potential. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to comfortably see a flop. Calling is better than raising here to keep the pot controlled while out of position against a stack that has been active. I expect the Big Blind (Elon) to call frequently as well given the odds, but Th9h plays well in multi-way pots.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 400 from Minni (who is aggressive and raises frequently from history) and a small blind call from Flash, with Kc5c suited. Getting excellent pot odds: calling 300 more into a 900 pot (pot odds ~3:1, requiring ~20% equity). K5s has decent playability multiway, suited for flush potential, king blocker, and live cards. Preflop history shows Minni raises wide from late position. Calling keeps the pot multiway cheaply, positionally disadvantaged but exploitable postflop check-down tendencies observed. Raising lacks strong equity/fold equity; folding too tight given odds. Call to realize equity and maneuver postflop.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten and a Nine kicker on a relatively low board of T-4-3. While my hand is strong, I am out of position in a three-way pot. The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how the action develops. Betting out (donking) would likely fold out worse hands and only get called by better or strong draws. By checking, I can maintain pot control and evaluate based on the size of any subsequent bets. If someone bets, I plan to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

Multiway pot on a draw-heavy flop (3c Ts 4s) with paired low cards and two flush draws possible (spades dominant). Holding Kc5c gives the nut club flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity standalone) plus king overcard and gutshot elements for added equity (~40%+ multiway vs. wide ranges). Preflop: Minni's button raise is wide (history shows frequent aggression), Flash's SB call speculative. I'm OOP first to act after Flash check. Checking risks Minni betting and forcing tough spot or check-raise, but betting semi-bluffs for fold equity (exploits Minni's occasional folds to aggression), gets value from weaker draws/pairs (e.g., Ax suited, pocket pairs below T), builds pot with my equity, and denies equity to opponents' draws. Sizing 2/3 pot (800 into 1200) balances value/protection. Check-down history noted, but equity too strong to give free cards. Stack depths allow (6905 effective vs. pot), SPR low post-bet favors our draw.

I opened to 400 preflop with KK and on this flop (3c Ts 4s) my kings are an overpair — strong but vulnerable to spade draws. Elon (BB, ~6105) leads out 800 into a 2000 pot (a fairly small c-bet). I want to both extract value from worse made hands (T, small pairs) and charge spade draws, while using position to isolate the bettor. A meaningful raise is correct; sizing up instead of a minimum raise helps deny equity to draws and forces weaker hands to fold. I’ll raise to 2400 total — big enough to charge draws and get value but not so huge that a single big 4‑bet automatically commits me without re-evaluating. If Elon calls (or Flash cold-calls) I’m set to continue in position; if I face a large polarized 4‑bet/shove I’ll re-evaluate but will usually call against a short­-stack shove given KK’s strength.

On a board of Tc-4s-3c, I have top pair with a Nine kicker. After I checked, Elon bet 800 into 1200, and Minni raised to 2400. This is a very strong sequence of action in a three-way pot. Elon's bet into two players suggests a decent hand, but Minni's raise over the top is particularly concerning. Minni could have sets (33, 44), overpairs, or a very strong draw like a flush draw combined with an overcard or straight potential. While I have top pair, the action indicates I am likely beat or facing a massive draw that has significant equity against me. My Nine kicker is also vulnerable to hands like AT or KT. Given the risk of being trapped between two aggressive players and the high probability that my hand is second-best, the most prudent tournament play is to fold and preserve my large chip lead.

Facing Minni's flop raise to 2400 after my 800 bet into a multiway pot that reduced to heads-up. Holding the nut club flush draw (9 outs) plus king overcard on a 3c-Ts-4s board heavy with draws (spade flush draw, gutshots, wheel potential). Pot odds excellent: calling 1600 into 4400 pot (~27% equity required); my equity ~35-45% vs. Minni's button raising range (value like Tx, sets, two-pair; semi-bluffs like spade draws J9s/85s/Axs, straight draws). History shows Minni aggressive preflop wide but mixes check-downs and calls light (e.g., Hand 11,18); likely some bluffs here. SPR post-call ~0.75 (4505 behind into ~6000 pot), committed anyway—shove turn/river with made flush or improved draws, fold weak turns. Raising risks isolating better hands or calling stations without enough fold equity; folding forfeits massive equity/odds. Call realizes equity, denies her thin value, exploits position OOP by controlling pot size while keeping leverage low.

Turn card 5d pairs my 5c for top pair (fives) with king overcard kicker and retains the nut club flush draw (9 outs). Board (3c Ts 4s 5d) is draw-heavy with completed straights (A2, 62) possible, spade flush draws, sets/Tx/two-pair in Minni's button raising/3-bet range. Heads-up OOP first to act in 6000 pot with 4505 behind (SPR ~0.75), fully committed spot. Equity strong (~50-60%+ vs. her polarized range of value like Tx/sets/straights and bluffs like Js9s/65s/Axs); history shows Minni aggressive preflop but folds to pressure sometimes (e.g., Hand 4,18) and calls light (Hand 11). Shoving extracts max value from worse pairs/draws (44, Tx, weaker 5x, unfinished spades), protects vs. her draws, denies equity, and exploits low SPR—no room for her to realize cheaply. Checking risks free river card to her draws or check-back losing value; betting smaller risks raise/fold equity loss. All-in balances thin value and semi-bluff elements while maximizing EV in tournament stack preservation/growth context.

Elon has shoved all‑in for 4,505 on the turn after calling my flop raise. I hold KK — a clean overpair to the 3c‑Ts‑4s‑5d board with no spade. Fold risks giving up a huge +EV spot; calling requires 4,505 and the current pot is 10,505, so break‑even equity = 4,505 / (10,505+4,505) ≈ 30.0%. Villain’s line (small flop c‑bet, call of my raise, then turn shove) is polarized: it contains some value (sets: TT/33/44 and now 55 on the turn, plus rare straights like A2) but also many one‑pair hands and spade semi‑bluffs that KK crushes. Combos that legitimately beat me are limited, so my equity vs his shove range is well above the ~30% threshold. Calling is +EV — I win a big pot most of the time and, if I lose, I’m left with ~3,340 chips but I’m maximizing expected chips. I call.

I am on the button with Ace-four offsuit in a heads-up situation. This is a standard opening hand. Minni has been quite active and just won a large pot, but A4o is well ahead of a random range and merits a raise to take the initiative and build the pot. I will raise to 300, which has been the standard opening size at this table.

Heads-up vs button open to 300. I’m BB with 3♣2♣ — a weak but suited, low connector with backdoor flush/straight potential. Stacks are deep (~18–21k) and the additional amount to call is small (I add 200 more, 300 total committed), giving roughly 33% pot odds to see a flop. I won’t 3-bet OOP with this junk — no fold equity and poor blockers — so the best line is to defend and see a flop where I can realize equity or fold to heavy aggression. Plan: call preflop, play cautiously postflop (fold to strong action if I miss, continue if I pick up improvements).

I called preflop with 3c2c to see a flop, planning to play cautiously. The flop is 7h 9s 9c — the board is paired and I have no pair, only a very weak backdoor club/straight possibility. Flash’s BTN raise is a wide HU range and includes many hands that currently beat me (9x, 7x, overcards, pairs); I have almost no fold equity OOP and lack useful blockers. With deep stacks I don’t want to bluff-raise or barrel without improvement. Best line is to check and evaluate: fold to strong bets, call small bets for pot odds, and only continue if I pair or pick up a club. Therefore I check.

On a paired board of 7h-9s-9c, I hold Ace-high. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on most boards, though a paired board like this can be tricky. I will place a standard continuation bet of 200 (approximately 1/3 of the pot) to test Minni's hand. This sizing is small enough to fold if she raises, but large enough to fold out her complete air or random overcards that have equity against my Ace-high.

Flop 7h 9s 9c. I hold 3c2c — no pair, only backdoor club and small pair outs. Flash (BTN) makes a very small bet (200 into a 800 pot), giving roughly 5:1 pot odds. With deep stacks and such a cheap price it's correct to defend and realize equity rather than fold or bluff‑raise OOP with air (I have poor blockers and little fold equity). I’ll call the 200 to see the turn and re‑evaluate: fold to large turn pressure unless I pair a 2/3, pick up a club, or get a clear read to continue.

The turn 3d pairs my 3c and gives me two pair (9s and 3s) — a meaningful improvement heads‑up. Flash’s line (open pre, small c‑bet on the paired 9 flop, then a check on the turn) is consistent with a wide, often marginal range: bluffs/missed overcards, medium pairs, and some 9x/7x hands that currently beat me. Against a wide checking range I want to charge worse hands and deny free cards rather than gift the river. I also don’t want to bloat the pot too much with a vulnerable two‑pair (there are still hands that beat me), so a modest value bet is best. I’ll bet ~½ pot (500 into 1000) to extract from weaker pairs and poor overcards, charge any backdoor attempts, and keep the pot controllable — fold to a large check‑raise (which likely indicates a 9/7/set), but proceed carefully if called and re‑evaluate on the river.

Minni's lead on the turn after check-calling the flop on a paired board (7-9-9) is a strong signal that she has a piece of the board, likely a 9, a 7, or a pocket pair. The 3d on the turn is a blank that doesn't improve my Ace-high or provide any additional equity like a straight or flush draw. Given the tournament context and her recent momentum, I should not contest this pot with a marginal Ace-high against a show of strength. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a difficult decision on the river.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) — I should be opening a very wide range. K♦2♠ is marginal but raising to take the initiative and apply pressure is standard. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) rather than min-raising to 200 to buy more fold equity and discourage light defenses/3-bets while keeping the pot manageable with a weak holding. If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable, dry or K/2-containing boards and play cautiously on heavy action. If he 3-bets to a substantial size I’ll fold K2o; against a very small 3-bet I may reassess by size and tendencies. Deep stacks (≈188bb) mean I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand without initiative.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a premier starting hand, especially in a heads-up scenario. My opponent, Minni, has opened for 3x the big blind. Given the strength of my hand, I want to build a pot for value and take initiative. A standard 3-bet size from the big blind against a button/small blind open is typically between 3x and 4x the original raise. I will raise to 1,000 to exert pressure and maximize value.

I opened wide from the button with K♦2♠ but Flash’s 3‑bet to 1,000 (pot 1,300; I must call 700) is a substantial defender size that polarizes toward real strength heads‑up. K2o is heavily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (AK, Kx, broadways and pairs) and has poor post‑flop playability despite being in position. A light 4‑bet bluff here would be far too thin and would bloat the pot unnecessarily with deep stacks. Given the sizing, opponent tendencies and my earlier plan to fold marginal Kx to a substantial 3‑bet, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have QTs on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand that performs well post-flop. I will raise to the standard 3x (300 total) to take the initiative and build a pot with my positional advantage.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have 8♣2♥ — a very weak, non‑connecting off‑suit hand with almost no postflop playability out of position. Pot is ~400 and I can call 200 more (total commitment 300) for a cheap look. With deep stacks I want to preserve chips and only 3‑bet bluff with hands that have better equity or blockers (Axs, suited broadways, suited connectors). Calling keeps the pot small, gives me a cheap chance to hit an 8/2 or backdoor draw, and allows me to fold to a large c‑bet on most flops. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to sizeable continuation bets on dry boards, continue only with a pair, good draw, or favorable runout/check‑back lines when appropriate.

Board K♥ J♣ 6♥ and I hold 8♣2♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only a runner‑runner heart possibility). I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser/button who will c‑bet this texture frequently. Pot is ~600 and stacks are deep, so preserving chips and pot control is correct. I’ll check and plan to fold to any standard continuation bet, only continuing if I pair or pick up a legitimate draw or if the bet is tiny and gives correct pot odds.

I have a double-gutshot straight draw (any Ace or Nine gives me a straight) on a board that favors my pre-flop raising range. Since Minni checked, I will lead out with a continuation bet to capitalize on my range advantage and fold out her air, while building a pot for my high-equity draw.

Facing a 350 bet into a 950 pot on K♥ J♣ 6♥ with 8♣2♥ out of position. I have no pair and no real draw (only a runner‑runner heart), so my equity is negligible. Calling 350 to win ~1300 requires ~27% equity — far more than this hand has versus the button’s typical c‑bet range (Kx/Jx/Tx, some Ax, and occasional bluffs). A bluff‑raise here would be speculative with almost no blockers and very risky deep‑stacked heads‑up. Preserve chips and fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Kc7h — in heads-up this is a playable hand and I want the initiative. Limping here gives the big blind a free look and concedes initiative; a standard open is better. I’ll make the minimum open to 2bb (200) to apply pressure, preserve fold equity, and keep the pot manageable if called. If Flash 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I play postflop with position and K-high potential.

I am holding a small pocket pair (22) in the Big Blind. Minni has min-raised to 200. With excellent pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 300) and deep effective stacks (about 180 BBs), calling to set-mine is a standard and profitable play. I will call to see a flop and look for a Deuce.

I have flopped bottom set, which is a monster on this board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to allow Minni to continuation bet. This keeps her bluffs in and allows me to extract value from her marginal hands or overpairs later. There is no need to raise yet and potentially scare her off.

I opened preflop to take the initiative and I still have position. The flop (T♠‑6♦‑2♠) is dry and favors a wide c‑bet — many of BB’s hands (unpaired overcards, small pairs, missed draws) will fold. My K♣7♥ has no pair and no spade, so I’m not looking to bloat the pot but I want to extract fold equity and deny free cards to spade draws. I’ll make a standard half‑pot continuation bet (200). If Flash folds I take it down; if he calls I can continue selectively on the turn with equity or favorable cards; if he check‑raises big I’ll fold most of my range (I have little showdown value).

I have flopped bottom set, a very strong hand on this relatively dry board. Minni has made a standard continuation bet. At this point, I want to keep her range as wide as possible. If I raise now, I likely fold out her air and bluffs, whereas calling allows her to continue barreling on the turn with a wider variety of hands. There are some draws (spades), but my hand is robust enough to navigate most turn cards. I will call and look to extract value on later streets.

I have bottom set on a board that is starting to become coordinated (flush draws and straight draws are present). I checked the flop to let the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) continue her story, and she did bet. On this turn, I will check again. This 'slow play' approach aims to induce another bet from her bluffs, overpairs, or top-pair hands. If she bets this turn, I plan to put in a check-raise to build the pot and protect my hand against the various draws that have materialized.

Heads‑up on the button with K♣7♥; flop c‑bet succeeded in getting a call and I retained position. Turn (9♦) checked to me. I have no pair, no spade, and very little showdown value — my hand didn’t improve and the 9♦ doesn’t meaningfully change ranges. Flash has shown a wide, calling posture postflop, so my fold equity barreling the turn is low and a bluff is unlikely to work often. Pot is small relative to our stacks (800 in pot vs ~18–21k stacks), so there’s no reason to bloat the pot with a weak bluff. I’ll control the pot and see the river — check and re‑evaluate on the river where I might bluff missed boards or value if I hit a King.

I have a set of deuces, which was likely the best hand on the flop. The turn went check-check, which often indicates that Minni has a marginal showdown hand or is giving up. The river 4s completes a potential spade flush, but given the turn check, it's less likely she was semi-bluffing a flush draw. I will lead out with a value bet of 500 into the 800 pot, aiming to get called by hands like top pair (Tens) or middle pair (Nines) that she might check back if I were to check.

I have K♣7♥ and missed the entire board. I was the pre‑flop/ flop aggressor, opponent called my c‑bet and then both of us checked the turn — that line narrows him to lots of pairs, tens, and medium strength hands (and some missed draws). The river 500 into ~800 is a small, probe/value bet that I beat only when he’s bluffing; my K‑7 has almost no showdown equity vs his calling range. Pot odds for a call (500 to win 1300) require ~38% equity, which K7 does not have, and a raise to 1000 as a bluff is too risky given his line. Fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K2o. In a heads-up situation, K-high hands have significant equity and should generally be played for a raise or at least a call. However, since I am in the Small Blind and the pot is small, I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 total to pressure the Big Blind and take control of the hand. This has been a frequent sizing throughout the game.

Heads-up in the big blind with JsTs (spades) — a very playable, well-connected suited hand against a likely wide button open to 300. Stacks are deep (~178/219 BB), so postflop playability and fold equity matter. Calling keeps the pot small and allows realization, but the minimum re-raise to 500 is too weak and will be called widely. I want to apply pressure, isolate the button and get value from worse broadways/suited connectors while denying equity to hands that dominate ours. A standard 3-bet sizing to 900 gives meaningful fold equity, sets up a pot I can play for stacks later, and keeps options postflop. If Flash 4-bets large I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarized shove from a tight opponent; call vs a looser sizing), but right now I raise to 900 to take the initiative and exploit a wide opening range.

I opened to 300 with K2o from the button, and Minni has 3-bet to 900. While King-high has some equity in heads-up play and I would have position post-flop, K2o is near the bottom of my opening range and does not play well against a 3-bet from an opponent who has shown significant aggression and stickiness in previous hands. Folding here preserves my chip lead and allows me to wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also SB) with 9♦6♥: this is a marginal but playable hand in position. I want to seize initiative, build some fold equity and avoid being forced to play out of position, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call. A standard 3x-ish open (300 total) is consistent with the table and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks — I’ve already posted 50, so this is an additional 250. If Flash defends I can continue postflop with position and a wide range; if he folds I pick up the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. This is a standard heads-up opening size. While A3o isn't a premium hand, it has significant high-card value in a heads-up situation and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I could 3-bet here to take control, but given our deep stacks (217 BB and 178 BB) and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, calling to see a flop is a sound defensive play. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to evaluate the board, whereas raising might bloat the pot with a hand that is often dominated by better Aces or pairs if called.

In this heads-up situation, I am the Big Blind and act first post-flop. The board is Qd 7d 2c, which is relatively dry, though it contains a Queen. My Ace-high (Ac 3s) has some showdown value but does not warrant a lead into the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni, the aggressor, reacts to the board. It allows me to keep the pot small and potentially catch a bluff or see a free turn card if she checks back.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop and have position. Flop 2c Qd 7d gives me no pair with 9d6h — only a very weak backdoor diamond draw (I’d need both turn and river diamonds). Flash checked, so I can take the initiative and try to fold out many of his marginal holdings (Kx, Jx, small pairs, missed broadways) with a standard continuation bet. I’ll bet about half the pot (300) to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. If I face a strong raise I’ll fold (no showdown value); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card.

In this heads-up situation, I have Ace-high (Ac 3s) on a flop of Qd 7d 2c. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 into 600). Ace-high still has significant showdown value in a heads-up match, and I have one overcard (the Ace) to the top pair on the board, along with some backdoor straight possibilities. Folding now would be too exploitable. I will call the 300 bet to see a turn card and evaluate Minni's aggression on the next street, as my hand is currently ahead of her pure air bluffs and has equity against her mid-range hands.

On the turn 8h, my hand remains Ace-high. The board is still relatively dry, though the 8h does connect slightly with some middle-range cards. As I check-called the flop, my standard play here is to check again and see how Minni reacts. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river if she checks back, or evaluate her bet sizing if she chooses to double barrel. I'm not in a position to lead out with Ace-high in this spot.

I hold 9♦6♥ on 2♣ Q♦ 7♦ 8♥. My backdoor diamond draw is dead (turn is a heart), but the 8 gives me an open-ended straight draw (5s or 10s) — ~8 outs, ~17% to hit the river. Flash checked the flop after calling my c-bet and checks the turn too, which signals weakness / pot control. Betting here gives me fold equity to take down the 1,200 chip pot right now while also playing a semi-bluff with live equity if called. I’ll fire a roughly half‑pot bet (600). If he raises I’ll fold (no pair / poor showdown value); if he calls I can still hit my straight on the river and re-evaluate.

I am currently holding Ace-high (Ac 3s) on a board of Qd-7d-2c-8h. I check-called a half-pot bet on the flop, but Minni has now fired a second barrel of 600 into 1200 on the turn. In heads-up play, Ace-high can sometimes be a viable bluff-catcher, but A3o is particularly weak here as it lacks any redraws or a significant kicker. The 8h turn card doesn't improve my position, and I'm likely facing a third barrel on the river if I call now. Given our deep stacks and my current lead, there is no need to make a marginal hero call with no equity to improve except for three Aces. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button (small blind) with 9-6 offsuit. While heads-up play often allows for wider opening ranges, 9-6 offsuit is a marginal hand. Given the recent history where Minni has been defending aggressively or 3-betting, I will opt to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than bloat a pot with a weak hand out of position post-flop if called, or face a 3-bet.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♥3♦. Pot is 150, I need to add 50 to call. J3o is weak but in heads-up and in position it’s worth seeing a flop for a cheap price — pot odds are favorable (50 to win 150). Raising to 200 with this rag would bloat the pot and put me in difficult spots versus a defending BB. I’ll call 50 more, play the flop cautiously (check/fold to significant aggression unless I improve or have good draw/plan to bluff). Total committed after this action will be 100 chips (50 SB already posted + 50 call).

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5d. Minni limped from the Small Blind. Given the strength of my hand and my position, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play. Raising here doesn't accomplish much against Minni's likely wide limping range, and I'd rather play a small pot with a speculative hand like suited gappers.

On this King-high board, I have a very weak hand with only 8-high and no direct draws. Minni limped from the Small Blind, and the pot is small. I will check to see if I can improve on the turn for free or if Minni gives up the pot on a later street.

Heads-up on the button with Jh3d on Kd-6s-Th (pot 200). Opp checked, showing some weakness. My hand has one clean overcard (J) to the T and very little equity otherwise (no made pair, no realistic flush/straight draw). In position I should try to take this small pot now rather than give free cards. A small continuation bet (about half pot) maximizes fold equity versus missed hands (weak Ax, small pairs, backdoor floats) while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: bet small to win it now; if Flash folds I take the pot. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — continue only with improvement or clear equity/plan; fold to sizable raises.

I have 8-high with no pair and no significant draws on a King-high board (Kd-Th-6s). Minni limped from the Small Blind and has now bet half the pot on the flop. Given I am out of position and have no equity in this hand, there is no reason to call or bluff. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. I will put in a standard open-raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) to build a pot and take control of the hand with position.

Opponent (button) opened to 300. From the big blind with Td6s I’ll defend rather than 3‑bet or fold. Heads‑up the opener’s range is wide, T6o has reasonable equity and can make top pair or strong draws, and I’m getting roughly 2:1 (need ~33% equity) to call. Stacks are deep so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand — call to see a flop and then evaluate. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously if I miss (check/fold to heavy pressure), value-bet thin when I connect, and exploit tendencies later with selective 3‑bets if needed.

I called preflop to see a flop vs a wide button opener. The flop Qc‑7c‑2s completely misses my T6s: I have no pair and no meaningful draw (only six low-card outs to make a pair by river, and there aren’t enough spades on board to ever make a flush). Out of position with deep stacks I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than bluff off chips here. Check to control the pot and induce a c‑bet — call small continuation bets for pot odds, fold to large pressure, and re‑evaluate on the turn if I pick up a pair or better info.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Jack and the flop comes Queen-high and dry. Since I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and likely take down the pot or define Minni's hand strength.

Call 300. The small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) gives me about 20% pot odds to call (risk 300 to win ~1500). With Td6s I have six clean outs (three tens + three sixes) and roughly 24% to hit by the river, so a call is +EV vs a wide heads‑up c-bet. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a bluff/raise with marginal equity. Plan: call and reevaluate on the turn — if I pair up I can go for value; if I miss and face heavy pressure I’ll fold; if villain shows weakness later I can exploit with bluffing lines.

Check. The turn Jc doesn’t improve my T6o — I’ve got only 6 clean outs (3 tens + 3 sixes), ~13% to hit on the river. Pot is ~1,200 and stacks are deep; villain has shown a wide opener and a small flop c-bet which could be many one-pair hands, weak overcards or draws. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with very little fold equity and poor equity when called. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my equity, and keeps the option to call a small river stab or fold to heavy action. Plan: check river if villain checks; call small/medium river bets occasionally for pot odds; if I hit a pair on the river, value-bet appropriately; fold to large river pressure if I miss.

I have turned second pair with a strong kicker (Ace). The turn also completes a potential club flush. Given that Minni called on a relatively dry flop and then checked the turn, she could be holding a Queen, a flush draw that just got there, or a weaker pair. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. I have decent showdown value, but betting here might only get called by better hands (like a Queen or a flush) or force out hands that I already beat. I will check to see the river for free.

Check. I have nothing — T6o missed the board and the river Jd only pairs the board, it doesn’t help me. Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet, check on the turn) is consistent with a wide range: weak Qx, missed overcards or a pot‑control/value hand; he’s shown willingness to call/come to showdown in prior hands, so pure bluffing river has poor fold equity. I don’t block club flushes or J‑combos, and betting OOP here would bloat a tiny pot with almost no showdown value and little chance to fold out better hands. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back we get to a cheap showdown but preserve chips for better spots.

On the river, I have improved to three of a kind (Jacks) with the best possible kicker (Ace). The board is paired and a flush is possible, but Minni has checked all three streets. Given my check on the turn, my hand is somewhat disguised, and Minni may call a value bet with a Queen or even a weaker pair thinking I am bluffing after missing the flop. I will bet 800 into a 1200 pot to extract value from her marginal holdings.

Fold. The pot was 2000 and Flash bet 800 on the river — calling 800 to win 2800 requires ~28.6% equity. I hold T♦6♠ and missed the board completely (no pair, no draw) and cannot improve. Flash’s line (small 300 c‑bet on the flop, check the Jc turn, then a small 800 river bet after pairing the board) is much more consistent with thin value (Qc, Qx, Jx or other showdown hands) or a small probe — not a heavily polarized bluff range. I don’t block his value combos, my hand only beats rare pure air, and raising as a bluff has low fold equity and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win. Preserve stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button (I have position) with Js3o — a weak holding but fine to open in heads-up because position and fold equity are powerful. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop, but opening to a standard 2x BB (to 200 total) applies pressure, narrows Flash’s calling range, and gives me initiative postflop. If Flash 3-bets hard I will fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favourable flops or proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression when I miss. Raise to 200 to steal the blinds / play in position.

In this heads-up situation, I'm getting 3:1 odds to call with 6h5s from the big blind against a min-raise from Minni. While 65 offsuit isn't a premium hand, it has reasonable playability in a heads-up pot, and defending the big blind frequently is necessary to prevent being exploited by wide opening ranges. I will call the 100 to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

I have a small flush draw (6-high) on a monotone hearts board. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first on the flop. Checking is the most standard and safe play here to see how my opponent reacts. Betting into this board without a strong made hand or a high heart is unnecessary, and checking allows me to potentially see a free turn or evaluate based on Minni's betting size.

I check. I flopped a weak made hand (pair of treys with Js3d on 3h Qh 9h) on a very wet, monotone heart flop that heavily favors the caller’s preflop range (many Qx/9x and lots of two‑heart combos). Betting here as the preflop aggressor would often get called by better hands (top pair or a heart flush) and only fold out the very weakest holdings — I prefer to keep the pot under control with position. By checking I can call a small probe (≈≤½ pot) to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn, but I will fold to large bets/raises because my pair is vulnerable and has poor implied value. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free card and continue with caution on later streets.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of sixes and still hold a low flush draw (6h). The flop went check-check, which suggests Minni likely doesn't have a made flush or a very strong hand like top pair with a high heart kicker. By checking again, I maintain pot control and can potentially see a free river to improve my hand or get to a cheap showdown with my pair. If Minni bets, I have enough equity with my pair and flush draw to call a standard-sized bet.

I check. I only have one pair (3s) with Js3d on 3h Qh 9h 6c — a vulnerable, low‑pair holding on a very heart‑heavy flop. Flash checked both the flop and turn, so betting here would be thin value: it will get called by Qx/9x and by any made heart flushes and will only fold the very weakest hands. Against that range I prefer pot control in heads‑up — keep the pot at 400, realize equity with position, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and call small river probes to see a cheap showdown, but fold to large aggression on the river if I remain behind.

In this heads-up pot, the action has gone check-check on both the flop and the turn. This sequence suggests that neither player has a particularly strong hand. I have a pair of sixes, which is currently fourth pair on a board of 3h-Qh-9h-6c-Kc. While the King on the river is an overcard, Minni's passivity suggests she is unlikely to have a Queen or a made flush. My pair of sixes has decent showdown value against her range of missed draws and Ace-high hands. I will check to see if she checks behind for a cheap showdown. If she bets, I will have to evaluate if she is trying to represent the King or a flush that she slow-played, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity.

Check. I have only a small pair (Js3d making trips? actually one pair of 3s) on a very heart‑heavy flop (board: 3h Qh 9h 6c Kc). Flash’s calling range preflop contains lots of Qx/9x and heart combos that beat me, and both players checked flop and turn — then Flash checked the river as well — so a river bet here would be thin value (called by better hands and heart flushes) and a bluff has poor fold equity. Pot control is optimal heads‑up: take the cheap showdown with this vulnerable hand rather than bloating the pot. I’ll check to see showdown; if Flash had shown small aggression I might call small probes but would fold to large river raises.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value and take the initiative in the hand. A standard opening raise to 3 big blinds (300 total) is appropriate here.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button heads-up, 10♠4♠ is marginal but perfectly defendable from the big blind. Calling costs me 200 more (300 total committed, since I already posted the 100 BB) into a 400 pot — good pot odds to see a flop with backdoor spade/straight potential and the chance to hit top pair. I avoid a 3-bet with this hand because it’s too weak to take initiative out of position against a wide button opening range and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with speculative holdings. Plan: call preflop, play cautiously OOP on the flop (continue with top pair/two pair/strong draws, fold or check-control on dry misses, and only commit with clear equity or favorable action).

I missed this flop entirely with 10♠4♠ — no pair, no meaningful straight draw, only a backdoor spade possibility. Flash opened wide from the button preflop and will c-bet many Ax, Jx and broadway combos, so I expect a lot of hands that have at least showdown value. Out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with air or bluff-raise; the correct play is pot control. I’ll check and concede the initiative. Plan: fold to a clear value c-bet or large barrel; consider calling a very small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot) for the free card equity, otherwise give up and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up a spade or pair.

I have flopped top pair with top kicker (Ace-King) on an Ace-high board. This is a very strong hand. Minni has checked to me, which is standard for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. I should bet here to extract value from weaker Aces, Jacks, and draws (like heart draws or gutshots). A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard for maintaining board control and getting calls from worse hands.

I have T4s on Jc 7h Ah — I missed the flop but have backdoor spades and can make a Ten or a Four by the river. Flash opened wide from the button and the c-bet is small: 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 into a 1200 pot requires ~25% equity to break even. My chance to make at least a pair of my ranks by the river (hit a Ten or a Four) is roughly 24% and the runner‑runner spade flush is ~5%, so the price to see a turn is justified. Raising OOP with air here would bloat the pot and be exploitative; I’ll call to realize equity and fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve. If the turn brings a spade or a Ten I’ll re-evaluate and can proceed more aggressively.

I called preflop and the flop to realize equity with T♠4♠. The turn (9♥) didn’t improve me and now there are three hearts on board (A♥,7♥,9♥), which strongly helps any heart-containing combos in Flash’s wide opening/c-bet range. I’m out of position, have only ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~12–13% equity) and want to keep the pot under control. I’ll check and fold to meaningful aggression — only consider calling a very tiny probe (≈≤1/3 pot). If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river.

On the turn, the Nine of Hearts is a significant card as it completes any heart flush draws and some straight draws (like T8). While I still have Top Pair Top Kicker with my Ace-King, the board has become quite coordinated and dangerous. Minni's check could be a trap with a flush or a set, or it could be a weak hand. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise that would put me in a difficult spot, and get to see the river for free. This is a standard pot control move with a strong but vulnerable one-pair hand on a three-flush board.

I missed this river completely with T♠4♠ — no pair, no meaningful showdown equity. Flash opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river: that line is consistent with a wide range that contains lots of A‑x, J‑x and unpaired broadways that will often call a small river probe. To make a river bluff I'd need a low bet and still require ~20%+ fold equity (e.g. ~300 into 1200 is ~20% break‑even). Given the line and how cheap the pot is relative to our stacks, I don't expect sufficient fold equity to justify risking chips on a pure bluff. Pot control and seeing showdown is the least exploitable play here — check and show down. If Flash were to lead now I would fold to meaningful aggression.

On the river, the board pairs with the Seven of Clubs, making my final hand two pair (Aces and Sevens) with a King kicker. Minni has checked for the third consecutive street. While I likely have the best hand, the board is quite dangerous with three hearts (making a flush possible) and the paired sevens (making trips or a full house possible). My check on the turn was for pot control, and since the river hasn't significantly improved my position relative to a possible trap, checking back is the safest way to ensure I win the pot and avoid a difficult decision if Minni were to check-raise. This allows me to realize my showdown value and claim the 1,200 chip pot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♦2♥: this is a weak but playable hand because I’ll have position postflop and HU opening ranges should be wide. Limping/calling 50 is too passive and relinquishes initiative; a minimum open to 200 preserves stack depth, generates fold equity, and keeps postflop decision-making simple. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this offsuit; if he calls I’ll use my positional advantage to c‑bet dry boards and control the pot on wet boards. Raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket treys (3s 3d). Minni, on the button, has min-raised to 200. In heads-up play, a pocket pair is quite strong, though 33 is vulnerable. I have 3:1 pot odds to call (100 to win 300). While I could 3-bet to take the initiative, I am out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop cheaply and potentially set-mine or play a small pot if the board is favorable. Given my stack lead, there's no need to over-commit pre-flop with a small pair.

On this 2d-Tc-2c board, I have a small pocket pair (3s 3d). This is a paired board where I'm ahead of most of Minni's air and behind her 10x or any deuce. As the Big Blind, checking is standard here to see how Minni responds and keep the pot controlled. If she bets, I will likely call one small street to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or hit my remaining out, as my pair still has value against her high-card bluffs.

I hold J♦2♥ on a 2♦ T♣ 2♣ flop — trips (three 2s). Heads-up vs a BB caller, his check is often caution or a middling hand (overcards, Tx, small pairs). Trips is a very strong value hand but vulnerable to the small subset of ranges that make a full house/quads (TT, T2, 22). I want to extract value from Tens and weaker pairs while not bloating the pot unnecessarily against the rare full-house combos. Bet half the pot (200 into 400) to keep worse hands and single-pair Tens in and to charge any turn cards that could change the board texture. If called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue extracting on blanks; if he check-raises large I’ll assume a very strong, likely full-house holding and proceed cautiously (fold or pot-control depending on sizing), while a small raise I’ll treat as a bluff or thin value and call.

I have pocket treys on a board of 2d-Tc-2c. In a heads-up situation, a small pocket pair like 33 retains significant value on a paired board where it is unlikely the opponent has a deuce. While I am behind any Ten, I am ahead of most of Minni's range, which includes many high-card combinations that missed this flop. Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, making a call profitable to see if I can reach a relatively cheap showdown or improve on the turn. Raising would likely fold out her bluffs and get called by hands that beat me, so calling is the most balanced approach.

On the turn 6h, the board remains relatively safe for pocket treys, as it's a total blank. My pair is still ahead of all of Minni's missed high cards (like AJ, KQ, etc.) but is crushed by any Ten or a Deuce. In a heads-up pot, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size and induce bluffs from her weaker range. Betting would likely only get called by better hands. I intend to check and will likely call a reasonable bet to see a river, as my hand still has significant showdown value against her button-opening range.

I have J♦2♥ for trips on a 2♦ T♣ 2♣ board; Flash (BB) checked the turn after calling my flop c-bet. His check narrows his range toward single-pair Tens, underpairs and some overcards — very few combos (TT, T2, 22) make a full house/quads and are rare. I want value from Tx and worse pairs while keeping the pot controllable against the unlikely full-house/quads hands, so I’ll make a value bet about half the pot. A 400 chip bet is big enough to get calls from tens and worse pairs but small enough to fold out marginal hands and avoid bloating the pot if I’m behind to a full house; if he check-raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand and proceed cautiously, while a small raise I’ll treat as a bluff/thin value and call. Bet 400.

I am holding a small pocket pair on a paired board (2d-Tc-2c-6h). In heads-up play, pocket treys retain decent showdown value against a button opening range that contains many high-card combinations. Minni's bet of 400 into a pot of 800 is a standard double-barrel. While I am behind any Ten, any Six, or any higher pocket pair, I am ahead of her entire range of missed overcards (like Ace-high, King-high, etc.). With 3:1 pot odds, this is a clear call to see a river and attempt to reach a cheap showdown. Raising would only serve to isolate me against hands that beat me.

I am holding pocket treys on a board of 2d-Tc-2c-6h-5h. My hand constitutes two pair (3s and 2s) with a Ten kicker. In heads-up play, this is a marginal showdown hand. I have check-called two streets, and since the river card (5h) doesn't change much but could have improved some of Minni's range, checking is the most prudent action. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (any 10, 6, 5, 2, or higher pocket pair), whereas checking allows Minni to check back her air or potentially bluff, giving me the chance to see a showdown.

I have trip 2s (J♦2♥) on 2♦ T♣ 2♣ 6♥ 5♥. Pot is 1600 and Flash (BB) checked the river after calling my flop and turn bets. His line looks like single Tx, underpairs or other marginal hands — full houses/quads (TT, 22, 55, 66) are possible but relatively rare. With no more streets to fear, I should extract value from tens and worse pairs that will call a reasonable river bet. A half‑pot value bet (800 into 1600) is large enough to get value from Tx/medium pairs yet keeps the pot controlled against the unlikely full‑house combos. If he check‑raises big I’ll assume a full house/quads and fold; a small raise I’ll treat as thin value/bluff and call. Bet 800.

I am holding pocket treys on a board of 2d-Tc-2c-6h-5h. In a heads-up situation, this hand serves as a classic bluff catcher. Minni has fired three consecutive barrels of roughly half-pot. This line can certainly represent a Ten, a Deuce, or a medium pocket pair, but it is also a common line for a button-opener to take with overcards (like Ace-high or King-high) to force a fold from marginal hands. Given the pot odds of 3:1, I only need to be correct 25% of the time to make this call profitable. Since my hand beats all of Minni's missed overcards and some of her potential busted draws, I will call to see the showdown.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with T2o. This is a very weak hand, but it's heads-up and I only need to call 50 chips to see a flop. However, T2o is near the bottom of my range. Looking at recent hand history, Minni has been active and aggressive. While calling 50 is tempting for the pot odds, T2o realization is poor and I am likely to face a bet on the flop if I don't hit. Fold is the safer play to preserve my chip lead, but a call is justifiable to see a cheap flop. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is better than putting chips in with a hand that is dominated by most of the opponent's range.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) and acting first preflop. T♥9♠ is a strong, very playable heads-up hand — good equity and plenty of postflop playability. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to simply call. With deep stacks (~191BB) and a relatively active BB, I want initiative and to narrow his range. I’ll raise to 300 total (~3x the big blind): big enough to fold out the weakest hands and get value from worse, small enough to keep his calling/3-betting range wide. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable dry flops and control the pot on wet/connected boards; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold and preserve my stack, but I’ll call reasonable-sized 3-bets to play postflop given the deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind with 9 of clubs and 4 of clubs. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me 2:1 pot odds, meaning I need roughly 33% equity to justify a call. Suited hands, even with a gap like 94s, have decent playability in heads-up situations, and folding too often to standard button raises allows the opponent to exploit me. Given my chip lead and the suited nature of the hand, I will defend my big blind and see a flop.

I have 9c 4c on a 7s Kd 6s board. I have no direct pair or draw, only a very weak backdoor straight possibility. As the Big Blind checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard practice here. I will check and see how Minni reacts.

Flop is K♦ 7♠ 6♠ — a wet, connected board that hits the BB’s calling range (Kx, mid pocket pairs, 7x/6x, some connectors and spade draws). I hold T♥9♠: only a gutshot to an 8 (4 outs → ~8.5% to hit the turn, ~16.5% by river) and a very remote backdoor flush possibility. Preflop I wanted initiative, but this flop is not a favorable c-bet spot — a bet will often be called and build a pot where I have low equity. With position and a marginal draw, I’ll control the pot and try to realize equity by checking. Plan: see a free turn; call small turn bets to continue with the draw, fold to large turn raises, and if I hit the 8 I’ll start extracting value.

On the turn, the board has an Ace and a King. I have 9-high with no draw. Minni checked back on the flop, which often indicates a lack of a King or a strong draw, but she could be slow-playing or simply have a marginal hand that now fears the Ace. I have zero showdown value, but since I am out of position and have no equity, leading out into an Ace-high board against the pre-flop aggressor is risky. I will check and hope to see a free river or fold to any significant aggression, as bluffing here is unlikely to succeed against a range that likely contains many Ace-X or King-X hands that checked the flop for pot control.

I raised preflop to take the initiative, but the flop and now the turn (K♦ 7♠ 6♠ → A♦) are very unfavorable for my T♥9♠. I have only a gutshot to an 8 and a tiny backdoor flush (needs both remaining community cards to be spades) — equity is low versus Flash’s likely calling range (Kx, Ax, pairs, sets, spade draws). Flash checked the turn, so I’ll control the pot and realize my equity rather than build a big pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check to keep the pot small, call small bets on future streets to see the river, and fold to large aggression.

I have 9-high with no showdown value on a board of 7s-Kd-6s-Ad-Jd. Both players checked the flop and the turn, which indicates a range for Minni that consists of marginal made hands or complete air. While the river Jack could have improved some of my range, checking is the most prudent play here. I am the chip leader, and bluffing into a small pot when the opponent could easily have a marginal pair (like a 7 or a 6) that they are willing to take to showdown is unnecessarily risky. If Minni bets, I will fold, and if she checks, I will likely lose to her high-card or small-pair strength, but I preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Check. I hold T9o on 7s Kd 6s Ad Jd — no pair, only the missed gutshot/backdoor. Heads‑up my preflop raise kept a wide calling range (Kx, Ax, pockets, connectors, spade draws). The action has been passive flop→turn (both checked), which is consistent with either missed draws or cautious value (K/A/pairs). Flash checked the river as well. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity against the most likely holdings (Kx, Ax, pairs) and would unnecessarily bloat the pot when I have essentially zero showdown value. With deep stacks and a small pot (600), I prefer to preserve chips and realize equity — check to showdown and fold to any sizable river raise. Action: check (amount 0).

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 7c 5h. It is a weak hand, but it is 2-handed (Heads-Up). Usually, a wide range should be played from the button. However, against a Big Blind that has been active and given the 50/100 blind structure, completing the small blind for only 50 more chips is a reasonable way to see a flop cheaply without committing too much. I will call to see the flop.

Heads-up vs a limp/call from the button (Flash). A♠9♥ is a strong heads-up hand — good equity vs a wide limp/call range and a valuable ace-blocker. I want to seize the initiative, isolate a likely weak range, and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop. Raising to 300 total (3× big blind) is a standard, low-risk sizing given our ~19k stack: it folds out many speculative hands, gets value from worse A-x/Kx/pairs, and keeps pot controllable. Plan: if he calls, proceed postflop with position dynamics in mind and c-bet dry boards; if he 3-bets large, re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets, call smaller ones to play for position).

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 7c 5h. While I have position, 75o is an extremely weak hand. Minni has shown a pattern of frequent pre-flop aggression. Calling a raise with such a low-equity hand, even with 2:1 pot odds, is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am forced to fold to continued aggression or get trapped with a weak pair. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠5♠ (suited ace) — this is a strong hand to open aggressively. I’ve already posted 50 (SB) and the pot is 150; calling 50 is passive and hands like A5s realize much more equity and fold equity when raised. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) to take the initiative, build the pot when I have position, and price out some of the better hands the BB would like to see a cheap flop with. If I connect with an ace or a flush/straight draw I can continue; if I miss a coordinated flop I’ll use position to control the pot or fold to significant resistance. Versus a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets, call smaller ones to play postflop), but overall I want to play this hand aggressively from the button.

I have pocket sevens, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. Against a button open, raising (3-betting) is the standard play for value and to take control of the betting lead. I will raise to 900, which is 3x the initial raise.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♥5♠ and Flash responded with a 3‑bet to 900. I need to put in 600 more (900 total). Heads‑up this hand has good playability — even offsuit A5 has solid equity vs a 3‑bet range and I have the positional advantage postflop. Pot odds (600 to win ~1200) are roughly 33% break‑even, which lines up with A5o’s equity vs a typical 3‑bet. A 4‑bet here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and folding is too nitty heads‑up. I’ll call to 900 total, plan to continue on A‑high flops and on strong draws, and control/close the action or fold to large aggression on completely dry boards.

I 3-bet pre-flop and have the range advantage on this Ace-high dry board.
