A small continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) is a standard play here to represent an Ace and put pressure on Minni's non-pairing hands like KQ or suited connectors that missed. It also helps define where I am in the hand; if she raises, I can safely fold, and if she calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn knowing my pocket sevens still have some showdown value against her bluffs.

I have top pair (A♥5♠) on a very dry A♠-6♣-3♦ flop against an opponent who 3‑bet preflop. Flash’s flop bet is small (600 into a 2400 pot), which looks like a mixed c-bet range (thin value and bluffs). Raising here would bloat the pot and polarize my range — I’ll often face better Aces (AK/AQ) or pocket pairs that continue, so a raise is more likely to get me in trouble than to extract clean value. Calling keeps worse Aces and medium pairs in, uses my positional advantage, and gives me the option to control the pot on the turn. Pot odds to call are 600/(2400+600)=20% break‑even, and A5 has sufficient equity versus a typical 3‑bet c‑betting range to continue. Plan: call the 600, re‑evaluate on the turn — bet for value if checked to, or fold/defend based on the turn card and opponent’s action.

The 9h on the turn is a relatively neutral card. Having 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, I've represented a strong hand, specifically an Ace. Minni's call on the flop suggests she likely has an Ace, a six, or a pocket pair. By checking now, I can control the size of the pot and evaluate Minni's reaction. Betting again is unlikely to fold out an Ace and mostly just bloats the pot when I am likely behind. I will check to see if she checks back or to see the size of her bet.

I have top pair (A♥5♠) on a very dry A‑6‑3‑9 board. Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop small, then checked the turn — a line that contains a mix of medium‑strength hands and bluffs, but also some hands that now beat me (99, 66, A9, maybe slowplayed sets). I want thin value from worse Aces and small pairs and to deny any free river card, but I don’t want to bloat the pot against sets/two‑pair. A small/medium value bet (~1/3 pot) is the best line: it gets called by worse hands, folds pure air, and keeps future decisions manageable if I face aggression. I’ll bet 1,000 into the 3,000 pot; if he check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider folding, if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and extract or pot‑control accordingly.

On the turn, after I checked to represent weakness or pot-control, Minni bet 1,000 into a 3,000 pot. This is a small 1/3 pot sizing. While she could have an Ace, she could also be betting middle pairs (like 88), a 6, or simply taking a stab at the pot because I checked. My pocket sevens are still ahead of her bluffs and small pairs like 44 or 55. Given the excellent pot odds (I need to be right about 20% of the time) and the fact that I want to keep the pot manageable, I will call to see a river and re-evaluate her action there.

On the river, the board is Ace-high and now includes a Queen. Having 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, I've already represented a strong range. My pocket sevens are now purely a bluff-catcher or have some marginal showdown value against her missed draws or smaller pocket pairs. By checking, I control the pot size and allow Minni to check back her weaker hands or bluffs. Betting myself doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Queen, and I don't want to get raised off the best hand if she's turned a pair into a bluff.

I have top pair (A♥5♠) but a weak 5‑kicker. Flash 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet a small flop, then checked the turn and called my 1,000 — a line that contains Ax (many with better kickers), some medium pairs/sets, and missed broadway-type hands. On the river (Q♠) he checked, showing some weakness. With the pot at 5,000 a small value bet (~1/3 pot) is the correct line: it extracts from worse hands (Qx, Kx, medium pairs and some worse Ax that will call), denies a free showdown card, and keeps the pot manageable against the parts of his range that beat me. I will not bloat the pot — if he check‑raises big I will fold. Bet a small amount to get thin value and protect my stack.

On the river, the board is As-Qs-9h-6c-3d. I am holding pocket sevens. Minni has bet 1,500 into a 5,000 pot (roughly 30% pot). This small sizing following a similar small bet on the turn (1/3 pot) is often indicative of a thin value bet from an Ace or a Queen, trying to induce a call from middle pairs like mine. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 1,500 to win 8,000 means I only need to be right about 19% of the time), it is difficult to find many bluffs in Minni's range that call a 3-bet pre-flop and a c-bet on an Ace-high flop. Most hands she would be bluffing with (like 45s) are unlikely to be in her 3-bet calling range. Given my hand is now a very weak bluff-catcher and she has shown strength on every street, I will fold and preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, 98o is a solid hand to play from the Button/Small Blind. The current pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to call, giving me 3:1 odds. However, I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure, especially since Minni has been quite active. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to represent strength and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on favorable textures.

Facing a raise to 300 from the button while I’m in the big blind with 4s7s. Heads‑up the opener’s range is wide, and calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives me roughly 33% pot odds. With deep stacks I have good implied odds to realize equity and can play pot‑control OOP postflop; 3‑betting here would bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand and a min‑raise likely won’t fold many hands. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously—look to continue if I hit or have strong draw, fold to heavy aggression if I miss.

I completely missed this flop (Jh 5d 5s) with 4s7s — no pair and only weak backdoor possibilities. Flash was the preflop raiser and his continuation range hits this board often (Jx, 5x, pocket pairs, and many hands that will c-bet), and I’m out of position. I want to control the pot and avoid getting blown off a marginal hand or turned into a big bluff, so I’ll check and see what he does. I’ll fold to significant aggression on the flop and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw on later streets.

On a paired board like J-5-5, as the pre-flop raiser, I have a range advantage. A continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) is appropriate here to test Minni's hand. If she has a Jack or a 5, she will likely call or raise, but many of her hands that called pre-flop (middle cards, low pairs) might fold to this pressure. If she calls, I will likely check behind on the turn unless the board improves my hand or offers a strong semi-bluff opportunity.

I missed this flop (Jh 5d 5s) with 4s7s — no pair and only backdoor spade potential — but Flash’s flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot). Calling 300 to win 1,200 gives me ~25% pot odds. My chance to hit a pair by the river is ≈24% and the two-card backdoor spade flush adds a few more percent, so the price is about right to see a turn. I’m heads‑up with deep stacks, so I have implied odds to realize equity. Raising would bloat the pot out of position with a weak holding and folding now gives up decent equity for a small price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve, and continue only when I pair or pick up a real draw.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop. I missed pairing but picked up a real draw: with 4♠7♠ and the board J♥5♦5♠K♠ I have 9 spade outs plus 6 pair outs (≈15 outs ≈ 32–33% to improve on the river). Pot is 1,200 and I’m OOP — this is a good spot to apply small semi‑bluff pressure to fold out many Jx/medium pair hands while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet a third of the pot (400). If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I still have ~33% to improve to the river; if he check‑raises I will respect the strength and fold (I’m not committed with just a draw).

On the turn, Minni has led out for 400 into a pot of 1200 after calling my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet. My hand, 9-high, has no showdown value and virtually no equity to improve to a winning hand on the river. While the King is a scare card that I could theoretically represent as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's lead suggests she has a piece of the board (a Jack, a Five, or perhaps a King herself) that she is unlikely to fold to a single raise. Given her history of being sticky and aggressive in previous hands, bluffing here is high-risk with a low probability of success. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with pocket fives (5c5h) and very deep stacks (~226 BB). Pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive; raising takes the initiative and uses position postflop. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) — big enough to get fold equity and isolate the BB, but small enough to keep postflop playability with deep stacks. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet most dry, uncoordinated flops and play cautiously on connected/overcard boards; call a reasonable 3-bet to play in position but fold to very large 4-bets that commit too much out of line.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a premium hand in a heads-up match. Minni is opening frequently from the button/small blind position. I will 3-bet to 900 to extract value and gain the initiative in the hand, as this hand is well ahead of a standard button opening range.

Call to 900. I’m on the button with 5c5h facing a 3-bet to 900 and very deep stacks (~220+ BB). Small pocket pairs are best played by set-mining in position: calling preserves my postflop advantage and maximizes implied odds if I hit a set. A 4-bet here is too thin and creates difficult spots; folding surrenders too much equity. Postflop plan: play cautiously on overcard/connected boards (check/call or pot-control), and extract value/commit when I hit a set.

On this board of Kc-Js-9s, I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (a Queen gives me an Ace-high straight). As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage, although this board texture connects well with a 3-bet calling range. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 600, which is 1/3 of the pot. This allows me to maintain initiative, capitalize on my fold equity against her air, and cheaply realize my equity with the gutshot draw.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 5s, deep stacks (~217 BB) and I’m facing a 600 bet into a 2400 pot after calling a preflop 3-bet. Pocket fives are set-mining hands — chance to hit a set by river from the flop is only ~8.5%, which is below the raw pot‑odds break‑even (~20%) — so calling purely on immediate equity is weak. However, because stacks are very deep and the opponent 3‑betted preflop and is now c‑betting, implied odds if I hit a set are huge and I have position. Raising as a bluff here is risky against a 3‑bettor (they will call or 4‑bet too often), and folding surrenders too much equity for a small price. I’ll call the 600 to realize my implied odds and keep the pot controllable; plan to proceed cautiously on a runout that doesn’t improve me (fold to heavy turn/river aggression), but if I hit a set I will start building the pot for value while watching for spade/straight texture.

The turn 5s is a highly impactful card as it completes potential flush draws. I currently hold the Ten of Spades, which gives me a flush draw to go along with my gutshot straight draw (any Queen). However, as the out-of-position player on a board that has become very coordinated, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially see a free river card to realize my equity. If Minni bets, I have significant outs to improve my hand and will evaluate whether to call based on her sizing.

I just turned a set (5c5h on Kc 9s Js 5s). Villain was the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑betted the flop but checked the turn — that line is consistent with a lot of Kx/Jx, some pocket pairs, and a few connected/spade combos (QT, two spades). My hand is strong vs the bulk of that range and vulnerable mainly to straights (QT) or two‑card spade flushes and larger sets (JJ/99) — but those are a minority. I should start building the pot and charge Kx/Jx and spade/straight draws rather than giving a free card. With deep stacks a medium size keeps worse hands and draws in while leaving room to extract more on the river. I’ll lead for about half‑pot to get value and deny equity. If I get raised I’ll re‑evaluate (small check‑raises I’ll often call/3‑bet; massive polarizing raises will force a tougher decision but likely indicate a very narrow, usually beating, range).

On this turn, the 5s completes the flush. I have the Ten of Spades (Ts), giving me a flush draw, and I also have a gutshot straight draw (any Queen). Minni has bet 1500 into a 3000 pot (1/2 pot sizing). To call, I need 25% equity (1500 / 6000). With 9 spades remaining in the deck (one of which is a Queen) and 3 additional non-spade Queens, I have roughly 12 outs to a very strong hand. 12 outs with one card to come gives me approximately a 26% chance of hitting on the river. Given the pot odds and the potential for implied odds if I hit my straight or flush against a King or a lower flush, calling is the mathematically correct decision. Raising is too risky as I would have to fold my equity to a shove, and checking-folding would be leaving too much value on the table given the strength of the draw.

On this river, I am left with Ace-high. I have the Ten of Spades which acts as a blocker to some flushes, but my overall hand strength is very low for this board texture (Kc-9s-Js-5s-8h). My check-call on the turn was based on the mathematical equity of my flush and straight draws, which did not materialize. Leading into Minni now would be a pure bluff; however, her range, which called a pre-flop 3-bet and a flop c-bet before leading the turn, is quite strong and likely contains many Kings, Jacks, or completed flushes that are unlikely to fold. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown or fold if she bets, as Ace-high has almost no value here against her betting range.

I have trip fives (5c5h) on Kc 9s Js 5s 8h — a very strong showdown hand versus Flash’s line. Flash 3‑betted preflop, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn but called my 1500 — that sequence is full of single pair combos (Kx, Jx), medium pairs, some A‑high hands and a subset of spade/straight draws. The 8♦ (8h) river is a relatively blank — it doesn’t add another spade (flush would require two spades in hand) and Q10 straights are a minority of his 3‑bet/call range. Flash checking the river looks like weakness/pot‑control, so I should extract value rather than checking behind and giving free showdowns. I’ll make a value bet about half the pot to get called by Kx, Jx and other worse pairs while keeping the price reasonable for him to call. Bet 3000 (≈50% pot). If he raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (a big check‑raise would usually signify a hand that beats me); I’m prepared to call small/medium raises for value but respect an extreme shove as a polarized line.

On this board texture (Kc-9s-Js-5s-8h), my Ace-high has very little showdown value. While I hold the Ten of Spades (Ts) as a blocker to some flushes and the nut straight (QT), Minni's betting line is extremely strong. She called a pre-flop 3-bet, called a flop continuation bet, and then led out on the turn once the third spade arrived and continued on the river. Her range is heavily weighted towards value hands like flushes, straights (QT), or two-pair (KJ, K9). Even her potential bluffs or weaker value bets like a Jack or a King beat my Ace-high. I need 25% equity to call the 3,000 bet into the 9,000 pot, but it is highly unlikely that Ace-high is ahead 25% of the time given her aggression on this coordinated board. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
KTo is a strong starting hand for heads-up play. Being on the button, I should take the initiative and open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind.

I call — total 300. Heads-up on the button opened to 300 and I have pocket deuces (2♠2♥) with very deep stacks (≈255 BB vs 140 BB). This is a classic set‑mining spot: calling is cheap (200 more) and gives huge implied odds if I hit a set. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to inflate the pot unnecessarily with a small pair by 3‑betting (little fold equity and lots of reverse implied odds vs overcards). Plan: see the flop; if I miss I’ll keep pot control and evaluate opponent’s actions, if I hit a set I’ll extract value. Only re-evaluate if Flash 3‑bets to a very large sizing — otherwise flat call is the most +EV line.

Check. I called preflop as a set‑mine and the flop Q♦ J♠ 5♣ is full of overcards to my pocket deuces. Out of position with a small pair and very deep stacks I want pot control and to preserve my implied odds rather than inflate the pot. Checking lets Flash take the lead — I’ll call small/medium c‑bets to realize set equity and fold or sharply re‑evaluate only against very large overbets/raises or strong lines; if Flash checks back I can see a cheap turn and extract value if I hit a set.

On a board of Q-J-5, I have King-high with a double gutshot straight draw (any Ace or Nine completes the straight). Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is the standard play. This board texture hits my perceived range well, and I have significant equity to improve even if called. A half-pot bet of 300 is appropriate to apply pressure and build the pot.

I have pocket deuces on a Q♦ J♠ 5♣ flop and Flash has made a small c‑bet (300 into a 900 pot). This is a classic set‑mine spot: calling 300 gives ~25% immediate pot odds and, with very deep stacks, excellent implied odds if I hit trips. I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a small pair, so calling preserves my chance to realize equity and to extract value only if I improve. I’ll call the flop and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold only to very large raises/overbets or lines that strongly represent a made hand; if I hit a set I’ll look to extract maximum value.

Board: 5♣ Q♦ J♠ T♠, my hand 2♠2♥. I called the flop as a set‑mine and the turn is a blank for my hand — I have only a small pair with no meaningful backdoor draws and I’m out of position. Flash checked to me, so betting here would bloat the pot when I’m likely behind to the portion of his raising/cbetting range (Q/J/T, broadways, two‑pair, straights) and I have very little fold equity. Pot control is the best line: check and see a river cheaply to realize my equity and only commit more if I improve or if he gives a weak/clear foldable line. If Flash bets the turn I’ll evaluate sizing (call small/medium bets to keep set‑mining, fold to very large overbets); with no bet now I check.

The Turn Ten gave me second pair (pair of Tens) while maintaining my gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine). Minni has checked for the second street in a row. While I have improved, the board is quite coordinated (Q-J-T), and many hands in Minni's range, like a Queen or Jack, still beat me. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid a potential check-raise, and get to see a free river card to potentially improve to a straight, trips, or two pair. This also allows me to reach showdown more cheaply with my current pair.

Board 5c Qd Js Ts 8s, my hand 2s2h — I never improved. Villain opened pre, c‑bet small on the flop and then checked turn; he checked the river so he’s shown pot control or missed equity. Pot is small (1,200). Betting here is thin: most hands that will call a river bet (Qx, Jx, some Txs) beat me, and a bluff won’t fold enough of his calling range to be +EV — plus I risk a check‑raise. Best line is pot control and take it to showdown. I’ll check and avoid bloating the pot; if Flash now bets a small amount I’ll reevaluate (call small, fold to big overbets).

On a board of 5c-Qd-Js-Ts-8s, I have a pair of Tens with a King kicker. The river 8s completes a possible straight for any hand containing a Nine (Q-J-T-9-8) and brings a potential flush with three spades on the board. Minni has checked all three streets (after calling my flop bet). While Minni's checks might indicate weakness, my pair of Tens is likely only middle-strength on this highly coordinated board. There is little value in betting as most hands that call me (like a Queen or a Jack) have me beaten, and I have sufficient showdown value to check back and hope to win against missed draws or lower pairs like 8s or 5s. Checking ensures I reach showdown without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button with K♣J♥ — a strong, standard opening hand in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call, but I want the initiative and postflop position. I’ll make a standard 2x BB open (min-raise to 200 total) to apply pressure, isolate the BB, and keep the pot manageable versus an opponent who has shown frequent preflop aggression. If called I’ll c-bet favorable K/J/A flops and control size on dry low boards; if met with a large 3-bet I’ll fold preflop or re-evaluate based on sizing/tendencies. Raise to 200 gives good fold equity and the positional advantage I want.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind against a min-raise with a hand like Qd7c is standard. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300), which means I only need about 25% equity to make the call profitable. While Q7o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to see a flop in a wide-range environment, especially given Minni's frequent opening from the button. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

I have flopped trip Queens with a 7 kicker on a Qc-Qs-Kd board. This is an extremely strong hand, especially in a heads-up pot. My goal is to maximize value. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, she is likely to continuation-bet a wide range of hands, including Kings, pocket pairs, and total air. By checking, I allow her to lead into the pot and potentially commit more chips. Leading out (donk betting) might reveal the strength of my hand too early and cause her to fold her bluffs or weak marginal hands. I will check to induce a bet and then decide whether to call or raise based on the bet sizing.

Flop: Qc Qs Kd — I hold KcJh: top pair (Kings) with a decent kicker. Heads-up vs a BB caller whose preflop calling range contains lots of Kx, broadways, and some Qx. The hands that crush me are any queen (trips/full or full houses) and a small subset of Kx combos with an Ace kicker (AK). Against the rest of his range I am a clear favorite or roughly flipping. I want to extract value from worse kings and many broadways while keeping the pot controlled and preserving an easy fold vs a large check-raise (which will usually represent a queen). A standard value c-bet of ~50% pot is appropriate to charge worse Kx and other calling hands and to deny free cards. Plan: bet 200; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll assume Qx/very strong and fold.

I have flopped trips with Queens. The board is Qc-Qs-Kd. Minni has bet half the pot (200 into 400). While my kicker (7) is weak, trips are an extremely strong hand in heads-up play. If I raise now, I risk folding out her bluffs or weaker Kings. By calling, I keep her range wide and encourage her to continue betting on the turn with air or marginal made hands. The board is relatively dry, so giving a free card is not overly dangerous. I will call and look to extract value on later streets.

On the turn, the board is Qc-Qs-Kd-8s and I have trip Queens with a 7 kicker. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. On the flop, I checked and called a half-pot bet. By checking the turn, I continue to disguise the strength of my hand and allow Minni to continue her aggression with bluffs or marginal made hands like a King. If I bet now, I risk folding out hands that are currently drawing thin but might still put money in the pot on the river or continue bluffing. If she checks back, I can look to lead for value on the river. My kicker is weak, so I'm not looking to build a massive pot yet unless she shows significant interest.

I have K♣J♥ on Q♣ Q♠ K♦ 8♠ — two pair (Kings and Queens). This is a very strong hand heads‑up but it’s vulnerable to any Q (trips/full house) and to K♣Qx (a full house). Flash called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn: that line can be either pot‑control with Kx or a trap with Qx. There are no meaningful draws on the turn, so betting for value is correct. I’ll make a medium value bet — ~1/2 pot (400) — to charge worse kings and broadways while keeping the pot manageable if I’m check‑raised. If he flats I can re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll treat that as Qx/very strong and fold.

I have trip Queens on a board of Qc-Qs-Kd-8s. While my kicker is weak (7), trips are an extremely strong hand in a heads-up match. I will call the turn bet to continue disguising my hand strength and to induce Minni to bluff or value-bet a King on the river. Raising here might scare off her bluffs or marginal hands, and given my weak kicker, I want to keep the pot manageable while still giving her the opportunity to commit more chips if she is behind.

I have trip Queens with a weak kicker (7) on a board of Qc-Qs-Kd-8s-2s. Having checked and called on the flop and turn, checking the river is consistent with my strategy of disguising my hand's strength and inducing Minni to bluff or value-bet a King. While I am likely ahead of her Kings and bluffs, my weak kicker makes me wary of a better Queen or a full house (like KQ or Q8). Checking allows me to see what Minni does; if she bets, I will likely call to catch bluffs or extract value from her one-pair hands, while minimizing losses against the top of her range.

I hold KcJh on Qc Qs Kd 8s 2s — two pair (Kings and Queens). Flash called my preflop open, called a standard flop c‑bet and then called my turn bet, then checked the river. That line looks like medium‑strength hands (weaker Kx, some underpairs or broadways) more than a slow‑played full house or trips (those would often raise earlier). The river is relatively dry aside from a possible spade flush, and Flash checked it, so I expect enough worse hands to call a value bet. I want to extract value but not shut out thin calls, so I’ll make a medium river value bet (~1/2–2/3 pot). If he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand (Qx/full house or a disguised rivered flush) and fold. Betting 1,000 chips should get called by weaker kings and some pair+kicker hands while maximizing value.

I have trip Queens with a 7 kicker on a board of Qc-Qs-Kd-8s-2s. Having checked and called on the flop and turn, checking the river was consistent with my strategy of disguising my hand's strength. Minni has now bet 1000 into a pot of 1600. While trips are very strong in heads-up play, my weak kicker (7) makes me vulnerable to better Queens (like QA, QJ, QT, or Q9) or a full house (KQ, Q8, Q2, KK). However, Minni could also be value-betting a King or bluffing with missed cards. Calling is the most prudent action as it allows me to win against her bluffs and Kings while minimizing losses against the top of her range that would likely call or re-raise a raise from me.

Holding Ad 2c on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a standard opening hand with high card value and an Ace blocker. Raising is the optimal strategy here to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300, which has been the consistent opening size in this game.

Heads-up vs the button who raised to 300; pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need ~33% equity to break even. I hold 5♠2♦ offsuit — one of the weakest hands preflop, very likely dominated and with terrible postflop playability and few useful blockers. A 3-bet bluff is possible in some spots, but not with this hand. Fold and preserve a deep stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠7♣ (Ah7c) is a clear raising spot — I have position and a hand that plays well postflop. I want initiative and fold equity versus the BB, so I’ll open-raise rather than just completing. A standard 3xBB-ish size (300 total) pressures wide calling ranges, keeps the pot reasonable vs my deep stack (23100), and gives me room to c-bet favorable flops. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold and preserve stack; if he calls I’ll use position to control the hand and extract value on A-high or dry boards.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 4c 2c. Minni, on the button/small blind, has raised to 300. This is a 3x open. While 4c 2c is suited, it is one of the weakest hands in the range. Pot odds of 2:1 are tempting, but playing such a low-equity hand out of position for the remainder of the street against an aggressive opponent is likely to result in a negative expected value (-EV). I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the button with QJ offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My stack is slightly smaller than Minni's, but still very healthy at 167 big blinds. Based on previous hands, a standard 3x open has been effective for maintaining pressure or taking down the blinds. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative.

I have KK in the big blind facing a button open to 300 heads-up. This is near the best hand preflop — I want to 3-bet for value and to deny equity to broadways and suited connectors. A standard ~3x sizing (900 total) extracts value from worse aces, kings and pairs, gives decent fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. I’ve already posted 100, so raising to 900 adds the right pressure compared with a tiny min-raise or just calling. If Flash shoves I can call; if he calls I’ll continue for value on favorable runouts.

In a heads-up match, QJo is a strong hand, but it faces a 3-bet from Minni. Given the depth of our stacks (over 160 BBs) and our positional advantage on the button, calling the 3-bet is the standard and most flexible play. Folding is too weak against a likely wide 3-betting range in this format, and 4-betting would turn a hand with good post-flop potential into a bluff unnecessarily. I will call to see a flop and use my position to navigate the rest of the hand.

I flopped trips (KcKd on Ks) on a fairly dry, rainbow K-T-4 board. This is a near-nut value spot — I want to build the pot and charge hands like Kx, Tx, pairs and worse broadways that called my 3-bet pre. A ~2/3-pot bet gets called by those hands without folding out too much equity. If called I'll keep extracting on later streets; if I face a large raise I'll re-evaluate but this hand is strong enough to call or commit for value.

On a board of K-T-4, my QJ offers only a gutshot straight draw (needing an Ace). Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop, has led out for 2/3 of the pot. This board hits a typical 3-betting range (containing hands like AK, KQ, KK, and TT) very hard. Given that my overcards (Q and J) might not even be clean outs if she has a King or a better Ace-high like AQ/AJ, and my gutshot only has about an 8% chance of hitting on the turn, I do not have sufficient equity to call this bet. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I want to play aggressive and take the initiative. 8♣4♦ is a weak hand, but in heads-up opening a very wide range is standard — a minimum raise applies pressure on the BB and lets me play postflop with position. The min-raise to 200 (2×BB) is small enough to be low-variance but still gives fold equity; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate and fold most of my bottom range. If he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or pot-control and fold to heavy resistance on dry boards. Raise to 200 to steal the blind and keep the initiative while preserving stack flexibility.
I have Ac Qc, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni raised to 200 from the small blind. I will 3-bet to 700 to take the initiative, build the pot, and capitalize on my strong holding.

Facing a 3-bet to 700 after my min-raise, I have 8♣4♦ — a very weak, poorly blocking, offsuit hand. Calling 500 to play a pot that would be 1400 gives me roughly 36% pot odds, and my equity vs a typical 3‑bet range is well below that. Although I’m in position, postflop playability is terrible (I’ll miss most boards and be difficult to play against aggression), and a small 4‑bet bluff here won’t credibly fold enough of his range and needlessly risks chips. Better to preserve my 23.9k stack and wait for a much stronger or more playable spot. Fold.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button. It has good connectivity and straight potential. I will raise to the standard 300 to take control of the pot and see how Minni reacts.

Facing a 3BB open to 300 from the button (Flash) with the pot at 400 and 200 to call, I’ll defend. Jc6d is a marginal hand but in heads-up big blind we should defend widely — call costs 200 to win a 600 pot (break-even equity ≈ 200/(400+200) = 33%). Both stacks are deep (my 23,850 vs his 15,750), so we have good implied odds if we hit two pair, trips, or a favorable flip. 3-betting as a bluff is feasible sometimes, but with J6o it’s often dominated or will lead to difficult out-of-position decisions postflop; folding is too nitty in heads-up. Plan: call to see the flop, play for pot control if we miss, and be prepared to fold to heavy action unless we improve or get a good read.

Check. Heads-up on A♣-3♠-9♦ with J♣6♦ out of position I have no pair and essentially no draw (flush is impossible and straight outs are minimal). Flash’s button opening range contains a lot of A-x and broadway hands, so I’m routinely behind. Betting as a bluff here risks bloating the pot into spots where I have very little fold equity against his value-heavy range and poor playability if he raises. Better line is pot control: check and see what he does, call small bets to realize equity or fold to large aggression, and look to improve (pair a J/6 or pick up a free card if he checks back). Given deep stacks and the dry board, checking keeps difficult decisions manageable and preserves chips for better spots.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. While my hand has some showdown value, betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands (Aces) will call, and worse hands will mostly fold, except for some drawing hands. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, keep her range wide, and potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff on later streets.

Check. Out of position on A♣-3♠-9♦-7♥ with J♣6♦ I have no pair and virtually no equity vs a typical button opening range (lots of A‑x and broadways). Both players checked the flop, which signals weakness; betting the turn would be a pure bluff that will be called too often and bloats a pot I cannot win at showdown. I prefer pot control: check and evaluate. Plan: fold to significant turn aggression (I have no real outs), call only very small probing bets (≈≤200) to see a cheap river, and if he checks back re-evaluate the river for a possible steal only on a very favorable blank or if I pick up a pair.

I have middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. After checking back on the flop to control the pot, Minni has checked again on the turn. While I might be able to get thin value from a Seven or a lower pocket pair, checking back remains the most consistent strategy to reach showdown cheaply. This avoids a potential check-raise from a trapping Ace and keeps her range wide, allowing me to catch bluffs on the river or simply win with my current hand strength.

Board A♣-3♠-9♦-7♥-K♠, I hold J♣6♦. Both players checked flop and turn, which signals either weakness or a slow-played A/K. The river K improves a lot of the button’s plausible opening range (Kx) and leaves Ax still ahead — I have no pair, no relevant redraws and very limited fold equity. The pot is only 600 versus deep stacks, so risking chips on a thin river bluff is poor EV; betting will often be called by hands that beat me or fold out only very weak holdings. I’ll preserve chips and check. If Flash decides to bet the river I’ll fold to meaningful aggression (unless it’s an absurdly tiny probe), but I won’t inflate the pot with a bluff here.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board containing an Ace and a King. After checking the flop and turn, the King on the river makes my hand even more vulnerable. Minni has checked for the third time, suggesting she likely doesn't have a strong hand, but she could be trapping with an Ace or a King. Given the pot size and my hand's moderate showdown value, checking back is the most prudent play to win the pot without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with A5s is a very strong, playable hand: suited Ace, good blocker, and I have position postflop. Pot is small (150) and calling 50 would forfeit the initiative. With deep stacks I want to open a wide but aggressive range to build pots and deny the BB good odds. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is appropriate — it pressures the big blind, keeps weaker hands in my range for value, and leaves plenty of room to maneuver postflop. If Flash 3-bets small I’ll often call and play postflop; I’ll fold to an excessively large 3-bet depending on sizing. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to extract value or realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened for a 3x raise from the button. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, KTo is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable, as 3-betting might force out weaker hands that I dominate while bloating the pot out of position against an aggressive opponent.

On this Ace-high board, I have middle pair (Tens) with a strong King kicker. While middle pair is relatively strong in heads-up play, the presence of the Ace means Minni's pre-flop raising range contains many hands that currently beat me. Checking is the most prudent action here to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet, but leading out would likely only get called by better hands or forced folds from hands I already beat.

Heads-up on an A-high, fairly dry flop I have Ac5c: top pair with a weak kicker and a backdoor club draw, plus the Ac blocker to many Ax combos. Flash called preflop from the BB so his range is wide (lots of Ax, pocket pairs, broadways and some suited connectors). I want to extract value from worse aces, medium pairs and draws while denying free cards and using position to control the hand. A medium c-bet (~50% pot) balances value and pot control — it gets called by worse hands but doesn’t bloat the pot vs stronger Ax. Bet 300.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. In heads-up play, middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue against a standard half-pot continuation bet. Folding would be too tight given Minni's wide opening and betting range on the button. Raising would likely only result in better hands calling and worse hands folding. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, retain Minni's bluffs in her range, and see how the board develops on the turn.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. On the turn, the Five of Hearts is a blank that doesn't significantly alter the board state. My hand is a classic mid-strength hand that is excellent for pot control. Leading out would likely result in being called by better (Aces) or folding out hands I currently beat. By checking, I maintain a manageable pot size, allow Minni to continue bluffing with her wider button range, and can comfortably call a standard-sized bet to see a river. I am playing a check-call strategy on this street.

I have Ac5c on Ad Th 2c 5h — I turned two pair (Aces and Fives), which is very strong heads-up. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c-bet; his range is wide (weaker Ax, Tx, medium pairs, occasional draws), so I want to extract value from worse aces, tens and pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable and denying a free river to the few runner-runner combos. The main hands that beat me are sets (TT, 22, or a 5) or AT (Aces & Tens), but those are less common. I’ll bet ~half the pot to get called by the worst part of his range but avoid folding out too much value — bet 600. If he raises strongly I’ll re-evaluate (a big raise is likely sets/AT).

On this Ace-high board, I have middle pair (Tens) with a strong King kicker. Minni's half-pot bet on the turn is a standard continuation for both value and bluffs. In heads-up play, folding a hand as strong as second pair on the turn to a single bet would be too conservative, especially against an active opponent who raises frequently from the button. By calling, I maintain pot control, allow her to continue with her range of bluffs or weaker hands on the river, and avoid bloating the pot against a possible Ace. I will evaluate the river action based on the card and her sizing.

On this dry Ace-high board, my middle pair (Tens) has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking the river, I am controlling the size of the pot and inviting Minni to check back with marginal hands or potentially bluff with missed draws. If I bet, I would likely only be called by an Ace or better, whereas checking allows me to realize my equity more efficiently. I will decide whether to call a bet based on her sizing and perceived range after she reacts.

I hold Ac5c on Ad Th 2c 5h 6s — two pair (Aces and Fives), a very strong hand heads-up. Flash called my flop and turn bets and now checks the river, which polarizes his line toward medium-strength hands or missed draws; the bulk of his continuing range (weaker Aces, single Tens, medium pocket pairs and bluff-catchers) is behind my two pair. Only a few hands beat me (primarily AT and sets like TT/22/55), so this is a clear value-spot. I want a medium-sized value bet to get called by worse Aces/Tens/pairs while controlling the pot size vs the rarer stronger hands — ~50% pot is ideal. If he raises big on the river it will usually be a set or AT and I'll re-evaluate (fold to an overbet shove; call small/medium raises). Bet 1200 into the 2400 pot.

On the river, the board is A-T-2-5-6 with no completed flushes. I hold second pair (Tens) with a King kicker. Minni has bet 1200 into a 3600 pot, offering me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, second pair is a very strong bluff-catcher. While Minni's three-barrel line often represents an Ace, her small river sizing (1/3 pot) could indicate thin value or a final attempt to take the pot with missed broadway draws like KQ, KJ, or QJ, which are all within her button-raising range. Given the excellent price and the need to defend my range against an aggressive opponent, I cannot fold here. I will call to realize my equity.

I am on the button with T7o. While this is a marginal hand, in a heads-up situation, I want to maintain an aggressive posture and attempt to take the pot down pre-flop or navigate post-flop with the initiative. I will raise to my standard 3x size. If Minni 3-bets, I can comfortably fold given the weakness of my hand.

Heads-up against the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I have K♣6♣ in the big blind. In heads-up play the SB opens very wide and K6s has decent equity and good backdoor club potential. The price to see a flop is small (I need to put in 200 more; total committed would be 300) relative to pot (400) and both stacks are deep, so I can realize equity and play post-flop. A small 3-bet here wouldn’t gain much (min-raise is tiny and a larger 3-bet unnecessarily bloats a pot with a marginal hand). Plan: call, see the flop; if I hit a king or get strong club/straight backdoors I will continue/aggress, otherwise play cautiously oop — fold to strong action on dry boards or proceed to controlled calls on moderate action.

Heads-up vs the SB (Flash) who opened very wide preflop. I have Kc6c — one overcard to the Q and a backdoor club draw. The flop Qh 4d 3c is very dry and I'm out of position. Leading or betting OOP with a marginal hand that has limited equity (6 clean pair-outs, ~12.8% to pair on the turn, only ~4% backdoor flush by river) unnecessarily bloats a pot with deep stacks. Better strategy is pot control and information: check and see what Flash does. If he continuation-bets a small/standard amount I'll call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold or strongly re-evaluate to large raises/shoves. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve or bluff-catch. Therefore I check now.

The flop is Q-high and dry (4d-Qh-3c). I have Ten-high and no real equity. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet to represent a Queen or a high pocket pair. A bet of 300 (half-pot) should be sufficient to fold out many of Minni's non-pairing hands.

Heads-up vs a very wide SB open: Kc6c has decent equity here — a King overcard to the small cards plus a backdoor club possibility (one club on board). Flash’s 300 into the ~600 pot (~25% break-even equity to call) is a standard, small c-bet and gives me correct pot odds to see the turn. Raising OOP with this marginal holding would bloat the pot and put me in difficult spots; folding concedes too much vs a wide c-betting range. I’ll call 300 to realize equity, keep the pot controlled, and re-evaluate on the turn: continue (call or occasionally raise) only if I improve (pair a King, pick up a club or a clear draw) or face weak action; fold to strong turn/river aggression without improvement.

Board is 4d Qh 3c 3s; I hold Kc6c — no pair, only an overcard and a very weak backdoor club possibility. I called the small flop c-bet to realize equity; the turn paired the board and Flash checked, which looks like weakness or pot control. I did not improve (no King, no meaningful club), and my chance to pair on the river is only ~13% (6 outs), so betting as a pure bluff here is thin and will often be called by better hands. Pot control and seeing a free river is the prudent play: check and re-evaluate on the river. If I hit a King or pick up a club on the river I can proceed with a value bet or a more credible bluff; otherwise I will fold to strong river aggression.

On the turn, the board pairs the three (4d-Qh-3c-3s). Minni has checked for the second time after calling my flop continuation bet. My Ten-high has no showdown value and no draws. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from marginal hands like pocket pairs (55-JJ) or a weak four, Minni's flop call on such a dry board suggests she likely has a Queen or is planning to be sticky with a middle pair. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am currently trailing in the match, I will check back to see a free river and control the size of the pot, as I am unlikely to fold out a Queen on this board.

River: board 4♦ Q♥ 3♣ 3♠ T♦, pot 1,200. I hold K♣6♣ — I missed (no pair, no flush). Flash opened very wide preflop and c-bet small on the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river, which reads as pot-control or a weak/medium showdown type hand (Qx, a small pair, missed draws, or a turned ten). There is no bet to call. Betting as a bluff here is thin — I don’t block enough of the combos that would call or raise (Q/T combos) and many of Flash’s hands will simply call or check behind; a bet also risks a check‑raise. With deep stacks and the goal of preserving chips, the prudent play is pot control and check to showdown rather than investing chips with K‑high. Action: check.

On the river, the Ten of Diamonds pairs my Ten of Spades, giving me a pair of Tens. The action so far (Minni calling a flop continuation bet and then checking the turn and river) suggests she may have a marginal hand like a small pocket pair or a weak four. By checking back the turn, I signaled that I was likely done with the hand or looking for a cheap showdown. Now that I have improved to a pair of Tens, I have significant showdown value. I do not believe betting will result in many worse hands calling, nor will it likely force a Queen to fold. Therefore, checking to see the showdown is the most prudent play to maximize my expected value while minimizing risk.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB). K2o is a marginal hand but in heads-up with positional advantage I should open-raise a wide range to steal the blind and take initiative. A minimum raise to 200 (total) applies pressure while keeping the pot controllable; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll usually fold K2o, if he calls I’ll play the flop last and use position to extract value or realize equity.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit (Ac Th) in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni (the Small Blind/Button) has raised to 200. While calling is an option to keep the pot controlled out of position, I prefer to 3-bet to 600 total here to take initiative and extract value from weaker hands in Minni's opening range. This also helps define where I stand in the hand.

Heads‑up I have positional advantage and K2o is weak but not worthless — it has reasonable showdown equity and great postflop playability against a wide 3‑bet range. The cost to continue is only 400 more into a pot of 800 (facing 400 to win 800 → ~2:1 pot odds), so calling is cheap and keeps the pot controllable. Calling preserves my position to see a flop and either take the initiative with a c‑bet or fold to heavy action if I miss. A light 4‑bet with K2o would be thin and unnecessarily inflates the pot; folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call and play the flop last.

On this monotone board of 6s-Ts-Js, I have hit middle pair with my Ten of Hearts. However, I do not hold a spade, which makes this board extremely dangerous. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I could continue with a bet, but checking is a safer way to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. My hand has some showdown value, but it is very vulnerable to any spade in Minni's range. Checking allows me to evaluate the turn based on her action and the next card.

I have K♦2♠ on 6♠ T♠ J♠ — no pair but I hold a spade and a K overcard. That’s a real semi‑bluff: 9 spade outs + 2 non‑spade kings + 3 twos = 14 unique outs (~30% to hit on the turn, ~51% by the river). Flash checked after 3‑betting preflop, so betting gives me fold equity against missed hands (A‑high/Q‑high, small pairs) while still having strong equity if called. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet to pressure non‑spade holdings but keep the sizing manageable if he fights back. Plan: bet to take it now or realize my draw cheaply if called; be prepared to fold to a large check‑raise (likely a made flush/set) but call smaller raises depending on odds.

On this monotone board (6s Ts Js), I have hit middle pair with my Ten of Hearts, but I lack the insurance of a spade in my hand. Minni's half-pot bet of 600 into 1200 is a standard continuation after I checked. While the board is dangerous, middle pair in a 3-bet pot is generally too strong to fold to a single bet. I have pot odds of 3:1 to make the call. Raising would be unwise as it would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws that have significant equity against me. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate, though I must remain cautious if another spade or an overcard appears.

The turn card is the King of Hearts, which is another overcard to my pair of Tens and completes several potential straight draws (like Q9). The board remains very dangerous with three spades present. Since I am out of position and my hand's relative strength has decreased with the arrival of the King, I will check to see how Minni proceeds. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, so pot control is my primary objective.

I have K♦2♠ on 6♠ T♠ J♠ K♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Flash 3‑bet preflop and then just called my flop aggression, so his continuing range contains made tens/jacks, medium pairs, spade combos (both made flushes and one‑spade draws), and some broadway/overcards. He checked the turn, giving me the initiative. Betting now accomplishes two things: (1) extract value from worse pairs (T, J, smaller pairs) and Kx’s; (2) charge and fold single‑spade draws that had decent equity on the flop but have only ~19% to hit on the river — too little to call a sufficiently sized bet. Pot is 2400; I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (1600). If he calls I can re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll give him credit for a very strong/flush hand and fold. Bet 1600 to protect my hand and extract value.

The board has become extremely coordinated (6s Ts Js Kh) with three spades and a potential straight (Q9 or AQ). My pair of Tens is now third pair, and I do not have a spade in my hand to act as a blocker or a draw to the nut flush. Minni's bet of 1600 into 2400 (roughly 2/3 pot) on the turn represents significant strength, likely a King, a Jack, a flush, or a straight draw with high equity. Given that I am out of position and my hand has very little chance of being the best if another bet follows on the river, the most prudent professional play is to fold and preserve my stack.

In heads-up play, Jack-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. If called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

Flash (SB) opened to 300. I’m in the BB with J♣7♦ — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and it’s only 200 to call, so pot odds (roughly 3:1) justify a defend. I don’t want to 3-bet with this hand (too thin OOP and likely dominated by many of his 3-bet/calling hands), so I’ll call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously — folding to heavy aggression if I miss or extracting value if I connect. Commit total 300 chips (100 already posted + 200 call).

I called preflop for pot odds but on this flop (3♥ 8♥ 5♦) I have J♣7♦: no pair, no hearts, no meaningful backdoors. Villain opened preflop and his heads-up range will contain lots of hearts, Ax/Kx and medium pairs that will at least call a flop c-bet — I have very little fold equity and few blockers, and I’m OOP with a deeper effective stack. Betting here would bloat the pot with a pure bluff that’s likely to be called by better or fold only the weakest holdings. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve stack; plan is to fold to significant aggression (e.g. a bet > ~1/2 pot) but consider calling a very small probe to see a cheap turn, and only continue aggressively if I pair or pick up a relevant draw.

I raised pre-flop with J7o and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes 3h-8h-5d, which is quite dry but does include a heart draw. Minni checked to me. While I only have Jack-high with two overcards, this is a good board to continuation bet (c-bet). A bet of 300 (50% of the pot) puts pressure on Minni's missed high-card hands and can often take the pot down immediately. If called, I still have position and two overcards to potentially improve on the turn.

Call 300. I’m OOP with J♣7♦ on 3♥-8♥-5♦ and Flash (SB) c-bet half-pot 300 into the pot (pot = 900, to call = 300). Calling requires ~25% equity; J7 has roughly six clean outs (three J’s, three 7’s) and about a ~24% chance to pair by the river — borderline, but the price is cheap and heads-up the opener’s range will include lots of bluffs/heart floats and weaker holdings, so I can realize equity. I have no hearts or strong backdoors to lead with, so raising is unjustified; I’ll call to see the turn and keep the pot controlled. Plan: fold to a large turn shove or raise unless I pair or pick up a relevant draw; if the turn checks or is a small bet I’ll re-evaluate and only press aggression with real improvement.

I check. I’m OOP with J♣7♦ on 3♥-8♥-5♦-2♠ — no pair, no heart draw and only 6 clean outs (three J’s, three 7’s). On the turn I only have one card to come, so my chance to improve to a pair is 6/46 ≈ 13%. The pot is 1,200 and Flash’s heads-up range contains lots of calls and heart floats, so betting here would be a thin bluff with little fold equity and would bloat the pot out of position. Numerically, facing a bet B into P=1,200, the break-even equity is B/(P+2B); solving for my ~13% equity implies I can only profitably call bets of roughly ≤ ~210 chips. Plan: check to control the pot and either see a free river or induce a tiny probe that I can call; fold to meaningful turn aggression (>~200–250) given my low improvement odds. If I pair on the river I’ll pursue value; if I miss I’ll largely check-fold to aggression on the river.

The turn 2s is a complete blank and does not improve my Jack-high. Minni's call on the flop indicates she likely has a pair of eights, a smaller pair, or a draw (like hearts or 6-4). Since she checked to me again, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to see the river for free to potentially hit a Jack for showdown value without risking more chips on a pure bluff against an opponent who has shown a willingness to call.

I have J♣7♦ on 3♥-8♥-5♦-2♠-T♠ — no pair, no heart/meaningful draw, so essentially zero showdown value. Flash opened pre and c-bet the flop then checked the turn, which suggests a range that contains many one-pair hands and heart floats that will often check the turn and call a small river probe. To make a bluff profitable I'd need him to fold a fairly high percentage (e.g. >~33% to a half-pot bet), but I have no heart or ten blockers and am out of position, so my fold equity is limited. Betting would bloat the pot with a very weak hand and risk a call from any pair or even many worse high cards. I’ll check to keep the pot small — if he checks back I can win at showdown vs air, and if he bets I can fold and preserve a large stack for better spots.

On the river, the board is 3h-8h-5d-2s-Ts. I have Jack-high (Jd 7c). Minni called my flop continuation bet but checked the turn and river. While her check on the river could indicate weakness, her call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. Most of her range (any pair, Ace-high, or King-high) beats my Jack-high. Given that Minni has a much larger stack and has shown a tendency to be a sticky caller in previous hands, a bluff here is unlikely to be successful. Checking back is the most disciplined play to minimize losses and see if my Jack-high can somehow win at showdown against a missed lower draw, though I expect to lose the pot.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the 50 SB) I have position postflop and can open a very wide range. T♣3♦ is marginal but perfectly playable heads-up — it has a decent high-card and playability in position. Limping gives the BB cheap odds and surrenders initiative. I’ll raise to 300 total (standard 3xBB-ish sizing) to apply pressure, deny a free flop, and take the initiative. If Flash 3-bets large I will fold; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet or control the pot depending on the flop texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-5 offsuit. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and playing very aggressively, has raised to 3x the blind. While the price to call is relatively cheap (2:1 odds), 6-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a strong opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge her lead.

In a heads-up situation on the button, Jack-Eight offsuit is a standard hand to open for a raise. It has decent high-card value and playability. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position if Minni calls.

Facing a button (SB) open to 300 while I'm in the big blind with K♥6♣. Heads-up the button's opening range will be wide, and Kx is worth defending. Calling 200 more (300 total) is cheap relative to the pot and my deep stack, lets me see a flop and realize equity, and keeps the pot controllable out of position. A 3-bet with K6o here is too thin — I prefer to 3-bet only stronger hands or use blockers for a bluff. Postflop plan: continue with a top pair (or strong draws), otherwise proceed cautiously OOP and fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss.

Flop is 7♦ 9♣ 9♠ and I hold K♥6♣ — no pair, no real draws and effectively no fold equity OOP. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so their range contains many hands that now beat me (any 9, most pocket pairs, many 7x combos). Best play is pot control: check and concede the initiative. If Flash checks back I scoop the pot; if they C-bet I will fold to any reasonable-sized bet since I have almost no showdown equity and don’t want to bloating the pot OOP. Action: check (0).

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am leading out with a continuation bet on this paired flop of 7-9-9. This board texture is generally good for a C-bet because it is difficult for the defender to have hit a piece of it unless they have a 9 or a 7. While I only have Jack-high, a bet here allows me to take down the pot immediately if Minni has missed, which is a large portion of her range. If she calls, I still have overcards and can re-evaluate on the turn.

Flop: 7♦ 9♣ 9♠. I hold K♥6♣ — no pair, no meaningful draws, and I’m out of position. Flash’s button opening range is wide preflop, but on this paired 9-board they still have a lot of value (any 9, many pocket pairs, some 7x) and will also c-bet frequently. The pot is 900 and they bet 300, which gives me 25% equity to justify a call (300/(900+300)). I estimate my actual equity vs their betting range is well under that, and I have virtually no fold equity or good turn play OOP. Raising is flatly wrong with this junk. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads‑up on the button with As3c is a profitable, aggressive open. I’ve already posted SB50 and calling would only total 100, but opening raises gain fold equity and lets me play the hand with position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — that’s 300 total committed from me. If Flash calls he must put in 200 more and the pot will be ~600, which I can play post‑flop in position. If he 3‑bets large I’ll fold (A3o is often dominated by better Aces); I may defend vs small 3‑bets because of the Ace blocker and position. This sizing balances applying pressure with keeping the pot manageable against aggressive reg tendencies.

In this heads-up situation, Minni is opening for 3x from the button. My hand, 9h 6d, is a weak offsuit hand that does not play well out of position. While the price to call is 200 chips to win a pot of 400 (2:1 odds), 96o is significantly below the average defending range against a standard open. Given that Minni has a substantial chip lead and has been playing aggressively, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable spot to engage rather than bleeding chips with a marginal holding.

Ten-Eight suited is a solid hand in heads-up play, well above the average holding. I'll open for a standard 3x raise to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with a hand that has good playability.

I’m in the big blind heads-up with 9♠6♥ facing a standard open to 300. This is a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Calling 200 more to make it 300 gives decent pot odds (200 to win ~600 after my call → ~33% required equity) and with deep stacks I have implied odds if I hit. I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with this weak offsuit holding against a wide opener — better to see a flop and make a disciplined decision OOP. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to large aggression with no improvement, continue with pairs/draws or apply pressure when appropriate.

Flop is Jc-6c-Ts and I hold 9♠6♥ — middle pair with a weak kicker and no club blocker. Flash opened wide preflop and will c-bet a high frequency, so out of position I want to control the pot rather than build it with a marginal hand. Checking allows me to realize equity, call small-to-medium continuation bets, and fold to strong aggression. If Flash checks back I get to a cheap showdown; if he bets small I’ll call to extract value and protect against draws; I won’t lead or check-raise here because my pair is vulnerable and raising OOP is high variance. Stack sizes favor postflop play, but pot control is the prudent line.

I have middle pair (tens) on a coordinated board with a flush draw and straight possibilities. Minni's check allows me to bet for value and protection against her draws and overcards. A 50% pot bet is standard here to clarify where I stand and deny equity to hands like Ace-high or King-high that may be drawing.

I have 9♠6♥ on Jc‑6c‑Ts — a vulnerable middle pair with a weak kicker. Flash opened wide pre and c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot). Calling is the prudent line: I’m getting ~20% pot odds (300 to win ~1500) so this is a cheap call with decent showdown value vs a wide c‑bet range. Raising OOP with this marginal hand is high variance and would build a pot I don’t want unless I improve. Plan: check‑call to control the pot and see a turn; fold to large turn aggression if I don’t improve to two‑pair/trips and avoid bloating the pot versus club draws or J/Tx. Call.

Check. I have a vulnerable middle pair (6) with a weak kicker on Jc‑6c‑Ts‑Td. Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop, so his range includes many Tx/Jx, overcards and bluffs. Betting OOP with this marginal hand would bloat the pot and force tough decisions versus raises or better tens; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize equity. Plan: check and see the river; call small‑to‑medium river bets against his wide range, fold to very large aggression unless I improve, and re‑evaluate on a safe river.

The Ten on the turn has improved my hand to trips. Given the board texture (J-6-T-T), I am currently ahead of most of Minni's likely holdings, such as any Jack-x, smaller pairs, or draws. Minni's check on the turn indicates she is likely in check-call mode or has missed. I will bet 600 into the 1200 pot to extract value from a Jack or a flush draw and to protect my hand against potential river cards that could complete a straight or flush.

I have 9♠6♥ on Jc‑6c‑Ts‑Td — that’s two pair (Tens and Sixes). Flash is an aggressive, wide opener who c‑bet the flop and fired a small turn (600 into ~1800). That sizing and line represent a very wide range (bluffs, single‑pair Jx, some Tx, club draws) — my two‑pair is often ahead of that range but vulnerable to any Ten or Jack (or a turned/set). Calling 600 is the prudent line: it’s a cheap price (~20% pot odds) to continue with a made hand, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and lets me re‑evaluate the river. Raising OOP would bloating the pot unnecessarily and risk getting exploited by stronger Tens/Jacks. Plan: call the 600, see the river, then extract value or fold to strong action depending on the card and opponent sizing.

I have two pair (Tens and Sixes) on Jc‑6c‑Ts‑Td‑8d. Flash opened wide, c‑bet the flop and fired a small turn, then checked the river — a line that often contains Jx, single Tens played cautiously, missed club draws and bluffs. Very few hands beat me (trip Tens/quads or rare full houses like T+J or pocket sixes), while a lot of his range is worse and will call a reasonable river bet. Checking gives up thin value; betting extracts from Jx and missed draws. I want a size that gets called by worse hands but doesn’t invite huge raises from the small number of hands that beat me, so I choose a ~50% pot value bet. Bet 1,200 into the 2,400 pot. If he raises big I will fold (he likely has trips/full house); if he calls I win most of those worse showdown hands.

On the river, the 8d has improved my hand from trips to a full house (Tens full of Eights). The board is Jc-6c-Ts-Td-8d. Minni, who check-called both the flop and the turn, has now led into me for 1200 (half the pot). This lead often signifies a strong hand that wants to get paid, likely a Ten or potentially a Jack. Since I have a full house, I beat all of her possible trip combinations (A-T, K-T, Q-T, 9-T, 7-T, etc.) and any Jack-x. The only hands that beat me are J-T (Tens full of Jacks) and J-J (Jacks full of Tens), both of which are possible but less likely given her passive play on the earlier streets. I must raise for value to maximize my return on this monster hand. A raise to 3600 total is a strong value bet that her trips or even a strong Jack might find a reason to call.

River: I have two pair (Tens and Sixes) with 9♠6♥ on Jc‑6c‑Ts‑Td‑8d. Flash raised to 3,600 after my 1,200 bet; I must call 2,400 more. Calling is the correct line — the call costs 2,400 to win ~9,600 (≈25% break‑even equity). Flash’s line (small c‑bet and turn bet, then a big river raise) is polarized between trips/full houses/Jx value and missed club draws/bluffs. My two pair is ahead of many of his bluffs and weaker pairs but behind tens/jacks and the rare full house, so the price to call is +EV. Raising would bloat the pot and put me in uncomfortable spots versus the small number of hands that beat me; folding surrenders too much equity. I will call the raise to extract value from bluffs and worse hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with pocket deuces. This is a hand I want to play for value and initiative — I’ll raise rather than just completing. A min-raise to 200 total (i.e. add 150 more) accomplishes three things: 1) it takes the lead and denies the BB correct pot odds to see cheap flops with a wide range, 2) it builds the pot when I’m ahead of a lot of Flash’s calling range, and 3) it keeps my plan flexible with deep stacks (I can c-bet dry flops, set-mine if called, or fold to a large 3-bet). If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll play position and evaluate flop texture; if he 3-bets large I’ll concede most of the time. Overall this small-sized raise maximizes my EV here.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button. I am in the big blind with 4c 2c and have already posted 100. It costs me 100 more to call into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 4c 2c is a weak hand, suited cards have enough playability and raw equity in a heads-up match to justify a defend against a small open. I will call and see if I can connect with the flop.

On this flop of Ah-Ts-4h, I have bottom pair with a 4. Since Minni was the pre-flop raiser and this board contains an Ace and a Ten, it favors her range significantly. I should check to see her reaction. Leading into her would be unnecessary and likely to get raised or called by better hands. My plan is to check and potentially call a small continuation bet, but I will be cautious given the strength of an Ace-high board in her range.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position, so I should continuation-bet to try to take the pot down and deny equity to broad parts of Flash’s defense. The flop (4♥ A♥ T♠) contains a high Ace that hits a lot of Flash’s calling range, so I don’t want to overcommit with just pocket deuces — I’ll use a half‑pot c‑bet to apply pressure but keep the pot controllable. Betting 200 into 400 folds out many Kx/Qx and unpaired hands, gets value from some worse holdings, and leaves room to fold to a big check‑raise or to continue if he passively calls and the turn is safe. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll usually fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and proceed accordingly.

On a flop of Ah-Ts-4h, I have bottom pair with my 4c. Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While this board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Ax and Tx combinations), folding bottom pair immediately to a single half-pot bet in heads-up play is overly exploitable. I have enough equity against her bluffs and semi-bluffs (like heart draws or gutshots) to justify calling one street. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and her action.

Minni checked back on the turn after the Queen of Spades was dealt. This action often indicates pot control with a marginal hand (like a Ten or a weak Ace) or a draw that wants to see a free river card. My bottom pair of fours has very little value, but it does have some showdown potential against her complete misses. By checking, I take the free card offered and avoid inflating the pot with a weak hand while out of position.

Check. I C‑bet the flop and got called, and the Q♠ on the turn is a very unhelpful card for my pocket deuces — it strengthens a large chunk of Flash’s calling range (Qx, KQ, QTx, a fair number of A‑x and T‑x combos that still want to continue) and it adds backdoor/spade equity to some holdings. I have essentially no showdown value (only two outs to make a set on the river, ≈4.3%) and rather little fold equity if I fire — Flash has shown a tendency to call flop bets, so a turn bluff will usually be met with a call, which just bloats a pot I’m almost always behind in. With deep stacks I should keep the pot small, realize my tiny equity, and see the river before committing more chips. If the river brings a deuce I can value‑bet; if it’s a blank and Flash bets I’ll fold most of the time. So I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river.

The river card is the King of Spades, making the board Ah-Ts-Qs-Ks-4h. This board is extremely coordinated, with four cards to a straight (A-K-Q-T). Any Jack now has a straight. My pair of fours, which was bottom pair on the flop, is now essentially worthless for showdown value, as almost any hand Minni started with or any piece of this board beats me. Minni checked back on the turn, which often indicates a marginal made hand (like an Ace or a Ten) or a draw. Given the board texture, it is unlikely she will fold a better hand to a single bet, and I have no value. I will check and expect to fold if she bets, as my hand cannot beat anything she would reasonably bet for value or even most of her bluffs.

Check to showdown. I opened preflop and c-bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn. The river (4♥ A♥ T♠ Q♠ K♠) leaves my pocket deuces with almost no value — a huge portion of Flash’s calling range (A‑x, K‑x, Q‑x, T‑x and any J for the straight) beats me, and Flash’s passive line (call on flop, checks turn and river) means my fold equity is very low. Betting here would rarely fold better hands and would only get called by hands I still beat infrequently, so the +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot and take my showdown.

In a heads-up situation, T9s is a very strong hand on the button (small blind). It has great playability, straight and flush potential, and is well above the average range for opening. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Flash opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind with 6♦7♠. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). Heads‑up this hand has playable equity and straight/paired potential vs a wide opening range. With deep stacks and being out of position, I don’t want to 3‑bet OOP light — a small 3‑bet commits chips with a marginal holding. Calling gives good pot odds (~3:1 on the call), lets me realize equity, and see a flop to continue only with real improvement or strong draw. I’ll call to 300 and proceed cautiously post‑flop (fold to heavy aggression without significant improvement).

I have middle pair (7s) with a weak 6‑kicker on a K‑T‑7 board with two diamonds. Out of position heads‑up, Flash’s raising range includes lots of Kx/Tx and overcards that currently beat me, and my hand has limited improvement potential (only backdoor diamonds or a runner‑runner). Checking controls the pot with a marginal hand, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me see how Flash proceeds. Plan: check and call a small C‑bet to realize equity, but fold to significant aggression since my kicker is poor and I’m rarely ahead of a large bet.

I have middle pair (Tens) on a board with a King and several potential draws (diamonds, straight possibilities). Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from draws or weaker hands like middle/bottom pairs and to protect my hand against overcards on the turn. If I get raised, I will likely look to fold as my kicker is mediocre, but a bet here helps define where I stand in the hand.

I have a single pair (7s) with a weak 6‑kicker on K♥ T♦ 7♦. Flash's flop bet is small (~1/3 pot) so his range heads‑up is wide: Kx/TX, overcards, some pocket pairs, diamond draws and bluffs. Raising OOP with this marginal holding and poor kicker is thin value and bloats the pot against hands that dominate me. Calling gives ~3:1-ish pot odds, lets me realize equity, and keeps worse hands/bluffs in the pot. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn; if Flash applies heavy pressure on the turn (big raise/jam) I’ll fold unless I’ve improved to two pair/trips or picked up a clear draw. If I improve on the turn (7/6 or meaningful draw) I’ll proceed more aggressively.

Turn: I’ve improved — I now have middle pair (7s) plus a 4‑to‑a‑flush (6♦ with 7♦‑T♦‑4♦ on board). Flash checked the turn, which looks like either a pot‑control with Kx/Tx, missed overcards, or a pot‑control with a weaker diamond. With deep stacks and a small pot (1,200), this is a good spot to semi‑bluff: I can take the pot now or get called by worse and still have ~30%+ to improve (flush, 7, or a 6). Betting also builds the pot when I hit and gains fold equity vs many Kx/Tx and overcards that checked behind. I’ll make a constructive half‑pot bet (~600) — big enough to fold out pure air and some overcards, but small enough to preserve stack flexibility if called. Plan if raised: respect a large check‑raise (fold to heavy pressure unless the raise is small enough to justify calling with my draw); if called, evaluate the river and look to extract when I hit or use fold equity again vs weak showdowns.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Nine kicker. The turn brought the Four of Diamonds, completing a possible diamond flush. Minni has decided to 'donk lead' into me for 600 after calling my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. This lead on a board-completing card often signifies either a completed flush, a slow-played King, or a semi-bluff with a high diamond. Given that I do not hold a diamond in my hand (Ts9s), I have no redraw to a flush and my pair is vulnerable to many river cards. While the pot odds are 3:1, calling here likely commits me to a difficult decision on the river if she bets again. Against an opponent who has shown they can play aggressively and value bet effectively, folding middle pair on a three-flush, one-overcard board to a lead is the safest way to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 7♣8♥ — a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability in position. Limping surrenders initiative and allows the big blind to see flops cheaply; I want to take the lead and leverage position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB): big enough to generate fold equity and discourage free cards, but small enough to keep pots manageable with a speculative hand. Plan: if Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll use position to C‑bet favorable textures and realize equity; if he 3‑bets large I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack (or re-evaluate versus a small 3‑bet).

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 5c, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although we are heads-up and I have a large stack, 7-5 offsuit is significantly behind Minni's opening range and plays poorly out of position. I've observed Minni being aggressive post-flop in previous hands.
