Given the current stack depths (approx 180 big blinds), there is no need to defend such a weak hand and risk being outplayed on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 7-4 offsuit. While heads-up play often permits opening a wide range of hands to take advantage of position post-flop, 7-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. Given Minni's recent aggression and willingness to defend the Big Blind or lead into me, I will opt for a fold here to preserve my stack and wait for a better starting hand rather than trying to steal the blinds with a very weak holding.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with deep stacks. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to see a flop — calling is cheap and gives excellent pot odds (50 to win ~200 => ~20% break-even). 8♠4♠ is a weak but suited hand that has real playability in position (can hit pairs, spade draws, or use position to bluff). Raising to 200 is a thin steal with a hand that plays poorly multi-way or vs a 3-bet; I prefer to keep the pot small and realize equity. Plan: see the flop in position, proceed aggressively only if I connect (pair/flush/strong draw), otherwise pot-control and fold to big aggression.

In Hand #89, Minni has limped in from the small blind/button. Queen-Jack offsuit (Qs Jd) is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. While raising to take control of the pot is a viable option, checking allows me to see a flop for free and keep the pot small while out of position. Given the stack depths and recent aggressive play from Minni, I will check to see the flop and evaluate the strength of my hand thereafter.

In Hand #89, I have hit top pair with a Queen kicker on a relatively dry Jack-high board. Since this was a limped pot pre-flop, Minni's range is extremely wide. By checking here, I allow Minni the opportunity to bluff or bet with weaker hands that might fold to a lead. My hand is strong enough to comfortably check-call any standard bet on this flop while keeping the pot controlled and deceptive.

I flopped a pair of fours (4s) with a weak kicker and I hold both spades, so I’m four‑to a spade flush (9 outs) — decent equity and a real backdoor draw. Heads‑up in position vs a checked BB, Flash’s checking range is wide and contains many hands I’m ahead of or have good equity against. I want to extract value from worse jacks/overcards and charge any backdoor/paired draws while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. A half‑pot c‑bet (~100 into 200) accomplishes that: gets value, folds out some equity, and leaves room to fold or continue depending on a check‑raise. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises large I’ll be cautious (likely folding to huge aggression, calling small raises with our pair+draw).

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a relatively dry Jack-high board. Since Minni limped pre-flop, her range is very wide. Her small 100-chip bet into the 200-chip pot looks like a standard continuation or feeler bet. By calling rather than raising, I maintain the deception of my hand strength and allow Minni to continue bluffing or betting with inferior hands on the turn. My hand is far too strong to fold, and a raise might isolate me against only the few hands that beat me (like 44, 22, or J2/J4).

I am first to act on the turn. The King of Hearts is an overcard to my pair of Jacks. Since Minni limped pre-flop and then bet the flop, her range is quite wide, but the King certainly hits many of the hands she might play this way (like Kx). Checking is the most prudent move here to control the pot size and see how she responds to the overcard. If I bet, I risk being raised or having to fold to a bluff, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or catch her in a bluff if she doesn't actually have the King.

I have second pair (4♠) with a weak 8‑kicker plus a spade flush draw (9 outs). The K♥ on the turn is a big overcard that improves a sizable portion of Flash’s calling range (Kx) and makes me vulnerable to check‑raises or turn/river value bets. He checked the turn after calling my small flop bet — his range can be both pot‑control (Kx) and missed/weak hands, but given he called the flop he’s sticky enough that a turn bluff is thin. With deep stacks and only medium equity, I prefer pot control and to realize my equity rather than bluff off chips. I’ll check back, see the river, and then decide: call small river bets with pair+draw, fold to large river aggression, or bluff/value bet appropriately if the river changes the texture.

On the river, I have second pair with a strong Queen kicker. The turn went check-check after Minni bet the flop, which suggests she likely doesn't have a King. If she had a strong King, she likely would have bet the turn for value or protection. By checking the river, I am playing my hand as a bluff-catcher. I am giving Minni the opportunity to bluff with any missed straight draws or lower-equity hands she might have held. If I bet, she is likely to fold those air hands and only call me with a King or a better Jack, making a check the most profitable play to either reach a cheap showdown or induce a mistake.

I’ve rivered a spade flush: my 8♠4♠ with the board 2♠-4♥-J♠-K♥-7♠ gives me a J‑high spade flush. Only hands that beat me are A♠x (A‑high flush) which are relatively rare. Flash’s line — called my small flop raise, then checked turn and river — looks like medium strength (Jx, Kx, single pairs, missed draws) rather than a super‑polarized value range. With deep stacks and a modest pot (400), I want to extract value from those worse hands while keeping the pot manageable against the small chance of an A♠. A half‑pot value bet (200) is the right play: it gets called by Jx/Kx and other sticky pairs/draws, folds pure air, and avoids bloating the pot vs the rare dominate combo. If he jams or raises very large I’ll heavily weight A♠ and fold; I’ll call small‑to‑moderate raises.

In this limped pot, the action has been relatively passive since the turn. Minni's check on the turn (a King) suggests she likely doesn't have a King in her hand. My pair of Jacks (second pair) remains quite strong in a heads-up situation where the pot was not raised pre-flop. Minni is betting 200 into a 400-chip pot on the river, giving me 3-to-1 odds to call. Given the way the hand played out, it's very possible she is betting a weaker Jack, a 7 that hit the river, or simply taking a stab at the pot with a missed draw. My hand is a very strong bluff-catcher in this specific sequence, and the pot odds make a call the most mathematically sound decision.

I am holding 6-deuce offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. While a wide opening range is generally recommended in heads-up play, 62o is at the very bottom of the range and is typically a fold even in small blind versus big blind scenarios. Given Minni's active defense in previous hands, I'll let this one go and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the SB with a small pocket pair (4h4d). This is a hand I want to play for value/set-mining but I also want initiative against a wide BB defending range. The pot is 150 and it's only 50 to complete, so I raise to 300 (≈3bb) — a standard heads-up open — to gain fold equity, isolate the BB, and keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. If called I'll c-bet appropriate flops and look to set-mine; I'll fold to a large shove/3-bet but may call a small-to-medium 3-bet given implied odds.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4s 6h, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3x from the Button. While the pot odds are 2:1, calling with such a low, non-connected, offsuit hand out of position is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am holding 8-5 offsuit in the small blind. While this is heads-up play where ranges are wider, 8-5 offsuit is still in the bottom tier of starting hands. Given Minni's recent aggressive play and the fact that I have a healthy stack of over 170 big blinds, there is no need to commit chips to a marginal or sub-par hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 4h7s — a weak offsuit hand. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, giving great pot odds and I keep the positional advantage post‑flop. Raising to 200 risks getting 3‑bet by a player who defends a lot and puts me in a tough spot with a marginal hand. I’ll complete the blind to 100, see the flop, and proceed only if I hit a pair or meaningful draw; otherwise keep the pot small and fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 2c. Minni, on the button, has limped into the pot. While suited queens have some value in heads-up play, Q2 is a relatively weak hand to build a large pot with out of position. Checking here allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) on a very low and connected board (2-3-4). While I have a piece of the board, it's vulnerable to many overcards and straight possibilities. Since I am out of position and the pot is small after a pre-flop limp, I will check to see how the opponent reacts and try to get to a cheap showdown or improve my hand.

Flop is 2s-3d-4s and I have 4h7s — top pair but a weak 7 kicker. Heads-up and in position with the BB checking, this is a clear spot to take the initiative and get value/protect, but I don’t want to build a big pot with a marginal kicker. A small value/protection bet keeps the pot manageable, charges overcards and single-card draws, and folds out pure air. I’ll lead for ~½ pot to extract from worse hands and retain fold equity. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate (likely a strong hand like two pair/set/connected 5x) and proceed cautiously or fold to big aggression; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

I have bottom pair on a very low, connected board. Minni's limp pre-flop and small bet on the flop could represent a range of hands, including overcards or small draws. With 3:1 pot odds, calling a single small bet to see the turn is a standard defensive play with a pair in heads-up, despite being out of position.

On a board of 2-3-4-5, any Ace or Six completes a straight. I have bottom pair (deuces), which is incredibly vulnerable. Since I am first to act on the turn, I will check to see how Minni responds. Betting into a board with a four-straight when I only have bottom pair is risky, as it's very easy for a limp-heavy range like Minni's to contain an Ace or even a Six.

I have 4h7s on 2s-3d-4s-5c — top pair but a very weak kicker and the turn 5 makes the board highly dangerous (any 5 now beats me, any 6 in hand just completed a straight, plus sets/trips/pocket 4s exist). Flash called my half‑pot flop stab and then checked the turn; that can be a trap but is also consistent with many hands that either improved or are float/call-down hands. Betting now would bloat a pot I’m often behind in; I want to control pot size and keep the option to fold to big aggression. I’ll check back, see how Flash reacts on the river, and call small-to-medium bets but fold to a large overbet.

The board shows a four-card straight (2-3-4-5) and a possible backdoor flush (spades). I hold the lowest possible pair (Deuces). Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a straight, but she could easily have a better pair or have connected with the Nine on the river. There is no value in betting because I will only be called by better hands. I will check and try to reach showdown cheaply.

Board is 2s-3d-4s-5c-9s and I hold 4h7s — I have a single pair of 4s with a very weak 7 kicker. I led the flop for protection and got a call, then both of us checked the turn; Flash checking the river too is consistent with a wide range: weak pairs, a 9 that just improved, or a slow-played stronger hand (trips/pocket 4s or even a turned straight from a 6). The river 9 actually makes many hands that beat me and there’s little fold equity left. Betting here would bloat a pot I’m often behind in and commit chips with a marginal kicker. Pot control is the priority: check back to avoid getting raised off my hand and only call small river bets; fold to large overbets.

In a heads-up situation from the button (SB), 9s 5s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. It has decent playability and the potential to make flushes or straights. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the hand early.

I’m in the big blind with 9♣T♥ facing a button raise to 300. Stacks are deep (22800 vs 16800) but I’m out of position, so 3-betting OOP with a medium connected offsuit hand is suboptimal. 9T has decent equity vs a wide open range and the cost to continue is only 200 more, so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: proceed on flops that give pairs or strong draws; be willing to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I have top pair (T♥9♣) on a Ts 2h 8h flop. Opponent opened wide from the button, so his calling/raising range contains lots of worse tens, small pairs and heart draws. Betting about half-pot (300 into 600) achieves value vs worse tens/pairs, charges heart and straight draws, and prevents giving free cards while keeping the pot manageable deep-stacked. Plan: if he calls, proceed cautiously on turn; if he check-raises big I’ll heavily discount my hand and likely fold/re-evaluate rather than commit the stack.

On a board of Ts 2h 8h, my hand (9s 5s) has very little current value and limited potential. Minni's lead (donk bet) for half the pot into the pre-flop raiser suggests she has either hit a piece of the board (like a Ten or an Eight) or is semi-bluffing with a heart draw. While I am in position, I have no direct straight or flush draws, only weak backdoor possibilities. Floating with 9-high against an aggressive opponent like Minni is likely to lead to difficult decisions on later streets. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠4♦ (As4d) is a standard open-raise spot. Pot is 150 and calling is only 50, but I want to take the initiative and play postflop in position. A raise applies pressure to the big blind, narrows their range, and gives me fold equity and better postflop control. Min-raise to 200 is too small and invites loose defenses; 300 (3BB) is a standard, balanced open size. With deep stacks (23k vs 16.7k) I can realize equity and extract value when I hit an ace, and I can check/control on dangerous boards. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and play the hand in position.

In heads-up play, Ace-Seven is a strong hand well above the average opening range of an aggressive opponent like Minni. While I have the option to three-bet to take control of the hand, calling is a solid play that keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position. It allows me to see a flop and evaluate, as A7o plays reasonably well against a wide button range.

On a flop of Queen-Ten-Three, my Ace-high has limited value. This board texture heavily favors a button raising range, which often includes many Queens, Tens, and straight draws (like KJ or J9). Since I am out of position and did not connect with the board, the most standard and disciplined play is to check and see how Minni proceeds. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this coordinated board would be risky without at least a pair or a strong draw.

Heads-up on the button with position and initiative after my preflop raise. Flop Q♦ T♥ 3♦ — I have A♠4♦: ace-high and a backdoor diamond (some blocker value to Ax combos). Flash checked, so a standard continuation bet is appropriate to fold out weak pairs and overcards, extract value from worse Qx/Tx/draws, and maintain initiative. I'll make a pot-pressure bet of ~55–60% to balance fold equity and leave room to continue or fold to a big raise on later streets.

On a flop of Queen-Ten-Three, my Ace-high has very little equity. This specific board texture (two Broadway cards and a flush draw) is highly favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Queens, Tens, and strong draws like King-Jack or diamonds. While Ace-high can sometimes be the best hand in heads-up play, being out of position without any meaningful draw (no flush or straight potential) makes a call here unprofitable. Calling would likely lead to a difficult decision on the turn if Minni continues her aggression, so folding now is the most disciplined way to preserve my stack.

94o is one of the weakest starting hands. While it is heads-up and I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand, Minni has been playing aggressively and frequently defending the blinds. There is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand when my stack is over 160 big blinds; I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q9o — this is a clear open-raise spot. I want to seize the initiative, apply fold equity and use position postflop. A 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) is standard here: bigger than the tiny min-raise so we don’t give the BB great odds to chase, but small enough to keep weaker hands guessing and preserve postflop maneuverability with deep stacks. If Flash folds we take the blinds; if he calls we play position with a queen-blocker and reasonable equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 4d. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2-to-1 odds. While 9-4 suited is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation, suited hands have decent playability and I have enough pot odds to defend my blind and see a flop. I will call the raise to see if I can improve on the flop without over-committing my stack.

I have completely missed the flop with 9-high and no real draws. On this board of 6h-8h-3c, Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, likely has the range advantage or at least better high-card value. I will check to see how she responds, intending to fold to almost any bet since I lack equity.

I’m the preflop aggressor in heads‑up so I have a range advantage and should c‑bet most flops — this one (6h 8h 3c) is relatively dry despite two hearts. I have Qh9c: no pair but two overcards and a useful queen‑heart blocker plus a backdoor heart draw. A half‑pot continuation bet applies pressure to unpaired hands and charges draws while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot (300). If Flash folds I take it; if he calls I’ll use position to evaluate the turn (call small/medium bets with my overcards and blocker, value/raise if I pair, fold to a large check‑raise given my current showdown weakness).

I have completely missed this flop with my 9-high hand, and I have no draws or significant backdoor potential on this board. Minni's continuation bet is standard, and while the price to call is relatively small (300 into 900), continuing with such low equity would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. I will follow the established pattern of raising to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Call 300 total. Heads-up the button’s open to 300 is very wide and J♦6♦ has enough playability (backdoor diamond flush, straight potential, occasional pair) to see a flop for a small additional investment. With deep stacks and being out of position postflop, I don’t want to 3-bet light and inflate the pot; calling keeps things controllable and lets me continue only when I hit or get a good draw.

Check to control the pot. On A♦ A♠ 5♠ with J♦6♦ I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond draw; betting OOP as the caller will bloat a pot I can't play profitably against a wide but often-Ax-heavy opening range. I want to see what the button does: call small continuation bets to float/bluff the turn when appropriate, fold to large donk-bets, and re-evaluate on the turn. Pot control and position-aware float lines maximize my stack preservation and postflop options here.

I have flopped trip Aces with a Queen kicker, which is a very strong hand. Since Minni checked to me, I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from any hands Minni might be holding, such as a 5, a pocket pair, or a weaker Ace.

Call 300. Heads-up the button’s open and small c‑bet here is wide; J♦6♦ has enough playability to float (backdoor diamond potential, turn pair outs and good pot odds). The bet is small into ~900 (calling 300 to win ~1200 needs ~20% equity), so calling OOP keeps the pot controlled and preserves stack depth for later streets. I won’t check‑raise or shove with no pair — plan is to evaluate the turn: continue (raise or call) if I pick up a diamond or a pair/runner‑runner improvement or if the turn gives a good bluffing opportunity; fold to heavy pressure on the turn if I miss and he continues with strength.

Check. I’m out of position with J♦6♦ on 5♠ A♦ A♠ K♣ and I have no pair and no meaningful draw (my backdoor diamond route is dead). Flash has been opening wide and c-betting small, but this board (two aces) strongly favors hands with an Ace or a King — hands that will call or raise a turn bet. Betting or raising OOP here would bloat a pot I’m almost always behind in and get called by better hands; checking controls the pot, keeps my stack intact, and lets me reevaluate on the river. Plan: check to see the river; fold to significant pressure if I miss, consider a light river bluff only if the texture and read justify it (e.g. a favorable diamond or a blank paired board and a credible story).

I have trip Aces with a strong Queen kicker. The King on the turn is a good card to bet on because it might have improved Minni's hand if she was holding a King, making her more likely to call. I'll bet 800 to continue building the pot and extract value from weaker Aces, Kings, or pocket pairs.

Fold. On 5♠ A♦ A♠ K♣ with J♦6♦ I have no pair and my backdoor diamond route is dead after the turn — only 6 outs (3 J + 3 6) to hit a pair on the river, ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Flash’s bet of 800 into a 2000 pot means I need ~28.6% equity to make a pure call (800 to win 2800), so a call is a clear -EV even before range considerations. Flash’s line (open pre, small flop c‑bet, turn bet) is easily consistent with Ax/Kx value hands on this paired ace board; while some bluffs exist, the sizing and line don’t give me enough credit to profitably call as a bluff‑catcher. I’ll preserve my 23k+ stack for better spots and fold rather than chase long odds on a single river card.

Heads-up on the button (also the small blind) with Q♠6♣ — a marginal hand but very playable in position. I want to be aggressive and take the initiative rather than just complete. A standard 3xBB open to 300 gives good fold equity and defines my range; if I get a 3-bet I can fold and preserve chips, if called I can C-bet many flops from position and play postflop with initiative. Raise to 300.
In a heads-up situation, J8o is a reasonable hand to defend the big blind with against a standard 3x button raise. The pot odds are 2:1, which justifies seeing a flop despite being out of position.

On a dry, Ace-high board like 3c-7h-As, I have no connectivity or showdown value with J-high. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will evaluate my next move based on Minni's action, but likely I will fold to any significant bet as I have very little equity here.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position; opponent checked the flop on A♠3♣7♥. Standard heads-up play is to continuation-bet to take the initiative and fold out many weaker hands (Kx, Qx without spade, small pairs, floats). My Q♠6♣ has little showdown value but has a backdoor club and can barrel turn cards; I don’t want to give a free card. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (300) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. If I face a check-raise I’ll fold; if I’m called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

On this Ace-high board, my Jack-high hand has no equity or draws. Minni's continuation bet is standard for an aggressor on this texture, and I have nothing to continue with. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In heads-up play, 84 offsuit is near the bottom of the starting hand range. While the button (small blind) should generally play a wide range of hands, 84o lacks playability and is often a candidate for a fold to preserve chips for better spots, especially against an opponent like Minni who has been active and has a stack lead.

Heads-up on the button with 3♠6♠ — a weak but playable suited hand. In heads-up play I want initiative and to leverage fold equity rather than limp and give the BB the lead. Pot is 150 and the cost to call is only 50, but calling is passive and invites aggression from Flash who defends fairly wide. I’ll open to 300 (a standard ~3x BB heads-up sizing) to isolate, gain fold equity, and keep the pot a manageable size with deep stacks (23,550 vs 16,300). Plan: if called I’ll C-bet on dry, A/K/T-type flops and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated or flush/straighty boards; call small 3-bets to realize equity, fold to very large 3-bets depending on sizing and reads. Raising here maximizes long-term EV by applying pressure and keeping position postflop.

I am holding 3c 5d, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call Minni's raise from the Big Blind, this hand has extremely poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Given that we are heads-up and I have plenty of chips remaining, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

In heads-up play, I am on the Button (Small Blind), which means I will have the advantage of being in position for all subsequent betting rounds. While Jack-Six offsuit is a weak hand, folding the button in heads-up is generally considered too tight, as I should be playing roughly 70-80% of my hands. Minni has been active and aggressive, but playing in position allows me to control the pot size and capitalize on her checks. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative; if she re-raises, I can easily fold, but if she calls, I will play the hand post-flop with the benefit of position.

Heads-up in the big blind with K♠Q♠ — one of the best non-pair hands heads-up. Flash opened to 300 from the button/SB; calling keeps us out of position and concedes initiative. I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity to isolate weaker Kx/Qx, suited broadways and medium pairs, while building a pot we can play postflop with good equity. With deep stacks (~150–160bb effective) a standard 3-bet sizing of ~3–4x the open is appropriate, so I’ll raise to 1200 total. If he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a shove or very large 4-bet, but continue against smaller 4-bets depending on pot odds/read).

I am holding Jack-Six offsuit, which is a very weak hand to defend against a large 3-bet. Minni has increased the bet to 1200, which is four times my original raise. While I would have the advantage of being in position post-flop, J6o lacks the equity and playability to justify calling 900 more chips against a range that likely consists of better high cards, pairs, or suited connectors. Preserving my stack of 160 big blinds is more important than fighting for this pot with a sub-par hand.

Heads-up on the button with T9s — a strong, suited connector that plays very well postflop and benefits from playing in position. I should not limp here: raising takes the initiative, builds the pot when I'm likely ahead of the BB's wide defending range, and generates fold equity. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my table image, pressures the opponent, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Flash calls I’ll play postflop aggressively on favorable textures and be cautious facing a large 3-bet; if he folds I win the pot outright.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has opened to 3x the big blind (300), and while I am getting 3:1 odds to call, playing such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive opponent is generally a losing proposition in the long run. I have been folding quite a bit recently, but I prefer to wait for a hand with better playability or equity realization to mount a defense or a counter-attack.

In a heads-up situation, 9-5 suited is a playable hand from the button. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand with the advantage of position for the rest of the street.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 10♣4♣. Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide, and I already have 100 committed; calling 200 more (300 total) gives good pot odds (~3:1) and lets me realize equity with backdoor club/straight possibilities. I don’t want to 3‑bet with a weak, non-premium hand out of position — I’d have poor fold equity and would be in difficult postflop spots. Plan: call to see the flop; continue with any club or a T/paired board, but fold to heavy aggression when completely missed.

I have T♣4♣ on a K♥–9♥–7♦ rainbow flop. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw (only a runner‑runner straight), and I’m out of position versus a preflop raiser whose heads‑up range contains a lot of Kx/broadways that dominate my Ten. I’m already committed 300 preflop and the pot is 600, so there’s no reason to lead with this marginal hand — that would commit chips in a bad spot. I’ll check to control the pot and see one more street. Plan after this check: if Flash checks back I can continue to the turn and realize showdown equity; if he bets small (roughly ≤½ pot ≈ 300) I may call to see a turn that could give me a Ten or a favorable bluffing card; if he bets big (>~½–2/3 pot) I’ll fold because I’m behind most of his range and don’t have the equity to continue.

I have flopped middle pair with my Nine. The board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and some straight possibilities. Since Minni checked, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from draws or weaker hands and to protect my current lead. If Minni raises, I will have to re-evaluate, but a bet here is standard to maintain initiative in position.

Heads‑up vs the button who opened wide, I have T♣4♣ on K♥–9♥–7♦ — no pair, only backdoor club/runner‑runner straight outs, and I’m dominated by most Kx/9x/broadway hands. I’m already committed 300 preflop and Flash’s flop bet is small (300 into 900). Calling 300 gets the pot to 1200 and requires ~25% equity to justify a pure call; my raw equity to improve on the turn is low, but against a wide heads‑up c‑bet range this price is reasonable to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and see a turn that can change the texture (a Ten or clubs). Plan: call the 300 to see the turn; fold to significant raise or large turn continuation if I don’t improve, but continue (or possibly bluffcatch) if villain checks or bets small on the turn.

I check. I have T♣4♣ on K♥–9♥–7♦–A♦ — no pair and effectively only 6 clean outs (three tens + three fours) to improve on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13% equity). The pot is 1,200 and both stacks are deep, so betting here would turn a marginal bluff into a large, awkward OOP pot against a wide opener who c‑bet the flop. Given my low equity and lack of fold‑equity, I should control the pot and see a river card. If Flash checks back I get to realize my ~13% chance; if he bets the turn I will fold to any meaningful bet. (Quick math: to justify a pure call with 13% equity I can only call about ≤ ~210 chips into this pot; his flop bet was 300, so most turn bets will be too large to call.) So I check and plan to fold to most turn bets, call only an extremely small probe, or proceed to the river if checked to.

On the turn, an Ace has appeared. This is a significant overcard to my pair of Nines and the King already on the board. While the Ace is generally a good card for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, I currently hold middle pair (Nines) with some showdown value. Minni's check on the turn after calling the flop bet suggests she might be drawing or holding a marginal hand. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to see the river for free, preserving my stack while still having a chance to win at showdown if the river is safe.

I have T♣4♣ on 9♥‑7♦‑K♥‑A♦‑A♥ — I missed everything and the board now pairs Aces. Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then checked the Ace on the turn, which leaves him with a wide range (Kx, Ax, some broadways and bluffs). I have essentially no showdown value and very little credible fold equity on the river: a bluff here will often be called by Kx/Qx/broadways and beaten by any A or K. With the pot only 1,200 and both stacks deep, betting is just needlessly risky. Best line is to check and take showdown (or fold to a river bet if he now shows strength). Action: check to avoid committing chips with no pair or realistic outs.

The board has paired the Ace on the river, making the final board 9-7-K-A-A. I have two pair, Aces and Nines. While my hand has some showdown value, the board is quite dangerous with both a King and two Aces present. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. There is little value in betting here, as it's unlikely a weaker hand than a Nine (like a Seven) will call, and any King or Ace has me soundly beaten. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and realize my equity without risking a check-raise from a potentially trapped monster hand.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 3s and deep stacks. This is a very playable hand in position: I want to take the initiative, build the pot a bit, and avoid giving the BB cheap odds to see a flop with dominated hands or big-card overcards. Table tendency shows many standard opens to 300, so I’ll raise to 300 (a standard 3x-style open rather than the min 200) to get fold equity and keep postflop SPR manageable for set-mining or protection. Plan: c-bet small on dry boards, control size or check/back if I miss and face heavy resistance, and extract/size up if I flop a set.

In heads-up play, Queen-Eight suited (Q8h) is a strong hand that performs well above average. Minni's 3x raise is a standard opening move. Given the 2:1 pot odds on the call and the playability of suited connectors/one-gappers, calling to see a flop is the most consistent play. I have a deep stack (164 BB), so I can afford to play post-flop and look for favorable boards where my flush or straight potential can materialize.

On this flop of Kc-6c-5h, I have a back-door heart flush draw and two overcards to the middle of the board (though behind the King). Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, is likely to continuation bet many of her hands. However, she has checked. This check often indicates either a moderate strength hand (like a weak King or a mid-pair) that wants to control the pot size, or a complete whiff. With my Queen-high and back-door equity, checking back is the most prudent play to see a free turn card and potentially improve or evaluate Minni's next action.

Heads-up on a dry K-6-5 board with pocket 3s (an underpair). My preflop plan was to c-bet small on dry flops to take the initiative and fold out overcards while keeping the pot controllable. Flash checked, so a ~1/3 pot continuation bet will accomplish that: deny equity to overcards, get value from any weaker pairs or floats, and keep SPR manageable. If Flash raises big I can fold and preserve my stack; if he just calls I retain position and can re-evaluate on the turn.

Minni has made a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet on a King-high board. I am holding Queen-high with a backdoor heart flush draw and two overcards to the middle of the board. Given the very favorable pot odds of 4:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1000), folding would be too tight. I have enough equity with my backdoor draws and potential overcard value to see a turn card, where I can re-evaluate based on the board development and Minni's subsequent action.

On the turn, the board is 6c-5h-Kh-7c. I am holding Qh-8h, which gives me a flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (5-6-7-8). With 15 potential outs (9 hearts, plus three 4s and three 9s that are not hearts), I have significant equity. In this heads-up structure where I act first post-flop, checking is a sound strategy with a strong draw. This allows me to see how Minni proceeds. If she bets, I have an easy call with my equity; if she checks, I get a free card to potentially hit one of my many outs on the river.

I have pocket 3s on K‑6‑5‑7 with two clubs on board. Pot is 1,000 and effective stacks are deep (~16k). My hand is an underpair that cannot improve to trips (I hold both treys), so my showdown equity is very poor versus a flop‑call range (Kx, 6x/5x, mid pocket pairs, sticky floats). Flash’s flop call then turn check looks like pot‑control or a marginal made hand — not always an easy fold. Betting the turn as a pure bluff is unlikely to get consistent folds from a caller-heavy opponent and needlessly builds a big pot when I have almost no way to improve. I’ll check to control the pot size, keep the option to fold to significant aggression, and re-evaluate the river (look for missed cards or a safe spot to bluff if he shows clear weakness).

I rivered a pair of Queens on a King-high board. Minni's check on the turn is a significant signal; it suggests she likely doesn't have a King, a set, or a completed straight (48 or 89), as she would typically bet those for value on such a connected board. By checking the river, I can induce a bluff from her missed draws or take a cheap showdown against her marginal pairs. Leading out might force a fold from the hands I beat and only get called by hands that have me beat (like a slow-played King). Therefore, checking is the most effective way to realize my hand's value.

Heads‑up on the river with 3♣3♠ on 6♣ 5♥ K♥ 7♣ Q♠. I checked the turn to control pot size and I haven’t improved; my hand is an underpair with almost no showdown value vs a caller-heavy flop range. Flash called my small flop bet and then checked the turn — that pattern points to a marginal made hand (Kx, 6x/5x, mid pair) or a missed draw that’s unlikely to fold to a river bluff. A bet here would have very limited fold equity and would mostly get called by better hands, while risking a meaningful portion of a ~23k stack to win a 1k pot. I’ll check back, keep the pot small, and go to showdown rather than bluff into a calling opponent.

In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong starting hand. Being on the button, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and see how Minni reacts. A6o is well ahead of an average random hand.

Facing a button open to 300 with pot 400 and 200 to call. 2♣5♥ is very weak, but heads-up I defend wide — the caller only needs ~33% equity and Flash’s opening range is likely quite broad. 3-betting with junk is unnecessary and folding too often surrenders value. I’ll call 200 more (300 total) to see a flop and proceed cautiously out of position: fold to strong continuation bets on dry boards, continue with any pair, backdoor draws, or good opportunities to bluff on later streets.

Check. I defended a very wide range preflop heads‑up, but on A♠‑9♠‑8♥ with 2♣5♥ I have nothing — no pair and no realistic draw. The Ace heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range, who will c‑bet a large percentage here; out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with air or attempt a thin bluff. Best line is to check and fold to meaningful aggression (only consider calling a tiny probe bet), preserve stack depth for better spots, and re‑evaluate on the turn if I pick up a pair or clear actionable draw.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a coordinated board of 9s-8h-As. While my kicker (6) is weak, top pair is a premium hand in heads-up play. Since Minni checked, I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot) to extract value from smaller pairs (9s or 8s), gutshot straight draws (like J-10 or 7-6), and flush draws.

Out of position with 2♣5♥ on A♠‑9♠‑8♥ I have nothing — no pair, no spade, no realistic backdoor. Flash’s flop bet of 300 into a 900 pot forces a 300 call to win 1,200 (≈25% pot odds), but his continuation range heads‑up is heavy in Aces, 9s, 8s and high cards so my actual equity is well below what’s required. The bet is a standard low‑risk probe and calling OOP with pure air just sets me up for larger streets and tough decisions. With deep stacks and plenty of better spots to invest in, the correct, risk‑averse line is to fold and preserve chips.

Heads-up on the button with 6♠T♠ — suited, has decent postflop playability and benefits a lot from having position. The BB (Flash) has shown a wide defending/3-betting frequency, so opening frequently from the button is profitable. I’ll take the initiative with a standard min-open to 2x BB (200) to build the pot with position, apply pressure to the blind, and keep pot size manageable. If he 3-bets I’ll reassess based on size (can call small-to-medium 3-bets to play postflop, fold to very large 3-bets/4-bets without equity).

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit (Ah 9d) in the big blind against a button min-raise from Minni. In heads-up play, A9o is a very strong hand. While calling is an option to keep the pot small, I prefer to three-bet for value and to take the initiative. Minni has been opening wide, and a raise to 600 (3x her open) allows me to extract value from weaker aces and king-high hands while potentially ending the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up on the button with 6♠T♠ vs a small 3-bet to 600. This is a deep-stacked spot and Flash has shown a fairly wide 3-betting/defending frequency, so I want to see a flop and use position. Calling 400 more (600 total committed) gives me ~33% pot odds (400/(800+400)) and T6s has reasonable equity plus good postflop playability. SPR will be very deep (~18), so calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity and apply pressure on later streets. 4-betting or raising here would be too thin with a marginal hand, so I’ll call and reassess on the flop.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Kings) with a Nine kicker. Since Minni (the Small Blind) has checked to me on this very static Ace-King-King board, I am choosing to check behind. While my hand is strong, this board texture heavily interacts with a 3-bet calling range which often includes King-high hands (K-Q, K-J) and better Aces. By checking, I control the pot size and prevent myself from being blown off the hand by a check-raise, while also allowing Minni to potentially bluff on later streets with weaker hands.

Flop is K♠ K♣ A♣ after I cold-called a 3-bet. Flash’s preflop 3-bet skews his range toward Kx/AK/AA/QQ+ but he 3-bets somewhat wide, so there are some bluffs — however this specific board hits his value range very hard. I have T♠6♠: no pair, very little showdown value and only a runner‑runner spade flush (requires two spades on turn+river, ~very small equity). Betting here would be a pure bluff with thin fold equity and a high chance of getting called or check‑raised by hands that beat me (Ks, Kx, AK, AA). With deep stacks and position, pot control is correct: check to see his reaction, fold to sizeable aggression, and look to apply pressure later only if he shows clear weakness.

On this extremely coordinated board of Ks-Kc-Ac-Qh, my hand (Aces and Kings with a Queen kicker) is very strong but vulnerable. Any King in my opponent's hand makes a full house, and a Jack-Ten makes a straight. Since I 3-bet pre-flop and checked the flop, I am in a 'way ahead or way behind' situation. Betting here doesn't extract much value because I am likely to be called only by hands that beat me or other Aces that result in a chop (as the Queen on the board acts as the fifth card for any Ace with a kicker lower than a Queen). Checking is the best play to control the pot size and see the river.

Board K♠ K♣ A♣ Q♥ with T♠6♠ in my hand: I have no pair and after the Q♥ the backdoor spade flush is dead — effectively no realistic outs. Flash checked the turn; his check can be either pot‑control with strong hands (Kx/AK/AA) or a probing check with a medium hand, but many of his holdings will call a bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff with low fold equity and would bloat a pot I have almost no chance to win at showdown. With deep stacks I’ll keep pot control and check to the river to see his action, then re‑evaluate: only continue aggressively if I improve or get a very favorable river reaction.

The board is Ks-Kc-Ac-Qh-Qd. My best five-card hand is Ac-Ah-Ks-Kc-Qd, which is two pair, Aces and Kings with a Queen kicker. Given the board texture, I am either winning against non-A/K/Q hands, chopping with any other Ace, or losing to a King (Full House), Queen (Full House), or JT (Straight). Since the action has been checked through on every street since the pre-flop 3-bet, it is highly likely that we are heading to a chop or that Minni has a weak hand that will not call a bet. Betting here serves no purpose as I won't get value from worse hands, and I don't want to be check-raised by a slow-played monster. I will check and take the showdown.

Final board K♠ K♣ A♣ Q♥ Q♦ — this heavily favors Flash’s 3‑betting range (Kx, AK, AA, QQ, KQ etc.). I hold T♠6♠ with no pair, no flush and effectively zero showdown value. Flash checked flop/turn/river; that line can be pot‑control with very strong hands or an inducement, but it also means many of his hands will call a river bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff with little fold equity and would only bloat a pot I’m very unlikely to win. Optimal line is pot control: check and take the showdown (and fold to any river aggression).

I am on the Button in a heads-up situation with Ten-Deuce offsuit. While I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand, T2o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has been playing relatively aggressively and defending their big blind effectively. I have folded similar hands in previous rounds (Hand #64 and Hand #96) to avoid unnecessary variance with low-equity cards. I will fold here and wait for a more favorable range to engage with.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) — you should be opening a wide range. Q♠5♦ is marginal but perfectly reasonable to raise in position to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the BB. I’ll make it a standard 3xBB open (300 total) — big enough to gain fold equity and discourage a cheap flop, but small enough to keep postflop decisions manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, and if called use position to c-bet favorable flops (bet when I connect or when board is dry), otherwise proceed cautiously and preserve stack.

I am holding Ace-Eight of diamonds in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the strength of my hand and the need to exert pressure and build the pot, I will put in a 3-bet to 900. This prices out many weaker hands and builds a pot for value if I am called.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♠5♦ and Flash (BB) has 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1,200; calling 600 more would give me ~3:1 immediate pot odds (need ≈25% equity). Against a typical BB 3‑bet range here (value heavy: TT+, AQ+, KQs, plus some polarized bluffs) Q5o is frequently dominated and has well under the ~25% equity required to make calling +EV once future streets and continuation bets are considered. A light 4‑bet to 1,500 is too thin — I don’t have the right blockers and with deep stacks I’d be committing chips with very poor playability if called. I’ll stick to my preflop plan and fold this marginal hand to a sizable 3‑bet, preserve my deep stack, and look for better spots to apply pressure in position.

In a heads-up situation, T8o is a solid hand to play from the button. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to exert pressure and gain control of the pot early, which has been a consistent strategy in previous hands.

Heads-up on the big blind facing a Button (SB) open to 3x. I hold 3♣9♥ — a weak, unsuited, low card holding with very poor postflop playability and many dominating hands in the opener’s range. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (break-even equity ≈33%), but I’ll be out of position postflop which raises the equity I actually need. With deep stacks I want hands that can realize equity and be played OOP; this one isn’t worth it. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot with position or a playable hand.

Heads-up on the button (I'm SB) I have T♣7♥. In heads-up play the button opens very wide — T7o is playable and I have postflop position. Min-raising to 2x the big blind (to 200) is standard: it gives fold equity to pick up the current pot (150) and keeps the initiative while keeping the raise size reasonable with deep stacks (I have 21700, BB 18150). Calling 50 would be too passive and lets the BB see cheap flops; a larger overbet isn't necessary. Plan: open to 200, c-bet or float in position depending on the flop texture and opponent reaction.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit (K9o) facing a min-raise from Minni. K9o is a strong starting hand in heads-up play and easily meets the equity requirements to defend against a 2x raise. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, and the hand plays well enough post-flop to justify seeing a board rather than 3-betting and potentially bloating the pot out of position.

In this heads-up situation, I am the Big Blind and checked to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, on a board of 10-7-4. My hand, King-Nine offsuit, currently has King-high with two overcards to the middle and bottom pairs. Standard play out of position in a heads-up pot is to check to the aggressor to see how they react. Leading here (donk betting) doesn't achieve much as it forces folds from hands I beat and gets called or raised by hands that beat me. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

I have T♣7♥ on a 4♥ 7♠ 10♥ flop — that’s two pair (Tens and Sevens) heads-up and very likely best versus Flash’s checked range. In position I should bet for value and protection: Flash’s check can include worse tens, sevens, single pairs, pocket pairs and some straight/overcard draws. Betting denies free cards to draws, extracts value from weaker pairs and keeps the initiative. Pot is 400, so I’ll make a sizable value bet (≈75% pot) to get called by worse hands but still charge draws. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (call smaller raises for value, fold/reshove depending on a very large raise).

On a flop of Th-7s-4h, I have King-high with two overcards. Minni has bet 300 into a 400 pot, which is a 75% pot-sized bet. This is a relatively strong continuation bet on a somewhat coordinated board. While I have two overcards, they are overcards to the 7 and 4, not the 10. My King-high has some showdown value and I have two backdoors (straight and flush are non-existent, but I can hit a King or Nine). However, calling 300 to win 700 (getting 2.3:1) out of position with just overcards on a board that likely connects with a button opening range is marginal. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to protect my stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot, as Minni has shown aggression consistently.

I am on the button with suited connectors (4h 5h). In heads-up play, this is a hand with good playability and potential to flop draws or sneaky two-pair/trips. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive button strategy and take control of the pot.

Fold. 8♥2♣ is the single worst HU starting hand and plays terribly out of position. Flash has opened to 300 and I must call 200 more to see a flop (pot odds ~2:1, need ≈33% equity). Versus a standard button opening range 8-2o is heavily dominated and will rarely have the required equity or postflop playability, especially OOP with deep stacks. No clear read suggests Flash is opening so wide that this becomes +EV, so I preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with K♠3♥ I should be aggressive preflop. Limping is too passive and a min-raise to 200 is often too small; opening to 3x (300 total) gives healthy fold equity vs the BB’s wide defending range while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks (my 21.8k vs his 18.05k). If he calls I have position and can c-bet a wide range; if he 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate based on sizing. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and exploit heads-up dynamics.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight suited. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play and easily warrants a call to see a flop. While a 3-bet is possible, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and realize my equity against a wide opening range.

On this paired board of 10h-9c-10s, I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here. King-high maintains some showdown value, and I want to see if the opponent continues their aggression before committing more chips to the pot.

Flop is a paired-board (T♣T♥9♣) that heavily favors the caller’s preflop range (tens, 9x, pocket pairs, strong showdown hands). I have K♠3♥ — no pair, no real draw and very little fold equity. A continuation bet here is thin: villain will call with tens, 9x, most pocket pairs and many draws, and can raise with the very strong hands, so betting just bloats a pot I’m likely to lose. Pot-control and chess: check back, see a turn for a cheap pair or a safe card to bluff on later, and fold to meaningful aggression unless I improve.

On the turn 2d, the board remains paired with Tens and a Nine. Both players checked the flop, suggesting neither has a strong hand like a Ten or a Nine. King-high still has significant showdown value in heads-up play. I will check again to keep the pot small and see the river for free, or potentially induce a bluff from a weaker hand that I can then evaluate.

I have K♠3♥ on T♥ 9♣ T♠ 2♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw and very little fold equity. Villain called preflop and checked flop and turn, a line that often contains tens, nines and other pocket pairs or strong showdown hands. Betting here would merely bloat a pot I’m likely behind in against his calling range; with deep stacks I should avoid getting raised off a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity if I catch a king or get a favorable river, and conserve chips for better spots. If Flash shows aggression on the river I can fold to large bets or re-evaluate based on sizing and the river card.

The board has double-paired (Tens and Nines), meaning my final hand is two pair, Tens and Nines, with a King kicker. Given that the flop and turn were checked through by both players, it is unlikely that Minni holds a Ten or a Nine. My King kicker is very strong in this situation, as it beats any hand that doesn't contain an Ace or a King. However, betting might only get called by an Ace or a better hand, while checking allows me to reach a showdown for free or potentially catch a bluff. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control on this board.

Check. I have K♠3♥ with no pair or draw on a river of T♥ 9♣ T♠ 2♦ 9♥ — the board already shows two pair (tens & nines), so most of Flash’s calling range (9x, Tx, pocket pairs, decent showdown hands) either beats me or ties to the board. Flash checked flop/turn/river, showing willingness to go to showdown and giving me almost no fold equity. Betting would only bloat a pot I’m likely behind in; I’ll check to control the pot and conserve chips (and fold to a meaningful raise).

In heads-up play, King-Six suited is a strong starting hand on the button, well above the average holding. I want to take the initiative and build a pot while having the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♦K♥ — a top-tier hand in this spot. Flash (SB/button) opened to 300; I’m getting no price to just call and I want to both build value and apply pressure to a likely wide opening range. With deep stacks I don’t need to commit now, but I do want a decisive 3-bet size that gets fold equity and narrows ranges. I’ll 3-bet to 1,200 total: big enough to force folds from weaker broadways and small pairs, but small enough to leave room to call or fold to a large 4-bet and to play postflop if called. Plan postflop: c-bet many Ace/King/high-card flops for value and fold equity; if Flash 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (call vs moderate 4-bet, fold to an overbearing shove depending on sizing/reads).

In heads-up play, King-Six suited is a strong enough hand to continue against a 3-bet, especially with the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Minni's 3-bet to 1,200 is a significant increase, but the pot odds and the playability of my suited King make a call the most appropriate response. Folding would be too conservative for heads-up play at this stack depth, and 4-betting would likely turn a hand with good post-flop equity into a bluff unnecessarily.

I 3‑bet preflop to 1,200 and was called, so as the preflop aggressor I still have a range advantage. The flop Js‑7c‑8h misses my AK but gives me two clean overcards and decent fold equity versus a wide calling range (broadways, weak pairs, some suited connectors). A half‑pot c‑bet (~1,200 into a 2,400 pot) achieves several things: (1) charges any turn/river equity hands, (2) folds out many weaker broadways and some pocket pairs, and (3) keeps pot control with plenty of stack behind if I get called. Plan: if called, re‑evaluate on the turn — bet for value if I pair an A/K, check‑control on blanks, and fold to heavy aggression on later streets since I currently have no pair. If I face a large raise on the flop I'll give the opponent credit for a strong made hand (Jx/straights/sets) and avoid committing chips with just overcards.

On a flop of Jack-Seven-Eight, my King-Six suited has missed completely, providing only a backdoor flush draw and one overcard (the King) that might not even be clean given Minni's pre-flop 3-bet. Minni led out for 1,200 into a 2,400 pot, showing continued aggression. This board texture connects well with a 3-betting range (containing hands like JJ, 88, 77, T9s, or AJ), and calling here with just King high and poor equity would be a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 5♥T♠ I want to be aggressive and seize initiative. I’m in position postflop, stacks are deep (23k vs 16.85k) so I can open a wide range and generate fold equity. My normal opening size at this table has been ~300; raising to 300 (total commit) applies pressure to the BB, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me the initiative. If Flash 3-bets I can fold this marginal hand without much cost; if he calls I’m playing in position with reasonable playability. Therefore I raise to 300 to steal the blind and maximize long-run EV.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. Minni, who is on the button and has a larger stack, has raised to 300. While the odds to call are 2:1, 94o performs poorly out of position and does not flop well. Given that there are still 35 hands remaining in the tournament, I should avoid defending with marginal hands that are easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

In a heads-up situation, 9-8 offsuit is a solid hand to play from the small blind/button. I will follow the standard strategy of raising to 300 to exert pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♥T♠ — a pretty strong heads-up defending hand. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more. With deep stacks and a wide opening range from the button, KTo has good equity and plays well postflop (top-pair, broadway and straight possibilities). I’m out of position, so 3-betting is an option, but KTo is marginal as a 3-bet-for-value and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position without a stronger hand. I’ll defend by calling the raise to keep the pot controlled and realize equity; on favorable flops I’ll be prepared to fire/raise, and on dry or heavily contested boards I’ll give up to big pressure. Calling also keeps his weaker/blur ranges in and preserves implied odds.

Heads‑up on Q♠ A♦ 5♣ with K♥ T♠ — I’m out of position and have no pair (just K‑high) with only a 4‑card broadway draw to a J and three kings to pair. The button’s opening range contains a lot of A‑x and Q‑x on this flop, so a c‑bet bluff will run into many dominated or better hands and can get me raised or priced out. With deep stacks I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: call small continuation bets to see the turn (I have ~16% to hit the straight by river and additional outs to pair), fold to large, and re‑evaluate/lead or bluff on favorable turns. If Flash checks back, I can take a free card or barreling the turn selectively.

The flop of Ad-Qs-5c is very favorable for my pre-flop raising range, as I am much more likely to have an Ace or a Queen than Minni. Minni checked, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to capitalize on the board texture and try to take the pot down immediately. If called, I will likely evaluate the turn carefully, as I currently have no real equity with 9-high.

I defend the BB with K♥T♠ on Q♠ A♦ 5♣. Flash (button) opened preflop and now fires a relatively small 300 c-bet. I have no made hand and no realistic flush draw (only one spade in hand + one on board), just a gutshot to J (~15–16% to hit by the river) and a few pair-outs (K/T) that are often second-best. Raising OOP with this marginal holding is too thin — it bloats the pot when I’m frequently behind and invites difficult decisions vs reraises. The bet is small enough that calling keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, controls the pot size, and lets me realize equity with deep stacks. Plan: call 300 to see the turn; if I miss and he barrels big I’ll fold; if I improve (pair or hit the J) I’ll apply pressure or extract value; if he checks I may take a free card or barrele a safe turn.

Flash checked the turn after a small flop c-bet. I have K♠T♥ (king-high) with only a one-card Broadway straight draw (a J on the river would make T–J–Q–K–A) and a handful of pair-outs. The board (Q♠ A♦ 5♣ 9♥) is very Q/A-heavy and his range contains lots of A‑x and Q‑x that will call or raise thin bluffs — betting OOP with this marginal holding just bloats the pot and creates difficult decisions. With deep stacks and only a small pot (1,200), I’ll exercise pot control and see a free river; if I improve (pair/straight) I can apply pressure or extract value, if he leads on the river I can fold to large bets. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and realize equity.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of nines. While this gives me some showdown value, I am likely still behind Minni's flop-calling range, which likely includes various Aces, Queens, or potentially a 5. Since Minni checked again, I will check back to control the size of the pot and see a cheap river. Betting here doesn't offer much benefit as I'm unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Queen, and I'd rather realize my equity with a mid-pair than turn it into a bluff at this stage.

River paired the queen (Qs ... Qc) and I hold K♠ T♥ — no pair, no real blocker to Q/A value hands. Flash’s line (wide open pre, small flop c-bet, check on the 9♣ turn) contains a lot of Qx/Ax and medium pairs that now beat me, and the paired Q on the river further increases the portion of his range that has value. This is a pure bluff spot if I bet, but my fold equity is thin vs that range and I’d be bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Pot is small relative to stacks, so preserving chips and avoiding marginal OOP bluffs is correct. I’ll check and concede to any meaningful river bet; if Flash checks back I avoid an unnecessary showdown with K-high.

The river brought a second Queen, giving me two pair (Queens and Nines) with an Ace kicker. Minni has checked all the way since calling my flop bet. While I have improved, any Ace in Minni's range still beats me (Aces and Queens vs Queens and Nines). Since she checked, I have a good amount of showdown value against her non-Ace hands and missed draws. There's little value in betting as better hands (Aces) won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 4♥6♥. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; against one opponent in heads-up I should be opening a very wide range from the button to take initiative and use position post‑flop. 4♥6♥ has reasonable playability (flush/straight potential) and raises often pick up the pot or define ranges. I’ll raise to the standard 2xBB (200 total) to gain fold equity and keep the initiative. Plan if called: play in position — c‑bet on favourable flops (especially hearts or connected low boards) ~1/3–1/2 pot, check/ pot‑control on dry high‑card boards, and proceed with draws. If Flash 3‑bets to a large sizing I’ll usually fold this hand; call only to small 3‑bets if the price and read justify it. Raising to 200 maximizes my EV here while preserving post‑flop options.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 (a min-raise). While the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call, 9-3 offsuit performs poorly out of position and rarely improves to a winning hand. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I'll fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot to challenge the chip leader.
I am on the button with pocket sevens, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300. I’m OOP with Q♠2♦ — a weak hand and often dominated, but the pot odds are attractive: pot is 400 and I need 200 to call (I’d be committing 300 total including my BB), so I need ~25% equity. Versus a wide button opening range Q2o has enough equity to justify defending in a deep-stacked spot. A 3-bet bluff with Q2o is too thin and folding is too nitty here. Plan: call, see the flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy action on bad/dry boards, realize equity and look for top-pair/decent turn improvements to proceed.

Check. I defended Q♠2♦ preflop heads‑up because the effective stacks and pot odds justified a call, but this flop (K♦ J♥ K♠) utterly misses my hand. There are two kings on board so the preflop raiser’s range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat me (any K, KJ/KQ lines, pocket pairs that can improve/slowplay). I have no pair and no meaningful draw, so the prudent, +EV line is pot control: check and see what Flash does. If Flash checks back I can realize my equity and reevaluate on the turn; if he leads I will fold to any realistic c‑bet size — only consider calling a very tiny probe (≤ ~1/3 pot) as a float if I have a clear plan to take it away on the turn — otherwise fold. I will not bluff‑lead here because the board texture and villain’s range make it a poor story.

On a board of Kd-Jh-Ks, my pocket sevens are vulnerable to any King or Jack, and almost any broadway card that Minni might have called with pre-flop. Since I have some showdown value but are unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, checking back to control the pot size and see a turn card is the most prudent play.

I have Q♠2♦ — completely missed. Board is K♦ J♥ K♠ A♦ (two kings and an ace). There are no realistic draws I can hit (the diamond backdoor doesn’t give a flush with one card to come), so I’m behind any K, any A, and most reasonable calling hands from an opener. Flash checked flop and turn; that can be a trap or simply pot‑control, but I have no value and very little fold equity — a bet is likely to be called or raised by better hands and would commit chips with no backup plan. With deep stacks the prudent line is pot control: check and fold to significant river aggression. I’ll see the river and reassess if he checks again, but I will not bluff this turn or call large bets without improvement.

The board is extremely coordinated with high cards (Kd, Jh, Ks, Ad), making my pocket sevens very weak. Since Minni has checked both the flop and the turn, I have some showdown value against her bluffs or missed draws, but betting here would likely only result in being called by better hands. I will check back to see the river for free and try to reach a cheap showdown.

Board K♦ J♥ K♠ A♦ T♦ — I hold Q♠2♦ and never improved. I defended preflop for price but the flop and turn (check‑check) and the final Ten give me zero showdown value and essentially no realistic bluffs left: the opener’s range contains many Ks, As, Js and pocket pairs that will not fold to a river probe. Betting here would risk chips for very little fold equity (need an unrealistically high fold frequency to make a small bluff profitable) and I have no value to extract. Preserve stack and keep pot controlled — check and concede the hand rather than bluff into a range that dominates me.

The board is Kd-Jh-Ks-Ad-Td. My pocket sevens are completely counterfeited, as any five-card hand using the board (A-K-K-J-T) is superior to any hand using my sevens unless I had a King or a Queen. Since Minni has checked the river after checking the flop and turn, it is highly likely she also lacks a King or a Queen. Betting would not accomplish much as she is unlikely to fold a hand that chops and will only call or raise with hands that beat me (like a straight or trips). Checking back is the most logical play to take the guaranteed chop.

4♠2♥ is a very weak hand, but heads-up on the button I have postflop position and good pot odds to see a flop. Calling 50 into a 150 pot (total commit 100) costs very little and gives me the chance to realize equity or float in position. A min-raise inflates the pot and risks facing a 3-bet with a junk hand; folding 50 is too tight. Plan: see the flop in position, fold to large aggression unless I connect (pair, strong draw), and extract value if I hit.

I am in the Big Blind with 10-7 offsuit. Minni has limped from the Button. While I could raise to try and take the pot now, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a marginal hand. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, there's no need to inflate the pot out of position with this holding.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a flop of 10-6-4. Since Minni limped pre-flop, her range is wide, but checking here allows me to keep the pot small and see how she reacts. I'm first to act and would be comfortable check-calling a bet from her to extract value from bluffs or draws, while avoiding building a large pot with a marginal kicker.

I flopped a small pair (I have 4♠2♥ and the board is Tc 6d 4c) in heads-up position after my preflop call. My preflop plan was to see the flop and extract value if I connected — this is a marginal but real made hand. Flash checked, showing weakness or a trap; I’ll bet to get value from worse hands (Ax/Kx/Qx, some random pairs) and deny free cards rather than slow-playing into potential overcards. I’ll make a half‑pot bet (100). If I face a large check‑raise I’ll give it up (likely a Ten/set); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and avoid committing more without improvement.

I have top pair (Tens) with a 7 kicker on a 10-6-4 board. Since Minni limped from the small blind, her range is extremely wide. Her half-pot bet on the flop could be a continuation bet with air, a draw, or a weaker pair. By calling instead of raising, I keep the pot manageable and allow Minni to continue bluffing or betting with worse hands on later streets. I am not yet worried about being behind, but I want to avoid overinflating the pot with a mediocre kicker.

On the turn, the second Ten gives me trips. Since I check-called the flop, my hand is somewhat disguised. Checking here allows Minni to continue any bluffs she started on the flop or to bet if she has improved to a marginal pair or is chasing a draw. Given that she limped pre-flop, her range is very wide, and leading out might cause her to fold her air. I'll check to induce a bet and plan to call to keep her range wide.

I have a very marginal made hand — bottom pair (4♠) on a now-paired board (T♣ 6♦ 4♣ / T♥). Flash called my flop c-bet, then checked the turn; that line is wide and can include lots of worse hands I beat (overcards, Kx/Ax, busted draws) but also includes hands that now dominate me (any Ten = trips, 66/set, higher pocket pairs). Betting the turn would bloat the pot and unnecessarily commit chips with a hand that has poor showdown equity and is vulnerable to a check‑raise or a river shove. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to let bluffs and weaker hands bluff the river. Plan: check back the turn, see the river, call small to medium bets for thin value or fold to large pressure. Action: check.

I have trips (Tens) on a board of 10-6-4-10-Q. Since this was a limped pot and Minni checked back on the turn, it's unlikely she has a better Ten or a strong hand like a full house. The Queen on the river might have improved a marginal hand in her range, or she might be willing to call a value bet with a pair of Sixes or Fours. I will lead out with a value bet to extract more chips from her wide range rather than checking and risking her checking behind with a mediocre hand.

I have bottom pair (4♠) on a fairly dry board that paired on the turn (T♣ 6♦ 4♣ / T♥ Q♠). Flash’s line — check pre, call my small flop c‑bet, check the turn, then a small river stab (300 into a 700 pot) — is consistent with a mixed river range: thin value (Qx, occasional pocket pairs, rare Tens/sets) and a decent frequency of missed hands/bluffs. Calling 300 into a 700 pot gives me a pot of ~1,000 after the call, so I need ~30% equity to break even. I beat many unpaired hands and worse pairs that would call/see the river, and the bet is small relative to my stack (22750), so the call is low risk with reasonable pot odds. Raising with this weak hand would bloat the pot and only get action from better hands; folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call 300 to see showdown.

In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) should open a very wide range of hands. Although 9-5 offsuit is a relatively weak starting hand, it is still within a standard opening range to exert pressure and take advantage of the positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand. Minni has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop raises in several previous hands, making a standard 3x raise a profitable play here.

Heads-up against a standard 3x open from the button, A♠6♦ is strong enough to defend the big blind but is still a marginal hand out of position. Flash has been opening widely to 300 a lot, so their range is fairly wide. I want to keep the pot reasonable and realize equity — calling keeps their range wide and lets me play flop for top-pair or good draws without committing a big chunk of my deep stack. A tiny 3-bet (to 500) would be weak and a large 3-bet is risky with an offsuit A6 out of position. Plan: call to 300, evaluate the flop — continue aggressively if I hit an Ace or strong draw, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy action.

Check. On 3♥-3♠-5♣ with A♠6♦ I only have Ace-high (no pair and no realistic backdoor flush/straight), so I want to keep the pot under control out of position. Flash opens wide a lot so they will c-bet many hands; checking lets me realize equity as a bluff-catcher and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and call small continuation bets, only inflate the pot if I pair my Ace (or pick up a clear two-card improvement); fold to heavy turn/river aggression without improvement. Preserves stack for later hands and keeps decision-making flexible.

On a dry, paired board like 3-3-5, a continuation bet is effective as the pre-flop aggressor. I have very little showdown value with 9-high, so I want to take the pot down now by making Minni's missed overcards fold. A half-pot bet of 300 is appropriate here to apply pressure without overcommitting with a weak holding.

Facing a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) on 3♥-3♠-5♣ after I checked with A♠6♦. I have only Ace-high and no real draws, but Flash opens very wide from the button and this size includes a lot of bluffs and overcards. Calling is the correct, low-variance play: the call costs 300 into a 1200 pot (≈25% equity required), so it's cheap to see a turn and realize showdown equity. Raising out of position with A6o would bloat the pot with marginal equity and be easily punished by stronger hands or a shove; folding gives up too much equity versus a wide c-bet. Plan is to call, keep the pot under control, and re-evaluate on the turn — proceed if I pair an Ace or pick up a clear improvement, otherwise fold to heavy turn/river aggression.

I called the small flop c-bet to realize equity and the turn (T♠) didn’t improve A♠6♦ — I still only have Ace-high with no real draws. Betting out of position here would bloat the pot with marginal equity and get me committed against hands that already beat me (Tx, 33, sets, paired 5s, overpairs). Flash opened and c-bet wide, so they have many hands that can continue, but there’s no reason to force the issue now. I’ll control the pot: check and see how they react. If they fire a small probe bet I can call cheap to try to get to showdown or fold to heavy pressure; if they check back I can take a free river and reevaluate only if I improve. Action: check to avoid unnecessary stack risk.

The turn Ten of Spades is an overcard to the board's five but doesn't particularly improve my Nine-high. Minni called my flop continuation bet, suggesting she has at least a small pair, a five, or a strong Ace-high float. Continuing to bluff on the turn is risky against a player who has shown they can be sticky with marginal hands. I will check back to control the size of the pot and see a free river card, giving up on the hand unless a favorable card appears or an opportunity to bluff the river arises.

Check. I have only A-high with no backdoor draws and both of us checked the turn, so the line is one of weakness or pot-control from Flash. The river 2♣ changes almost nothing — there’s no meaningful value I expect to extract (worse hands that would call a river bet, like K/Q/J-high, often folded to my flop call/turn check or will call small bets, while hands that beat me — any pair, trips, sets, Tx — will definitely continue). The pot is tiny (1,200) relative to our deep stacks, so bluffing here risks chips for little expected gain and is thin versus this opponent’s tendencies (wide openers who will call small river bets). Checking preserves stack and lets me realize showdown equity; if Flash now bets a small amount I can call depending on sizing and their river frequency, but I’ll fold to large river aggression without an improvement. Bottom line: pot control and low-variance showdown — check.

On the river, the board is 3h-3s-5c-Ts-2c. I hold Nine-high, which has no showdown value. Minni checked for the third time (post-flop), suggesting she has a marginal hand like a small pair (5x), a weak Ace-high, or is perhaps trapping with a Three. However, she called my flop continuation bet, which indicates she has some piece of the board.
