Given that I checked back the turn, a river bet might look like a delayed bluff or a thin value bet with a Ten. However, Minni has shown stickiness in recent hands, and I don't want to risk more chips when my range doesn't strongly favor a bluff success here. I will check behind and concede the pot, preserving my stack for better opportunities in the final 30 hands.

Heads-up on the button I have positional advantage post-flop. Q3o is marginal but perfectly fine to raise for initiative in heads-up play. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is cheap, but a small raise to the minimum (200 total) maximizes fold equity vs the BB and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal holding. I raise to seize initiative — if Flash folds I take the blinds; if he calls I play post-flop in position; if he 3-bets big I can fold without risking much.

I am in the Big Blind with 82o. Minni has min-raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 82o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stage of the tournament and the goal of maximizing my stack, I prefer to fold this bottom-tier hand and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips to a hand with very little equity or playability.

I am on the button with a strong suited connector, Jc 9c, in a heads-up situation. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the hand and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB while I'm in the big blind with 6♥4♣. I already have 100 in and would need to call 200 (pot odds ~33%), but this hand is weak and plays terribly out of position against a wide opening range. With deep stacks this would commit me to multi-street decisions where I'm often dominated or forced to fold to aggression. 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive (no good blockers, out of position). Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 3♠4♥ — a weak, unsuited hand but in heads-up play I should be opening a very wide range to seize initiative and use positional advantage postflop. Calling 50 is too passive (surrenders initiative), folding is overly tight, and a min-raise to 200 (2×BB) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if Flash calls. With a deep stack I'll C-bet good flops or check/plan accordingly versus aggression. I raise to 200 to take the lead and exploit heads-up dynamics.

I am holding pocket deuces in the Big Blind. Minni has min-raised from the Button. Pocket pairs are quite strong in heads-up play, even the lowest one. Folding is out of the question given the 3:1 pot odds. While I could 3-bet to take control of the hand, pocket deuces play better in a smaller pot unless I flop a set. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate. If I flop a set, I have significant implied odds against a wide button-opening range.

I have pocket deuces on an Ace-high board. As the Big Blind, I am the first to act on the flop. This board (Tc-6c-Ad) hits a significant portion of a standard button opening range. With only an underpair and no real draw, I have very little equity. My plan is to check and likely fold to a bet, as pocket deuces are very difficult to play out of position on this texture.

I raised preflop from the button and Flash called, so I still have initiative and a very wide perceived range. On Tc-6c-Ad I have nothing (3♠4♥), no club and no pair, but Flash checked — a good spot to continuation-bet to take advantage of position and fold out many weaker hands and non-Ax holdings. Because the Ace on board favors the caller's defending range, I’ll keep the bet small (≈1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. If I get a raise I’ll fold most air; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

On this Ace-high board, my pocket deuces are an underpair with almost no equity. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor, and this texture (Ad-Tc-6c) strongly favors her range. While her bet is small, calling out of position with the lowest possible pair on such a board is generally a losing play, as I will likely face further pressure on the turn and river. Sticking to my strategy of checking and folding to a bet on this unfavorable board texture to preserve my stack.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 98o. In a heads-up situation, this hand is well above the fold threshold and is a standard opening candidate. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a post-flop play with position. I will stick to my established opening size of 300 chips.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I hold Kc Jd in the big blind — a strong heads-up hand that’s worth defending. Pot is ~400 and I only need to put in 200 more (total committed 300), which is cheap relative to my 21,950 stack and gives good pot odds (~3:1). I’m out of position so I don’t want to 3-bet light and face tougher post-flop decisions; calling preserves a wide defending range and keeps weaker parts of villain’s opening range in. Plan: see the flop, continue with top pair or strong draws, and fold to overwhelming aggression on dry miss boards. Therefore I will call to see a flop.

I have top pair (KcJd) on a dry K-T-6 flop heads-up. Flash (preflop raiser) checked to me, so betting for value and protection is preferable to checking back out of position. A 2/3-pot sized bet (~400 into 600) charges worse kings, tens, and Jx/Qx hands that will call, and prices out random backdoor draws. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate (he’s representing a very strong hand or a bluff) but for now extract thin value and deny equity with a 400 bet.

I am currently holding 9-high on a board of K-T-6 rainbow. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into a 600 pot. This action typically represents at least a pair or a strong draw (like QJ). My hand has zero connection to the board and very little potential to improve on future streets. Given that I have no equity and the board favors a calling or leading range, there is no reason to float or bluff here. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with deep stacks — I should be opening very wide to seize initiative and pick up the blinds. 5♣2♠ is garbage for value but fine as a bluff/opening candidate in heads-up. Raising gives fold equity and leaves me in position postflop; if Flash 3-bets I can fold and preserve chips. I’ll use a standard 3x-ish open to apply pressure and be consistent with my prior sizing. Raise to 300 total (min-raise is 200) — if he folds I win the pot outright, if he calls I play postflop with position and can proceed cautiously.

K7o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind with in a heads-up situation. Minni's 3x raise is standard, and with the pot odds provided (200 to call into a 400 pot), I have the necessary equity to see a flop. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary with a hand that has good showdown value but isn't a premium.

I am out of position on an Ace-high board with only King-high. Since the Ace favors the pre-flop raiser's range and I have no significant piece of this board, the standard and most prudent play is to check and see how Minni proceeds. Leading into the raiser here would likely be a waste of chips as I have very little equity to continue if raised or called.

Heads-up I opened very wide preflop and got the flop checked to me on A♥ 8♣ 3♣. I have nothing (5♥2♠) and no club draw, but villain checked which gives me fold equity. Against a wide calling range a standard continuation bet will fold many hands (weak pairs, floats) and deny equity to club draws. I’ll size ~2/3 pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with air. Plan: bet 400; if he raises heavily I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn with position.

On this Ace-high board with two clubs, Minni makes a continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot. I have King-high with no draw and no pair. This board favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly, and I have very little equity to continue. Attempting a check-raise bluff here against a player who has shown aggression throughout the match is risky, especially given that an Ace is well within her opening range. Folding is the most disciplined and chip-preserving play here.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Ten-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, especially with the deep stacks we currently have (over 170 BBs). A raise to 3x the big blind is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the hand or potentially win the blinds immediately. Minni has been showing a mix of folding and calling to my pre-flop raises, and T9o is strong enough to play post-flop if called.

Heads-up vs Flash's standard open to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Qc7c — a suited queen with decent connectivity and blocker value. Calling is the best option: I get good pot odds (call 200 to win ~400) and Q7c has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range plus backdoor club/straight possibilities. The min-raise to 500 is a small, inefficient 3-bet size that I don’t want to commit with a marginal hand out of position; a larger 3-bet would be needed to take the initiative. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively when I hit (top pair, strong draws), fold to heavy action with nothing. Preserve deep-stack maneuverability rather than bloating the pot preflop with a marginal holding.

I have Qc7c on a Qh-7h-8d flop — I’ve flopped two pair (Queens and Sevens) in a heads-up pot, which is a very strong hand. Flash’s wide button opening range includes many single-pair hands (Qx, 8x, 7x), straight draws (e.g. 9-10) and a few two-heart combos with backdoor flush draws. I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge/deny correct odds to straight/flush draws. A pot-sized bet both builds the pot when I’m likely ahead and makes many drawing hands (OESDs) pay incorrect odds to continue. I’ll bet pot (600) and re-evaluate on the turn — if Flash raises big it’s likely a very strong hand (set/boat) and I’ll proceed cautiously.

I have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack) on an 8-Q-7 flop. Minni has led out with a pot-sized bet (600 into 600). With 8 outs to a straight, I have roughly 32% equity to improve by the river. Calling in position is the best play here; it allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially realize my implied odds if I hit my straight, while also giving me the opportunity to take the pot away on later streets if Minni checks.

I hold Qc7c — two pair (Queens and Sevens) heads-up on 8♦ Q♥ 7♥ — a very strong holding in this spot. The turn A♣ is concerning in that it gives some of Flash’s calling range (AQ, A7, A8, maybe some slow-played sets) hands that now beat me, but his flop call also includes a lot of worse top-pair/second-pair hands and drawing hands (T9 open-enders, heart combos) that I want to charge. I should not check and give free equity to many draws; I should extract value and protect. A ~2/3-pot bet (~1200 into the 1800 pot) accomplishes both: it charges OESDs and heart draws (they get wrong odds to chase) while keeping many Qx/7x/8x hands in to call. Plan if raised: re-evaluate — call small-to-medium raises (my two pair still has good showdown equity vs bluffs/Ax) but fold to very large shoves that represent sets or two-pair combinations that dominate me. Bet 1200 to maximize value and deny equity to draws.

On a board of 8d-Qh-7h-Ac, I am holding Ts-9h, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack). However, Minni has led into the pot on both the flop and the turn. Her 'donk bet' on the flop for full pot (600 into 600) and her follow-up on the turn (1200 into 1800) demonstrate significant strength. While the Ace of Clubs on the turn is technically a good card for me as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's decision to continue leading suggests she likely has a strong hand like top pair (AQ/Ax), two pair, or a set, or she is semi-bluffing with a heart draw. To call 1200 to win a current pot of 3000, I need roughly 28.5% equity. With only 8 outs to the straight (approximately 16% chance to hit on the river), I am not getting the direct pot odds to call. Furthermore, hitting my straight might not guarantee a large payout if the board becomes too coordinated, and Minni has shown she can be sticky or aggressive. I will fold and wait for a better spot to protect my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I'm the small blind) with Ten of Clubs and Four of Spades (T4o). In heads-up this is well inside a standard opening range — I want initiative and fold equity rather than limping. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) to apply pressure, keep the pot manageable with a marginal hand, and leverage position postflop. If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll evaluate the flop and c-bet favorable textures; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold given my weak kicker and limited playability.

Holding Ts 7d in the Big Blind, I am facing a min-raise from Minni on the button. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 100 to win a 400 total pot), which makes it a standard call in a heads-up match to see a flop. T7o has enough equity against a wide button opening range to justify defending the blind.

On a K-2-2 board, as the Big Blind who defended against a pre-flop raise, I will check to the aggressor. My Ten-high hand has little value here, and this board texture generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range which includes more Kings.

I opened preflop with T4o to take the initiative; Flash’s call is wide. Flop Ks 2s 2h is a dry, highly c-betable texture — it hits a relatively small portion of his calling range (Kx, any 2x, pocket pairs) and misses lots of broadways and weak Ax hands. I have no pair but do hold a backdoor spade and the Ten blocker to some broadways. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (200 into 400) to apply pressure and pick up the pot now when he checked. This size keeps the pot controllable if called. Plan: fold to a sizable raise (T4 has poor showdown equity vs aggression); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously, barreling only when the turn helps my range or gives additional equity (spade/T) and checking back when a blank arrives to control the pot.

On a board of Ks-2s-2h, my Ten-high hand has very little equity against Minni's range, which contains all the strong Kings and pocket pairs. While Minni's half-pot bet could be a standard continuation bet with air, I have no real draws and only marginal backdoor potential. Given the board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, I will fold and conserve my stack for better opportunities.

In heads-up play, Ten-Seven offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button (Small Blind). I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately, or play the hand with positional advantage if called.

Heads-up on the big blind facing a standard open to 300 from the button. I hold 4c2s — essentially junk. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is cheap relative to stacks and gives me the opportunity to see a flop with reasonable pot odds and realize some equity. A reraise would be a pure bluff with very little equity and unnecessary risk; folding is too tight in heads-up where defending a wide range is profitable. With deep stacks and most of the tournament still to play, I prefer pot control and to evaluate on the flop rather than commit more now.

Heads-up on a paired high-card flop (Jh Jc Kh). I hold 4♣2♠ — complete air: no pair, no backdoors. Flash was the preflop aggressor and his continuation-bet frequency will be high; his range here contains many Jx, Kx and strong overcards that dominate me. Betting as a bluff has very little fold equity vs that range and is an unnecessary risk with deep stacks. I should pot-control and see how he reacts. Plan: check now, fold to a large bet, but consider calling a very small c-bet for pot odds to see a turn and then re-evaluate.

On a board of Jh-Jc-Kh, the range of the Big Blind defender contains many hands that connect well, including any Jack, any King, and various straight draws like QT or Q9. While I was the pre-flop raiser, my Ten-high has very little equity if called, and this specific board texture is quite 'sticky,' meaning an opponent is unlikely to fold even a weak pair or a gutshot. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to realize what little equity I have or potentially bluff on a later street if the board runout becomes more favorable or if my opponent continues to show weakness.

I have A: 4♣2♠ on J♥ J♣ K♥ 7♥. I have zero showdown equity (no pair, no heart) and I don’t block heart combos — the turn completed a heart flush for any two‑heart holdings. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop; his range heavily includes Jx, Kx and many heart combinations that now beat me. Leading as a bluff here has very little fold equity versus that range and would risk a large chunk of my deep stack out of position. Pot control is the priority: check and see the river, fold to a meaningful bet, only entertain calling a very small stab. Checking preserves stack and keeps future options open.

I have turned a pair of sevens on a very coordinated board (Jh-Jc-Kh-7h). While I have improved from Ten-high, my hand is still quite marginal given the presence of a pair of Jacks and a King on the board, along with the potential for a flush. By checking back, I control the pot size and look to realize my showdown value or potentially catch a bluff on the river, rather than betting and potentially being raised or called by a better hand.

Board: J♥ J♣ K♥ 7♥ 8♠ — I hold 4♣2♠ with no pair, no heart and effectively zero showdown equity versus any reasonable portion of Flash’s preflop raising range (Jx, Kx, any pair, even many two‑pair/overpair combos). Flash checked the flop and turn, which can indicate a slowplayed value hand or a medium show‑down hand — either way my bluffing/folding equity is very poor. The pot (600) is small relative to stacks and there’s no need to risk chips on a low‑EV bluff here. Priority is pot control and stack preservation: check to the end and fold to a river bet (or only call an absurdly small stab).

On a board of Jh-Jc-Kh-7h-8s, I have a pair of sevens. Minni has checked to me on every street after calling my pre-flop raise. While my sevens have some showdown value, the board is quite dangerous with a pair of jacks, a king, and a possible flush present. Betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call. By checking back, I can see the showdown and potentially win the pot against her missed draws or Ace-high hands without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button with a mid pocket pair (8c8d). I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the pot is only 150; calling 50 is permissive and allows the BB to see flops cheaply. Against Flash’s wide defending/raising tendencies and with deep stacks, I want to take the initiative, build the pot and deny equity to overcards. A standard 3xBB sizing (raise to 300 total) is big enough to get value from his calling range, discourage some speculative hands, and still leaves room for postflop play if he 3-bets. I’ll raise to 300 (total committed), plan to continue aggressively on many flops, and re-evaluate if he 3-bets large.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has opened with a standard 3x raise from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 400), Ten-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the range and performs poorly out of position. Given that Minni has been consistently aggressive and I have a significant but not critical stack, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a more playable holding rather than bleed chips with a hand that has very little post-flop potential.

I am on the button with 6h 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. While I am behind in the overall chip count with only 20 hands remaining, 6-4 offsuit is not a hand I want to invest chips in against an aggressive opponent like Minni, especially since Minni has been defending the big blind well and putting pressure on post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to make up the chip deficit.

AcJs on the button heads-up is a strong open. I want the initiative and to play post‑flop in position, so I’ll raise rather than just calling the extra 50. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is my standard, balanced sizing here — it builds the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, preserves fold equity, and is in line with my prior opening tendencies. Stacks are deep (~235bb vs 163bb) so I can comfortably call or continue if Flash 3‑bets to a reasonable size; I’ll re-evaluate if they 3‑bet very large. For now: raise to 300.

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has been very active from the button, opening a wide range of hands. A9o is significantly ahead of a standard button opening range in heads-up play. Based on previous hands (like #75 and #109), Minni has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets pre-flop. I will raise to 1000 (a 3.3x 3-bet) to extract value, take initiative, and potentially end the hand immediately, which is a good result given I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

A♣J♠ on the button facing a 3‑bet to 1000 is too good to fold and I prefer to keep the pot controllable while preserving positional advantage. The 3‑bet size is moderate; I need to put 700 more to call, which gives me roughly 35% break‑even equity to continue and I have good equity vs a wide/medium 3‑bet range. With ~154bb effective stacks I can comfortably play multi‑street in position and realize implied odds. 4‑betting is a viable polarized line but a small 4‑bet (to 1700) often bloats the pot and can put me in tough spots vs QQ+/AK or a shove; a larger 4‑bet or shove is unnecessary with AJo. I’ll call to 1000 total, see a flop in position, and proceed based on texture and villain action.

The flop of Jd Kh Qs is extremely coordinated and connects very well with the range of hands Minni would use to call a 3-bet pre-flop (broadway cards, pairs, and suited connectors). Although I have an inside straight draw (needing a Ten), I am currently holding only Ace-high. Betting into such a texture is risky because Minni is unlikely to fold any pair or draw, and many of her hands have likely improved. I will check to see how Minni reacts, planning to evaluate based on her bet sizing or take a free card if she checks back.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (A♣J♠) on a very connected K–Q–J rainbow flop. Flash checked after a preflop 3‑bet — his range includes lots of hands that beat me (AK, AQ, KQ, sets) but also weaker Jx, broadway hands and some air. My Ace blocks a number of AK/AQ combos, improving my relative equity. I want to both get value from worse jacks and charge/price out drawing hands while keeping the pot manageable in case of a check‑raise. A half‑pot c‑bet (1000 into 2000) is a balanced line: it extracts from worse and folds out pure air, but won’t overcommit me versus a large raise. Plan: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I continue to play in position and re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big I assign him very strong combinations (two‑pair/straight/set) and will fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Nine offsuit to take initiative against Minni's button raise, but the flop of Jack-King-Queen is one of the worst textures for my hand. This board connects heavily with the broadway-heavy range Minni likely uses to call a 3-bet. After checking to her, she bet 1000 into the 2000 pot. While I have a gutshot draw to the nut straight (needing a Ten), my 4 outs give me only about an 8% chance to hit on the turn, which does not justify calling a 50% pot bet (requiring 25% equity) when out of position. My Ace-high is also likely behind any pair she holds, and I have no fold equity against a range that hit this board so well. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.

In a heads-up situation, Kc 8c is a very strong starting hand. I am on the button (Small Blind), and standard practice is to open-raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous sizing and appropriate for this hand strength.

Button (Flash) opened to 300; I’m in the big blind with Js7s. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me (total committed will be 300). Heads‑up the button’s opening range is very wide and Js7s has good equity and post‑flop playability (suited, decent blocker). Calling requires ~33% equity (200 into a 600 pot after my call) — well within range vs a wide open. I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand by making a tiny 3‑bet to 500 (that size won’t fold a lot and leaves me out of position), so I’ll call to realize equity and use position/board texture to decide continuation lines. Plan: continue aggressively when I pair or hit spade/strong draws; control the pot or fold to heavy action on dry, dominated boards.

The flop (Tc 2c 9c) is a monotone, club-heavy board that heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range. I have Js7s with no clubs — only an overcard and backdoor potential — so very little showdown value and almost no fold equity if I fire. I’ll check to control the pot OOP and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: call a small c-bet (thin probe — roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 200) to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn, but fold to large bets/raises (≈1/2–pot or bigger) since the button’s range likely contains many made or strong club draws. If villain checks back I’ll consider a delayed c-bet on a safe, non-club turn or pursue aggression only if I improve (pair or strong draw).

I have flopped a King-high flush on a Tc 2c 9c board. The only hand currently beating me is the Ace-high flush (Ac). I must bet for value to get paid by sets, two pairs, lower flushes, or high single clubs like the Ac or Qc that may be drawing. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard value-building size that keeps Minni's range wide while building the pot for my strong hand.

Fold. To call 400 into a pot that’s effectively 1400 you need ~22% equity. On Tc‑2c‑9c with Js7s I have almost no showdown value or clean draw: only ~4 clean outs (Jd/Jh/7d/7h) → roughly 16–17% to hit by the river, and many other outs are clubs that simply give the button a flush. The board massively favors the raiser’s range (lots of clubs / made hands), so my real equity is likely below the break‑even point. Raising OOP with no clubs and poor blockers is too risky and will bloat the pot when I’m behind. This is a clear fold — preserve stack, wait for a better, more playable spot.

Heads-up on the button (posted SB) with A♥K♦ — a premium hand that should be played aggressively. Pot is 150 and my cost to call is only 50, but I want to seize initiative and deny the BB good odds. Min raise is 200; I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) — a standard, table-consistent open that builds the pot and isolates while keeping postflop play flexible. Postflop plan: continuation-bet a wide range on favorable runouts; if Flash 3-bets, I’ll evaluate sizing and tendencies — against small 3-bets I’ll often call and play postflop given deep stacks, vs very large/responsive 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate or fold depending on polarization.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Minni raises from the Small Blind to 300. In a heads-up situation, Ace-high hands have significant value and are standard defends against a button/SB raise. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position with a relatively weak Ace. Given the current tournament stage (Hand 133 of 150) and the stack sizes, maintaining a healthy stack while defending my blinds is the priority.

In this heads-up situation on Hand #133, I have flopped top pair with an Ace, albeit with the weakest possible kicker (Deuce). Since I am in the Big Blind, I act first on the flop. Standard play in this position after calling a pre-flop raise is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) into the pre-flop raiser on an Ace-high board often narrows the opponent's range to hands that beat me or causes them to fold hands I want them to bluff with. I will check and plan to call a standard continuation bet, as top pair is too strong to fold in heads-up play, even with a weak kicker.

Flop A♠-4♠-5♣ and I hold A♥K♦ — top pair with the best kicker. Heads‑up on a checked flop I should be getting value and denying equity to drawing/medium hands. Flash’s calling range from the BB is wide (worse Ax, Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors and occasional two‑spade combos that have a one‑card flush draw), and he often check‑calls rather than bet out. I’ll make a value/protection c‑bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) to get called by worse aces and pairs, charge any spade draws, and fold out pure floats. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue for value on blanks, pot‑control if a scary card appears). If he check‑raises big I’ll assume strong made hands (set/two‑pair or a polarized shove) and proceed cautiously (call smaller check‑raises, fold to an overbet shove depending on sizing).

I have flopped top pair with an Ace. Although my kicker (Deuce) is the weakest possible, top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play and cannot be folded to a single continuation bet. Minni's 40% pot-sized bet (400 into 1000) is standard for an aggressor on an Ace-high board. I will call to see the turn, keeping her range wide and allowing her to continue bluffing with weaker hands. Raising would likely only get action from better Aces and fold out hands that I currently beat.

The turn card is the Six of Spades, which is quite a dynamic card. It completes a potential spade flush and adds several straight possibilities (such as 2-3 or 7-8). Having flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Deuce), my hand has become more vulnerable. In heads-up play, out of position, the most prudent strategy with a marginal top-pair hand on a wet board is to check. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts to the board texture. If she checks back, it's likely my Ace-high is still good; if she bets, I will have to evaluate based on her sizing whether to continue or fold to heavy pressure on a board that favors her range.

I have A♥K♦ — top pair with the best kicker — after c-betting the flop and getting called. The turn 6♠ completes a three-spade board (A♠-4♠-5♣-6♠), which turns many of Flash’s flop-callers (two-spade combos) into made flushes. Betting again here mostly bloats the pot and gets value from hands that already beat me (flushes) while only extracting thin value from worse Aces/pairs. With a 1,400 pot and deep stacks, the prudent line is pot-control: check the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river. If Flash bets a small-to-medium amount I can call for value or fold to large overbets / polarized check-raises that will usually represent flushes or two-pair/set. Given his check-call tendencies in the history, checking now minimizes risk and preserves options.

The river is the Seven of Spades, completing a four-flush (spades) and a four-straight (4-5-6-7) on the board. My hand is top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Deuce) and no spade. This board is extremely dangerous as any single spade or any 3 or 8 makes a much stronger hand than mine. After checking the turn, checking the river is the only sensible move to control the pot. If Minni bets, I will likely have to fold given how many combinations of hands beat me on this texture, but for now, checking is the standard play to see if we can get to a cheap showdown.

I have A♥K♦ on A♠-4♠-5♣-6♠-7♠. After my flop c-bet Flash called; both of us checked turn and river. The river completed a four‑spade board so any single spade in Flash’s hand now makes an Ace‑high flush — a large portion of his flop‑callers (K♠x, Q♠x, suited connectors, etc.) have converted into hands that beat me. There are also straight combinations (3x or 8x) and the usual sets/two‑pairs in his range. Betting here would mostly bloating the pot and get value from only a thin subset (worse Aces/pairs), while risking a check‑raise or a thin‑value call from better hands. Given his check‑call tendencies and the low fold equity, pot‑control is the prudent line: check to showdown and avoid committing a big chunk of my stack with a vulnerable one‑pair hand.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Seven offsuit is a hand that is well within the standard opening range from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand, potentially winning the blinds immediately or positioning myself to take the pot on a variety of flop textures with a continuation bet.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with T9♦ — a very playable, suited connector heads‑up. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (I’ve already posted the BB). I’ll call to 300 total. Rationale: T9s has strong equity vs a wide BTN opening range and excellent implied odds with deep stacks; calling keeps his range wide and lets me realize equity cheaply. 3‑betting OOP with this hand is marginal — a tiny 3‑bet won’t fold out enough of his range and a larger 3‑bet bloats a pot out of position without a made hand. Plan: see the flop and continue with pairs/draws or exercise pot control/fold if the flop is unfavorable.

Flop: Ac Ks Js. I hold T♦9♦ — only a single-card Broadway straight draw (any Q = 4 clean outs). That's ~8.5% to hit on the turn and ~16.5% by the river. There is no realistic diamond flush possibility (I have two diamonds but only two cards remain, so I can't make a 5-card diamond flush). Pairing a T or 9 is usually too weak versus a preflop raiser’s range (lots of A/K/J/broadways) and I'm out of position. With deep stacks and modest equity, the prudent line is pot control and to realize equity: check and play the turn based on Flash’s sizing — call small/medium c-bets for the draw, fold to heavy pressure.

On a board of Ac-Ks-Js, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing any Ten). This board is extremely heavy and likely connects well with Minni's calling range (Aces, Kings, Jacks, and various draws). While a continuation bet is standard, checking back here allows me to see a free turn card and realize my equity without risk of being check-raised off my draw. Given the stack depths and the board texture, keeping the pot manageable while holding only a gutshot is a prudent play.

Check. Board Ac Ks Js 4c and I hold T♦9♦ — I only have Q outs (4 outs) to make the Broadway straight, which is 4/46 ≈ 8.7% to hit on the river. Pot is 600 and Flash (the preflop raiser) checked both flop and turn; that line suggests weakness or pot control rather than a hand I can credibly fold out with a semi‑bluff. Betting the turn would be a pure semi‑bluff with limited fold equity (a small bet needs ~20%+ folds to be profitable) and runs the risk of a check‑raise or getting called by Ax/Kx/Jx that dominate me — if called I still only have ~8.7% to improve. Checking lets me realize equity for free and preserves stack size. If I hit the Q on the river I can extract value; if I miss I should fold to any meaningful river bet — to justify a call with 4 outs into a 600 pot villain would have to bet ≤≈57 chips, which is unrealistic. Plan: check, go to the river, then re‑evaluate (value when I make the straight, bluff selectively if the river and read allow, otherwise fold to significant river pressure).

The turn 4c is a blank for my hand. Minni has checked for the second time, which might indicate weakness or a marginal hand looking for a cheap showdown. Given the board of A-K-J-4, many hands in a standard calling range have at least some piece of this board. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts, and by checking back, I ensure I see the river for free without risking a check-raise. This also keeps the pot small, which is preferable when I only have Queen-high equity at the moment.

River: Ac Ks Js 4c 6s — my T♦9♦ missed. I have no pair, no straight and no flush; my only straight outs were Qs/Qx and they didn't hit. Flash was the preflop raiser but checked both flop and turn, a line consistent with either a medium-strength made hand (Ax/Kx/Jx) or a weak holding trying to pot-control; either way it greatly reduces my fold equity. Bluffing the river here is thin — small bets will be called by most hands that beat me and large bluffs are unnecessary risk given the pot (600) and deep stacks. Follow my earlier plan: take the free showdown equity and check; fold to any meaningful bet.

On a board of Ace-King-Jack-Four-Six, my Queen-high holds very little showdown value, but the board texture is highly coordinated and likely connects with many hands in Minni's calling range. Minni has checked three times, indicating either a weak made hand looking for a cheap showdown or a completely missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff to take down the small 600-chip pot, my previous checks on the flop and turn make a river bet look like a late-stab attempt rather than a value bet. Given the stack depths and the fact that even a weak pair from Minni will likely call a small bet, I will check back and concede the pot if beaten, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with T4 hearts (suited, but a weak hand). Calling the 50 to complete the big blind is the cheapest, most +EV option: I keep positional advantage, see a flop for a small price (50 into a 150 pot → good pot odds), retain stack flexibility, and have backdoor flush/straight potential. Raising to 200 would be a thin steal with a poor holding and can get me committed out of position post-flop. Plan: call to see flop, then proceed based on texture (contend for value if I hit, fold or control pot if I miss and face aggression).

I am holding QTs in the Big Blind. Minni has limped in from the Small Blind. QTs is a very strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of a typical limping range. I will raise here to build the pot and take the initiative, as this hand plays well post-flop and has significant equity. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard sizing to punish the limp.

Call 400. Heads-up on the button with T4h is weak but playable: suited, in position, and with backdoor flush/straight potential. Flash’s raise to 400 looks like a standard wide heads-up raise here (he’s been opening a lot), and calling keeps the pot manageable while preserving positional advantage. Pot math: I need to call 300 more to win a ~800 pot (37.5% equity), which is reasonable versus a wide range and given my fold/realization equity post‑flop. A 3-bet would be a thin bluff with poor holding and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position if called; folding is too tight given the price and hand equity. Plan: see the flop, proceed with aggression only if I improve (top pair/strong draw) or use position to float and take the pot; fold to large, committed aggression on dry boards if I miss.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped a strong flush draw with two overcards to the bottom card on a paired board. Since I am first to act on the flop as the Big Blind in heads-up play, I will lead with a continuation bet. A bet of 400, which is half the pot, is a standard sizing to maintain initiative, represent a strong range (including Jacks or overpairs), and build the pot for when I hit my draw.

I have T♥4♥ on J♠ J♥ 4♠ — that’s two pair (Jacks and Fours) on the flop. Heads-up this is a very strong made hand versus a wide c-bet range: most of Flash’s continuation-bet hands (overcards, single-pair hands, medium pocket pairs) are behind, while only holdings with a Jack (trips/full house/quads) or specific 4x combos with a better kicker truly dominate. Flash’s sizing here is standard/relatively small, so I want to extract value and deny free cards while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll raise to the min (800 total) — this charges worse hands to continue, gets value from single-pair and overcard calls, and gives me initiative to control the turn. If Flash shows heavy strength (a big 3-bet or shove) I’ll re-evaluate (range will be narrowed to strong J/4 combos) and respond based on sizing and reads.

I am holding a strong spade flush draw with two overcards to the board's four on a paired [Js Jh 4s] board. After my continuation bet of 400, Minni has min-raised to 800. The pot is currently 2000 chips, and I only need to call an additional 400 chips. This provides me with exceptional pot odds (16.7% required equity). Given that a flush draw alone has roughly an 18% chance of hitting on the turn and a 35% chance by the river, calling is a mathematically sound decision. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card, as my draw remains very live and the price is too good to fold.

I am out of position against Minni, who showed aggression on the flop by min-raising my continuation bet. The turn 3c is essentially a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Since I am still on a flush draw and have not improved my hand to a pair, checking is the most appropriate action. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the turn card. If she checks behind, I get a free card to try and hit my flush on the river. If she bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking maintains flexibility and controls the size of the pot while I'm still drawing.

I have T♥4♥ on 4♠ J♠ J♥ 3♣ — two pair (Jacks and Fours). Heads‑up this is a very strong made hand versus a wide c‑bet/call range; only trips/full houses (hands with a Jack, or unlikely 3x/4x full houses) really beat me. Flash led small on the flop, I raised to 800 and he called, then checked the turn — that line can be either weakness or a trap. I want to extract value and deny free cards, but also keep the pot from getting bloated if I’m up against trips. A half‑pot bet (~1,200 into 2,400) hits that balance: it’s large enough to get called by single‑pair hands, medium pocket pairs and draws, yet small enough to avoid committing too much if I face a big check‑raise. Plan: bet 1,200; if Flash check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider folding (that line is usually trips/full house), if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river for thin value or pot‑control.

I am holding a strong queen-high flush draw on a paired board. Minni has bet 1200 into a 2400 pot after I checked the turn. To call, I need to commit 1200 chips to win a total pot of 4800, giving me pot odds of 3:1 (requiring 25% equity). With 9 spades remaining in the deck, I have approximately a 19.5% chance of hitting my flush on the river. Although I am slightly below the immediate mathematical requirement for a call, the implied odds in a heads-up match are significant; if I hit my flush, I can likely extract more value from Minni, especially if she holds a Jack. Additionally, my overcards (Queen and Ten) could potentially be the best hand if she is semi-bluffing or holding a lower pair, though I am primarily calling for the draw. I will call the 1200 and evaluate the river.

The board has completed with an 8c, and I have missed my flush draw, leaving me with only queen-high. Minni has shown consistent aggression by min-raising the flop and betting half-pot on the turn, which strongly suggests she holds a Jack or better. I have zero showdown value, but leading out as a bluff into a range that has shown such strength is highly risky. I will check to see if she checks behind with a marginal hand or a missed straight draw, but I am prepared to fold to most bets given the board texture and previous action.

I have T♥4♥ — two pair (Jacks and Fours) on 4♠ J♠ J♥ 3♣ 8♣. Heads‑up, Flash was the preflop raiser, called my flop raise and my turn bet, then checked the river. His line contains a lot of single‑pair and medium‑pair hands that are behind my two‑pair (we can expect calls from Jx (single pair), 88/99/TT, and other marginal hands), while only a small number of combinations (full houses — pocket 4s/3s or rare Jx/quads) actually beat me. Checking back gives up value; a value bet is the +EV play. I’ll size to extract calls from worse hands but avoid bloating the pot against the few full‑house combos. A half‑pot river bet of 2,400 is large enough to get value from worse pairs/floats and small enough to fold to a heavy check‑raise (which would almost certainly be a full house). Therefore: bet 2,400 to maximize value while keeping risk controlled.

On the river, the board is 4s Js Jh 3c 8c. I have missed my spade flush draw and am left with only queen-high. Minni has shown consistent strength throughout the hand: she min-raised my flop c-bet, bet the turn, and has now fired a 2400 bet into a 4800 pot on the river. This line strongly suggests she has at least a Jack for trips, or perhaps a pocket pair that she played aggressively. I have zero showdown value, and given the line she has taken, a bluff raise is unlikely to succeed as she appears committed to her hand. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my remaining 11,300 chips for the remaining 15 hands of the tournament.

I'm on the Button (SB) in a heads-up situation with J9o. This is a standard opening hand. My goal is to build the pot and take control of the hand, especially since we're approaching the end of the tournament (Hand 136 of 150). Minni has a significant chip lead, so I need to pick up pots where I can without being overly reckless. A 3x raise is my standard opening size.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I’m the big blind with KcQc — a very strong, well-blocking, suited hand heads-up with good equity vs a wide opening range. I can either call and see a flop or 3-bet to take the initiative and extract value from worse kings, broadways and weaker suited hands while folding out some bluffs. I’ll raise to 900 (≈3x the open): big enough to get fold equity and build a pot when ahead, but not so large as to commit my deep stack unnecessarily. If he 4-bets large or jams I’ll re-evaluate (KQc is usually a fold to an extreme 4-bet from a narrow range); if he calls I’ll play aggressively on favorable flops and use the club suit for backdoor/flush possibilities.

I opened to 300 with J9o, a hand that plays well enough in position but is vulnerable to a 3-bet. Minni has 3-bet to 900. While I am in position, J9o is a weak hand to call a 3-bet with against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. Calling 600 more with a hand that is often dominated or has poor post-flop playability against a strong range is not ideal when I need to preserve my stack for better spots in the remaining 14 hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button/SB with A♠K♠ — a premium hand with great equity and position. I want the initiative and to build the pot while isolating the BB. Standard heads-up sizing is ~3x the big blind; raising to 300 total achieves that: it’s big enough to fold out some of his worse hands and get value from worse A/K and broadway holdings, but small enough to leave maneuvering room post-flop and not overcommit my deeper stack. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (AsKs is often good to continue), but opening here for value and position is the +EV play.

K9o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind with in a heads-up situation, especially receiving 2:1 odds on a call. Minni has been frequently opening from the button, and while my stack is smaller than hers, K9o has enough high-card value to see a flop and realize its equity. Raising is an option, but calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to play post-flop with a hand that often dominates some of Minni's wider opening range.

On a monotone flop of Qc 5c Jc, I have the 9c giving me a weak flush draw, plus a backdoor straight draw and one overcard (the King). Being out of position against a big stack who raised pre-flop, checking is the standard play to see how they react to this texture. I'll evaluate based on their bet size, as my 9c gives me some equity to continue if the price is right.

Heads-up on a Qc–5c–Jc flop after I opened preflop. I have A♠K♠ — two overcards and decent showdown equity but no club draw, and this board is very draw-heavy (flush possibilities). Flash in the BB called preflop with a wide range and then checked, which includes a lot of missed hands and single clubs; a half-pot continuation bet pressures those hands, folds out pure air/small pairs, charges club draws, and keeps the pot controllable if called. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300) to get fold equity while retaining room to fold to a raise or proceed cautiously on dangerous turns; if he calls I can continue selectively or check the next card depending on the turn.

On a flop of Qc 5c Jc, I have the 9c which gives me a flush draw. Although it is not the nut flush draw, in a heads-up pot, having a piece of the board (a flush draw) plus an overcard (the King) is usually enough to continue. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call. Folding would be too tight given I have a flush draw and a backdoor straight draw. Raising is risky against a large stack on a monotone board, so calling to see a turn is the most prudent play.

On the turn 7s, the board remains very dangerous with three clubs already present. I have the 9c, which gives me a flush draw, but it is not the nut flush draw. Since I called the flop bet, leading out on a blank turn doesn't represent much strength and might provoke a raise from Minni, who has a significant stack advantage. Checking is the most solid play here; it allows me to see if Minni checks behind, giving me a free card to hit my flush, or to evaluate the size of a bet if she decides to continue her aggression. If she bets small, I can justify a call to realize my equity, but checking first is mandatory in this position.

Board is Qc-5c-Jc-7s; I have A♠K♠ — two overcards but no club and no real draw. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop bet, which polarizes him to either a slow-played two-club flush or medium-strength hands (Qx/Jx, pairs, or floats). Betting the turn here has poor fold equity against that calling range and exposes me to a check-raise or committing chips when I have only overcards. With a big effective stack advantage I prefer pot control: check the turn, see the river and then decide — I can call a reasonable river bet with A/K sometimes, fold to large river aggression that represents the flush, or pick up a delayed bluff on a safe river. Checking preserves chips and keeps maximum options for the river.

On the river, the Ten of Hearts completes a King-high straight for me (9-T-J-Q-K). The board texture is quite dangerous with three clubs, but Minni's decision to check back on the turn suggests she is unlikely to have a completed flush, as she would typically bet for value and protection against a fourth club. By betting 600 into the 1,200 pot, I am targeting value from her one-pair hands like top pair (Queens) or middle pair (Jacks) that she might check-call with. If she holds a King, we likely chop the pot, and I am only losing to exactly Ace-King or a slow-played flush. Given the heads-up nature and the turn check, a value bet is the most profitable line.

Board Qc–5c–Jc–7s–Th; I hold A♠K♠ and checked the turn to control the pot. Flash’s small river stab (600 into a 1800 pot, pot after bet = 2400) requires only ~25% equity to call (600/2400). His line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) contains a mix of strong clubs and Q/J/T value hands and some thin bluffs/floats. I have essentially no showdown value versus his value hands and no club blockers to credibly represent a flush by raising, so a raise would be a high-variance bluff that’s likely to get called or reraised by better combos. Folding throws away the chance to catch his bluffs; calling is cheap relative to my stack and gives me the correct pot-odds to call off against a bluff-heavy portion of his river range. I’ll call 600 for showdown.

In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) should play a very wide range of hands because they have position for the rest of the hand. However, Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o) is a relatively weak hand that falls into the bottom half of starting hands. Given that Minni has a significant chip lead (28,500 vs 11,350) and has been active with 3-bets recently (e.g., Hand #136), I will choose to fold this junk hand to avoid getting involved in a potentially expensive pot where I am likely dominated or forced to fold to a re-raise. While folding the button is generally tight, it minimizes variance against a chip leader in the closing stages of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button with a live 8♦7♥ — a playable, connected hand that performs well in position. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call; the min-raise is 200. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to a wide BB range, and retain flexibility post-flop, so I’ll open to a standard heads-up raise of 300 total. That size (≈3x BB) buys me decent fold equity, keeps the pot manageable versus a shorter-stacked opponent (Flash ~11,250), and allows me to continuation-bet many flops from position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small 3-bets occasionally, and play aggressively post-flop when I hit or can credibly represent a strong hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 2d, a very weak starting hand. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the stack sizes and the weakness of my hand, there is no reason to defend or contest this pot. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.

In Hand #140, I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. While heads-up strategy generally favors playing a wide range from the button, Ten-Six is a weak hand. Given that there are only 11 hands left in the tournament and Minni has a significant chip lead and has shown aggression in the blinds, I will fold this hand to avoid losing chips in a marginal spot and wait for a better opportunity to bridge the gap in the remaining hands.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 3♥9♣ — a weak, unsuited hand but cheap to play in position. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop (about 4:1 pot odds). Raising to 200 would be a thin steal against a player who defends and 3-bets relatively often; with this hand and deep stacks I don’t want to build a bloated pot out of position or face a shove. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop and use position: c-bet or float only if the flop offers me a pair or decent backdoor equity, otherwise check-fold to significant aggression.

With only 10 hands remaining and a significant stack deficit compared to Minni, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Minni limped from the button, which is unusual given her previous aggressive patterns. Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand, and while I have the option to raise, there is no value in bloating the pot out of position with such a poor holding. I will take the free flop and see if I can improve or outplay Minni on later streets if the board texture allows.

I have Queen-high on a dry, disconnected board of 4-6-9. Since I am out of position against the chip leader and have no made hand or significant draw, checking is the most prudent move. I want to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or if the board improves for me on the turn without bloating the pot with a weak hand.

I have top pair (9s) with a very weak 3-kicker on a dry 4h-6s-9d flop. Heads-up this is usually a hand you want to bet for value and to deny free cards — many of Flash’s checking range are worse pairs, floats or overcards that will call a small bet. The pot is only 200 and stacks are deep, so I’ll make a half-pot bet to extract value while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; if Flash check-raises large I will fold given my weak kicker.

On a board of 4-6-9, my Queen-Deuce offsuit has no connection and zero draw potential. While the pot odds are 4:1 to call Minni's small bet, I am out of position against a significant chip leader who has been very active. Calling here would likely lead to me folding on the turn to a second barrel anyway. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and a large stack deficit, I need to be more strategic about which pots I contest. Bleeding chips by floating with no equity is not a viable comeback strategy; I will wait for a better hand to make a stand.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 2c 4s. This is a very weak hand. While the pot odds to call the extra 50 chips are technically good, I am significantly behind in chips compared to Minni (11050 vs 28800) and we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 142 of 150). Playing weak hands out of position or in marginal spots against a much larger stack is unnecessary risk. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot in the remaining 8 hands.

Heads-up on the button/SB with A♣K♥ — a premium heads-up hand. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but I want the initiative and fold equity as well as to build a reasonable pot with position. Standard heads-up sizing of ~2.5–3x the BB is appropriate; min-raising to 200 is too small and invites too many cheap flops/3-bets. I raise to 300 to get value vs worse hands, isolate, and retain flexibility postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (call or 4-bet shove depending on size); if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable textures and use position to extract value.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 4c, a relatively weak holding. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, which is her standard opening. Given the current tournament status at hand 143 of 150 and my stack size of approximately 110 BBs compared to Minni's 286 BBs, I need to be cautious. J4o does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent like Minni. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands.

I am on the button with pocket deuces. In a heads-up situation, any pair is a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size, which allows me to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or see a flop with the initiative.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button with 7♥2♦ in the big blind. This is one of the absolute worst hands (dominated too often and terrible out of position), and the pot odds to call (200 into ~400, needing ≈33% equity) are not met by 7‑2o against a normal opening range. I have a large stack advantage and no compelling read to defend this particular holding — preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with Ac6c — a suited Ace with good postflop playability. I’m already priced to call (50) but against a single big blind I want to take the initiative and play the hand in position. With deep stacks (289bb vs ~110bb) a standard 2.5–3x BB open is appropriate: it builds the pot when I have the betting lead, denies free or cheap flops to marginal hands, and isolates the opponent. I’ll size to 300 total (3x BB) — large enough to put pressure but small enough to fold to a committed 3-bet if Flash repopulates aggressively. If he calls I’ll use position and my Ace‑high + club backdoor equity to c‑bet favorable flops and control pot size on others.

I am in the Big Blind with T7o, facing a 3x raise from Minni. T7o is a marginal hand, but given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), it's a standard defend against a wide button opening range. I will call and see a flop.

On this flop of Jd-4c-8s, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how they react. I have some equity with the gutshot and a backdoor straight possibility, but I'm not strong enough to lead out. I will check and evaluate based on the size of Minni's continuation bet.

I was the preflop aggressor (3x) and Flash just checked the dry Jd–4c–8s flop. With Ac6c I’ve missed but hold a valuable Ace blocker and a backdoor club possibility. Heads‑up this is a textbook c‑bet spot: Flash’s defending range is wide and contains a lot of hands that fold to pressure (missed broadways, small pairs, weak connectors), while many stronger Jx/pair hands will still pay off a reasonable bet. I’ll make a half‑to‑two‑thirds pot continuation bet to buy the pot down and extract folds/value — I size to 350 into the 600 pot (~58% pot). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll control the turn (check back most blank turns, fire again only on favorable cards like an A or a club or when I pick up equity); if he raises I’ll give credit and fold to a substantial raise since I have no pair.

On this Jd-4c-8s flop, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). Minni has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 600 after raising pre-flop. This is a fairly standard sizing. While I only have ten-high, the pot odds (calling 350 to win 1300) are roughly 3.7:1, which makes a call reasonable given that I have four outs to a likely best hand (the 9) and some limited overcard equity (the Ten). I will call the bet and look to improve on the turn.

The 2d on the turn is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still only have Ten-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). Since I am out of position and Minni was the aggressor on the flop, the most standard play is to check to her. I want to see if she will check back, giving me a free river card, or continue her aggression, which would force me to evaluate the pot odds for my draw.

I was the preflop aggressor with Ac6c, c‑bet the flop (~58% pot) and Flash called. The turn (2♦) is a blank for my hand — I still have only Ace‑high and the backdoor club is dead with one card to come. I have very little equity (only 3 Aces + 3 Sixes ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to pair) and no meaningful redraws, so I should avoid bloating the pot. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range, and lets me realize equity cheaply to the river. Plan: check back; if Flash leads a small bet I can consider a call for showdown with my Ace blocker, but I will fold to a sizable turn shove or raise.

The river brings a second deuce, pairing the board. I have missed my gutshot straight draw and am left with Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. Minni checked back on the turn, which usually indicates either a marginal made hand (like a pair of eights or a weak Jack) looking for a cheap showdown, or a total air hand that has given up. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a Jack or a slow-played trip deuces, Minni's significant chip lead and previous stickiness make a bluff risky. I will check and hope to see a check back from a worse hand, though I expect to lose to most of her range.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop but missed — I only have Ace‑high with no river redraws. After Flash called the flop and both players checked the turn, the river paired the board (2d/2s) which doesn’t help my hand and actually makes my bluff less credible (paired board reduces bluffs that miss). Betting here would be thin: most of Flash’s calling range (Jx, 8x, 4x, small pairs and some floats) either already beats me or will comfortably call a small river bet, while the hands I might fold out are marginal and unlikely to be many. With the pot only 1,300 and two players left, pot control and preserving stack are the right priorities — checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range, avoids bloating the pot with only Ace‑high, and lets me realize showdown. I’ll check and take the safe showdown line.

With 95o, I am holding a very weak hand in the Small Blind. Given the current stack disadvantage (approximately 1:3) and only five hands remaining in the match, I prefer to fold this hand and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity. Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the match, and playing a weak hand out of position post-flop or inducing a 3-bet pre-flop is not an optimal strategy here.

Heads-up on the button (SB) — standard strategy is to open a very wide range and take the initiative. K♣5♠ is a hand I’m happy to raise with: it has decent high-card value and plays well in position. My stack (296bb) is much larger than Flash’s (≈102bb), so I can apply pressure without risking my tournament. Raising to 200 (2x BB) is the standard open size here — it maximizes fold equity, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me position postflop to c-bet on many runouts. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll typically fold K5o; I’ll call smaller 3-bets and play postflop according to texture.

In a heads-up situation with only 4 hands remaining in the tournament, Ace-Ten offsuit is a very strong holding. Minni, as the chip leader, has been opening frequently. I need to defend my blind aggressively to close the chip gap. A 3-bet here is standard to take initiative and build a pot with what is likely the best hand. I will raise to 600 to see where I stand and potentially end the hand right here.

I opened to 200 on the button and Flash made a small 3-bet to 600. K♣5♠ is marginal and often dominated by the value portion of his 3-bet range, but it plays well in position and the sizing is small enough that he can be 3-betting light. 4-betting with K5o is too thin (dominated, poor blocker), and folding surrenders position and too much equity. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me realize equity and exploit postflop. I’ll call the extra 400 (600 total) and proceed: c-bet when I hit or on very dry/K-connected flops, check/control on wet boards, and fold to heavy aggression when clearly behind.

On a low, connected board of 2h-3h-5c, my Ace-Ten offsuit still holds significant equity as Ace-high, and I have the nut flush blocker with the Ace of hearts. Having 3-bet pre-flop, I maintain the range advantage for high overpairs (though these are less likely given the board texture). I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of 400 (1/3 of the pot) to exert pressure on Minni's likely broadway-heavy range that missed this flop, while also potentially getting value from weaker draws. If raised, I can comfortably fold knowing the board favors a caller's range more than mine, but a bet allows me to take down the pot now if she has non-pairing high cards.

I have K♣5♠ on 2h‑3h‑5c — top pair (5s) with a decent kicker. Flash 3‑bet pre and now fires a very small c‑bet (400 into the ~1200 pot), which is consistent with a wide range that includes lots of bluffs/hearts, weak pairs and some value (sets/A5/55). The sizing gives me very good pot odds and, more importantly, I have position, so I prefer pot control and to keep his weaker holdings and draws in the pot. Raising here would bloat the pot out of position vs a 3‑bettor and commit more to hands that beat me; calling lets me extract value from worse hands and use position to make better decisions on the turn. Plan: call 400, evaluate the turn — if a heart or heavy action comes I can fold to obvious strength; if a blank comes or he shows weakness I can take the initiative.

The 7d on the turn is a relatively neutral card that doesn't improve my Ace-Ten. Having 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, I have represented a strong range, but Minni's call on the low flop suggests she likely has a piece of the board or a pocket pair. I will check here to control the pot size and see how she responds. If she checks back, I can try to take it down or reach showdown on the river. If she bets, I'll have to decide if my Ace-high and nut flush blocker have enough equity to continue, but checking is the best play to avoid over-committing against a range that currently beats me.

Board: 2♥–3♥–5♣–7♦. I hold K♣5♠ — middle pair (pair of fives) with a decent kicker, in position. Flash 3‑bet pre and fired a small flop bet, then checked the turn: that line often represents a wide/marginal range (missed hearts, overcards, small pairs, floats) or pot control. Checking back surrenders value and gives free river cards that can improve bluffs/draws. I want to extract value from worse one-pair hands and missed draws while keeping the pot manageable against stronger holdings, so I’ll bet a medium probe. Betting ~1/2 pot puts pressure on pure air and gets called by worse Kx/Ax/low pairs and draws. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously (bet again for thin value on a blank, check/fold to strong action if the board pairs or a heart completes). If he raises large I’ll respect it and likely fold — my hand is vulnerable to trips/two pair.

Minni's bet of 1000 into a 2000 pot after I checked the turn suggests she likely connected with this low board (2-3-5-7) or has a pocket pair. While I have Ace-high and the nut heart blocker, the board texture heavily favors a range that calls a 3-bet and a flop continuation bet, such as small pairs or suited connectors. Since I am trailing in chips and there are only three hands remaining after this one, I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation rather than calling down with only Ace-high on a coordinated board.

I am currently in the Small Blind with Q2o, which is a very weak hand. Given that there are only three hands remaining in the match (148, 149, and 150) and Minni holds a significant chip lead, I am looking for higher-equity opportunities to potentially double up. Q2o performs poorly against most of the Big Blind's defending range and post-flop play. I will fold this hand to preserve my stack for the final two hands where I might receive a more playable starting hand.

Heads-up on the button with K♥8♠ (unsuited). In heads-up play this is well within an open-raise range — I want the initiative and to exploit position. Flash (BB) has 9,200 behind; I have a huge stack, so a standard 3BB open is appropriate to get value and fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll raise to 300 total (a standard open size at this table); if Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed very cautiously), but I want to play the hand in position when possible.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament and holding a decent starting hand like Jack-Nine offsuit in a heads-up situation, folding is too passive. Minni has been raising frequently from the button. Jack-Nine has enough equity to defend the big blind against a standard raise. I will call the additional 200 to see a flop and look for an opportunity to improve or outplay Minni post-flop, keeping in mind that I need to accumulate chips quickly to close the gap in the final hands.

I have hit middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high flop. In a heads-up situation, middle pair is a respectable hand, but because the Ace is a very strong card for the pre-flop raiser's range, I should proceed with caution. I will check to the aggressor (Minni) to see how she responds. This allows me to keep the pot small and evaluate the strength of her hand based on her betting action. Betting into her here might force out hands I beat while getting called by better ones like any Ace.

I opened preflop and still have initiative heads-up. The flop (7♠ A♠ 9♣) contains an Ace — a card I missed — but Flash checked, which often represents a wide checking/calling range (missed hands, Kx, small pairs, or float hands). My K♠8♥ has no pair but does have a spade and a couple of blockers; it has little showdown value so I should try to take the pot now. I’ll make a standard continuation bet ~2/3 pot (400) to get folds from unpaired hands and small holdings while keeping the pot controllable if called. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll fold (I don’t have the equity to continue); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and opponent’s response.

I have hit second pair (Nines) on an Ace-high flop. In a heads-up situation with only two hands remaining, middle pair holds significant value, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni who frequently opens and continuation bets. While the Ace is a scare card, folding now would be too exploitable. I will call the 400-chip bet to see how the turn develops, as my hand has decent showdown value and some potential to improve to two pair or trips.

I have middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position and the turn card is a complete blank, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she reacts. This allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to call a bet based on the sizing, or potentially see a cheap river if she checks behind with a weaker hand or a draw.

I missed the Ace and have only K‑high with no backdoor flush (my 8♠ is blocked by the A♠). Flash called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn — a line consistent with a medium-strength holding (Ax, 9x, small pair) or a float. I have essentially no showdown value, so I should try to take the pot now with a controlled bluff rather than allow a cheap river. Betting about half the pot keeps the pressure and preserves stack depth if I get raised: I can fold to a sizeable check‑raise and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If Flash calls I’ll pot‑control on the river and re‑evaluate, but a half‑pot probe here will fold out many worse hands and wins the pot right away often enough to be +EV.

I have second pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board with a Jack kicker. The turn card is a Trey of Diamonds, which is a complete blank and doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni has bet 700 into a 1400 pot (offering 3:1 pot odds). In a heads-up match against an aggressive opponent, second pair is quite strong and certainly holds more than the 25% equity required to make this call profitable. I will call to see the river, as I can still improve to two pair or trips, or win if Minni is betting with a draw or a weaker pair like a Seven.

On the river, the board has paired the Trey, resulting in a final board of As-9c-7s-3d-3h. I hold middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. In a heads-up situation, middle pair is a respectable showdown hand, but it is not strong enough to bet for value because it is unlikely to be called by many worse hands, while an Ace or a better Nine will certainly call or raise. Since I am out of position, I will check to see if Minni checks behind with a weaker hand or a missed draw (like the spade flush draw), which allows me to get to showdown as cheaply as possible. If she bets, I will have to decide if my hand is strong enough to call based on her sizing and previous aggression.

I have K♥8♠ — no pair, missed everything. Line: I c‑bet the A♠7♠9♣ flop for 400 and probed the turn 3♦ for 700; Flash called both streets. That calling line narrows him to a sticky range (Ax, 9x, 7x, some medium pairs/overpairs and occasional floats). The river pairing the 3 (3♥) actually helps his range and gives him easy check‑calls. Pot is 2,800 and Flash has ~7,900 behind — any river bluff needs meaningful fold equity to be profitable. Rough break‑even math: bet 900 needs ~24% folds, 1,400 needs ~33%, 2,000 needs ~42%. Given he’s shown willingness to call twice and then checks the river, I estimate his fold frequency is below the levels needed to make a bluff +EV. I also don’t block the key A/3 combos. With essentially zero showdown value and poor fold equity, the exploitative and low‑variance play is to check and concede — avoid risking chips on a marginal bluff. If he bets I will fold to a meaningful value bet.

Holding Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation is a strong starting hand. As this is the final hand of the tournament, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional edge.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) — he opened to 300 from the SB which is a wide/steal-heavy range. I’m OOP after the flop (BB acts first), and J♠5♥ is a marginal, poorly-connected offsuit hand that doesn’t make a good 3-bet bluff out of position. Calling 200 more (total committed 300) gives me ~33% break-even equity on a pot that will be ~600 — against a wide open range that’s acceptable to see a flop and play cautiously. Plan: call to see the flop; continue only with a pair, strong draw, or good board texture, otherwise check and fold to significant aggression. I’ll avoid a tiny 3-bet (would bloat the pot OOP) or folding a decent spot to defend in heads-up.

Flop: 5♦ 9♦ 6♦. I hold J♠5♥ — middle pair (pair of 5s) with a decent jack kicker, but the board is monotone diamond and very draw-heavy. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains lots of diamonds, overcards and some pairs/sets. I’m out of position and checking here just gives free cards to many profitable draws. I want to deny correct odds to diamond draws and get value from worse pairs/draws while keeping the pot manageable. A ~2/3-pot bet (~400 into 600) charges draws enough to fold some equity hands and extracts calls from weaker holdings; if he jams/raises big I’ll put him on a very strong hand (flush/set/two-pair) and fold. Therefore I bet to protect and build value.

This is the final hand of the tournament, and my goal is to maximize my stack. On a monotone board of 5d-9d-6d, my hand (Queen of Spades, Ten of Clubs) has very little equity. Minni's donk-bet of 400 into 600 suggests she has either a made hand (like a pair or a flush) or a significant draw. Without a diamond in my hand, I have no insurance against a fourth diamond appearing, and even hitting a Queen or Ten might not be enough to win against her range on such a coordinated board. Folding now preserves my remaining stack of 10,100 chips, which is the most strategic move to avoid unnecessary losses in a low-probability situation.
